engagements.body: Transition note 3/17/26 Can't fill out the churn risk playbook since there is no deal associated, so this will serve as the churn risk playbook. CHURN RISK (RED) Requested to pause, but won't give a legitimate reason We have granted them a 3 month pause in the past Tough implementation, and I think they have some financial troubles since they were frequently late on payments I think their card has been failing permamnently, so they might be out of business or fully churned at this point Not launched, but we did get close to finishing implementation. I've reached out several times recently, but no response from Sadie or Brett

Meeting 7/17 with Sadie Meeting Summary Josh Baker and Cabinet Trends (Sadie) discussed shipping account setup, Allmoxy functionality, and refinements needed for their product configuration and output documents. The conversation also covered launching options, pricing strategies, FedEx integration, and customization of user experience on the Allmoxy platform. They addressed several technical adjustments and clarified next steps for launching operations efficiently. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Cabinet Trends is near launch but still waiting for FedEx account clarification (freight account inactive). FedEx Ground appears functional without needing a dedicated freight account number. FedEx quotes may underestimate shipping; Josh recommended a 2.3x multiplier to compensate. Custom shipping methods (e.g., distance-based pricing) are available as a backup option. Technical Issues & Fixes FedEx freight account is currently archived/inactive, causing setup delays. "Nailers" not showing in box parts list — Josh to investigate further and involve Sarah if needed. Duplicate paint-grade front material found in product options — one was hidden successfully. Drawer front type showing on packing slip despite not being user-selected — removed from output pages. Implemented a "notes" field to capture custom color input alongside the “custom color” selection. Custom color label updated to read “Custom Color (Use Notes)” to prompt user input. Mass-adding notes field to all products is not possible; must be done individually. Decisions Made Proceed with 2.3x shipping multiplier for FedEx pricing as a placeholder until more data is gathered. Nailers should appear in box parts if supported by Allmoxy — Josh to confirm functionality. Customers will not be given public ordering/bid access via the website. Duplicate paint grade option hidden; only preferred material remains visible. Use of output pages vs. Cabinet Vision still undecided but Cabinet Vision will be used initially. Launch may start by manually entering customer orders into Allmoxy instead of customer self-service. Challenges or Concerns Freight account issues are pending with FedEx and causing delays in full shipping integration. Confusion around max weight limits between FedEx Ground and Freight. Uncertainty about whether FedEx freight account is required for palletized shipments. Mass editing product attributes (e.g., adding notes field) is manual and time-consuming. Action Items Josh to follow up with Sarah regarding: Why "nailers" may not be showing in box parts. Clarification on freight account number requirements for FedEx Ground vs. Freight. Recommended max weight setting for freight shipments. Sadie to check with FedEx rep about freight account reactivation and max weight per pallet. Cabinet Trends to: Decide on initial launch method (manual order entry vs. self-service). Inform Brett of the option to use Allmoxy for pricing or controlled internal entry. Begin manually adding "notes" field to other relevant products as needed. Next Steps Await response from FedEx rep on freight account and weight requirements. Josh will send meeting recording and continue support on the nailers/box parts issue. Cabinet Trends to move forward with local shipping method as fallback if FedEx delays persist. Further testing and adjustments to shipping methods and pricing logic will be conducted as needed. Preparation for soft launch with close customers to test Allmoxy workflow and ensure proper pricing configuration.
Call with Sadie 7/17 Nailers part not showing up on Box Parts list

Canyon Creek Scope 7/15 Canyon Creek is behind on invoices (working their way back to 0) Really struggling financially until they can get Allmoxy to some customers and some orders in The last thing we had to complete for them before they launch to customers was the export to Cabinet Vision I told them to send me some example CSV, ORD, or Excel files that could be imported to CV. They were confused by this, so I told them to send some CV orders/ reports to us and let them know "the more information the better" They sent us 3 examples of what they wanted to get from Allmoxy into CV and wanted an estimate before we started to build things out. The estimate we came back with for the 3 total exports was 24 hours for everything (broken up in increments of 3 hours, 5 hours, and 16 hours per export) Total dollar amount it took to scope the exports was $375 I did not give them a heads up that the scope would be billable time (and I also didn't know it would take that amount of time) The original plan was to roll in the cost of the scope into one of the exports; however, after presenting one of the exports as an option to start with, they decided to hold off until they are launched and generating revenue. The problem I see arising is that they are going to give significant pushback to the scope of an export that they are not planning on using right now I also did not tell them the scope would cost money prior, which is my mistake, but I also didn't imagine the scope time would be more than one hour. I think I can sell them on a scope of $150 that we can roll into an export once they decide to move forward, but I know they will be blindsided by a $375 scope. Note: Implementation didn't do anything wrong here, Ray and Aaron just scoped the information that was provided to them. Account context Juan started the build last summer, the owners' business partner left, and they ghosted us for nearly a year Ray and I began working with them again, and they wanted some significant changes made (and frequently changed their minds), so the implementation cost increased. The customer is struggling, and their plan is for Allmoxy to help them sell more cabinets I'm not sure what the total implementation cost has been up to this point, but I know it is a lot.

Onboarding Meeting 7/3 with Sadie and Sarah Brett needs to be quoted and given a heads up before we work on anything else Funds are extremely tight, they just need it to be done at this point Meeting Summary The meeting focused on technical integration with Cabinet Vision, shipping logistics (including FedEx setup), Stripe configuration progress, and product pricing adjustments in Allmoxy. The team discussed CSV mappings for Cabinet Vision imports, evaluated pricing inconsistencies, set up temporary price adjustments, and confirmed next steps for local delivery setup and shipping methods. They also reviewed how drawer box parts appear in output pages and discussed limitations in existing part-level granularity. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Cabinet Vision CSV Integration: Cabinet Vision configurations vary, so the team needs a sample CSV file that matches Cabinet Trends' system headers. Cabinet Trends to send a screenshot or a sample export of an existing job from Cabinet Vision. Allmoxy product variables (e.g., product name, cabinet width, hinge type) need to be mapped to Cabinet Vision-compatible labels. Output Page Drawer Parts: Cabinet Trends inquired about whether drawer parts could be broken down further than just "drawer box" level. Current parts list does not show individual drawer components—only complete drawer box assemblies. Decision made to proceed with current granularity due to system limitations. Shipping Configuration: FedEx account not yet fully set up; Cabinet Trends working to finalize it. Discussion on how to populate FedEx integration fields (e.g., meter number) once account credentials are available. Local delivery pricing logic was discussed; Josh offered to set up custom local delivery rules based on mileage tiers once parameters are sent. Stripe Setup: Stripe integration is nearly complete—pending final input of bank details. Discussion included refining triggers and customer communication for order delivery dates and ship date modification logic. Technical Issues & Fixes Cabinet Pricing Discrepancies: Wall cabinets with two shelves priced higher than expected; possible cause was unnecessary toe kick calculation. Investigation showed minimal impact from toe part on pricing; issue left unresolved for now. Ray advised not to manually tweak formulas further to avoid cascading miscalculations. Decisions Made Leave current wall cabinet pricing as-is due to marginal variance and risk of broader pricing impact. Implement 20% price adjustment for tall cabinets and planned upcharge for garbage pullouts via Allmoxy price adjustments. Avoid overcomplicating formulas—price adjustments to be used for isolated pricing issues instead of formula rewrites. Confirmed customer ability to request delivery dates will be re-enabled in settings. Challenges or Concerns Toe kick part miscalculations affecting wall cabinet pricing accuracy. Current export structure does not support granular drawer box parts. Limited internal expertise to continuously modify Allmoxy formulas—price adjustments preferred. Action Items Cabinet Trends to: Send Cabinet Vision CSV or screenshot of part/material names. Finalize and input FedEx account details. Provide written rules for local delivery pricing (e.g., cost based on mileage tiers). Review and verify price adjustments with Brett—especially for tall cabinets and garbage pullouts. Enable setting for customers to request ship dates (already done during meeting). Josh to: Finalize 20% price adjustment for tall cabinets. Set up additional price adjustments if needed (e.g., garbage pullouts). Await delivery pricing logic for local delivery configuration. Sarah to: Continue assisting with pricing formula evaluation as needed. Follow up with Ray regarding unnecessary toe kick calculations for wall cabinets. Next Steps Cabinet Trends to send over Cabinet Vision export data or screenshots. Once FedEx is configured, Cabinet Trends will complete shipping integration in Allmoxy. Cabinet Trends to email Josh delivery pricing structure for local delivery setup. Team to reconvene next week if needed for further refinements. Continued testing of order flows and pricing accuracy before customer-facing launch.

Onboarding #2 with Sadie 6/26/25 Meeting Summary Josh Baker and Sadie from Cabinet Trends discussed ongoing progress with Allmoxy setup and pricing adjustments. Sadie confirmed that Brett has not yet reviewed the latest pricing edits due to both being busy and her recent illness. They explored pricing logic, automation options, email triggers, and delivery/shipping configurations. Josh walked Sadie through several technical settings and processes within Allmoxy to help prepare for launch and ensure all operational pieces are aligned. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Brett has not yet reviewed Ray’s latest pricing changes. Pricing logic was reviewed, including conditional pricing adjustments (e.g., product-based surcharges or discounts). Sadie has edited and reviewed multiple customer-facing and internal trigger emails in Allmoxy. Sadie is still working on Stripe setup, waiting on necessary details from Brett. Email triggers include welcome messages, order confirmations, and order status updates. Cabinet Trends will initially focus on dealers, not individual/homeowner customers. Sadie asked about and received guidance on tagging companies for tiered pricing structures. Delivery dates and default lead times were clarified (10 calendar days currently). Discussed how to manage customer shipping date requests and override them internally. Technical Issues & Fixes Some difficulty embedding images and hyperlinks in triggers. Confusion over Allmoxy's “will call” setting was resolved (pickup option). Clarified that Stripe setup requires Brett’s full info and a dedicated session. Adjustments were made to triggers and order settings to reflect Cabinet Trends’ preferences. Allmoxy output pages (invoices, fronts, box parts) were reviewed; plan to fine-tune them for shop floor use. Decisions Made Default lead time updated to 10 calendar days. Customers will not initially be able to request shipping dates (feature turned off). Triggers will use the asterisk system (*) to mark active ones for easy visual tracking. Cabinet Trends confirmed Allmoxy is primarily for dealer use at launch. Order status emails will not notify customers about verification unless deemed necessary later. Test triggers were created and then deactivated after confirmation. Challenges or Concerns Stripe account setup is pending Brett’s input. Shipping configuration via FedEx requires Cabinet Trends’ account details. Need to finalize output page details to support shop operations. Delay in testing and finalization due to Sadie’s absence and upcoming holidays. Action Items Sadie to: Coordinate with Brett to review Ray’s pricing updates. Finalize edits to Allmoxy trigger messages. Complete Stripe setup once full information is available. Review output pages with Brett for customization and shop use. Provide FedEx account details to enable shipping setup. Confirm final decision on customer ability to request delivery dates. Josh to: Follow up with a recap and reminders of key to-dos. Schedule Thursday morning follow-up (9 a.m.) before the July 4th holiday. Coordinate with Sarah on assisting Cabinet Trends with FedEx integration. Next Steps Cabinet Trends to prepare for internal review of pricing and Allmoxy setup by mid to late next week. Follow-up meeting scheduled for Thursday morning to check progress on pricing, Stripe, FedEx, and output pages. Continued collaboration to finalize Allmoxy system readiness for dealer launch.

Re-engaged onboarding #1 with Sadie and Brett 6/24/25 Setting up accounts, how an order moves through Allmoxy, tags/ triggers/ price adjustments. Ask a few questions about financials (next time financials, shipping) Tags These are ways you can group customers together for a certain purpose How do you think you might group certain customers together? Retail Wholesale These can be referenced in triggers and price adjustments Ex. you have a group of customers that are cabinet shops, and they get a certain discount. You can give this group of customers a "cabinet shop" tag on their company profile that can then be referenced later. You can also put tags on products A good example would be if you had a group of products that were more difficult to produce, you could give those products a tag and give a surcharge in a price adjustment based on those tags. You can also place a tag on orders A good example would be to have your salespeople put their name on the order in a tag, then you can use the order report to see how much they sold in a given timeframe. Price Adjustments Do you have customers that are priced differently than others? Why? Use price adjustments to achieve this Say you have a customer who is your oldest and most loyal customer, you always give them a 10% discount no matter what. Price adjustments are basically discounts or surcharges that can be applied to customers, groups of customers, or products. They are adjusted PER ITEM, not the total order amount Priority For when there are multiple price adjustments competing with one another If one discount has a higher priority than the other, the highest priority will override the other discount You can also discount customers or products with certain tags Only with other accumulations This means that the discount or surcharge is only applicable when combined with other applicable discounts. Always My preferred method so you can know that the discounts/ surcharge will be applied every time no matter what Triggers How are you currently interacting with customers? Is it manual calls, emails, etc? Triggers can automate that process for you Notify you, and your customers of something happening in Allmoxy Triggers to edit: 1, 6, 12, 16, 18, 19 Does he want to be notified of a bid? Or an order?

Meeting Summary 5/13/25 The meeting focused on onboarding progress with Allmoxy, addressing product pricing inconsistencies, catalog setup, and attribute validations. Discussions included applied ends, fillers, and drawer box pricing formulas. There was also a review of user account setups, including adding new team members and future plans for site customization. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Reviewed base fillers and applied ends pricing discrepancies; many items are underpriced. Cabinet Trends team will compile a list of dimensions and desired pricing for applied ends and fillers. Pricing issues are tied to unclear or misapplied formulas, especially square foot calculations and attribute-based pricing. Drawer box pricing is too low; Ray will adjust formulas to target a $70 price point per drawer box. Discussed J Fillers and the confusion around width and depth validations. Determined that current validations and attribute labels may be flipped or unclear. Validations for certain products are incomplete; Ray is working on this and will notify once completed. Drawer box material options to be expanded to include 5/8 Hard Rock Maple and a Specify Color option. Agreed to remove the Back Material attribute since it should always match the Box Material. Discussed eliminating unnecessary options to simplify customer choices and ensure catalog accuracy. Technical Issues & Fixes Identified anomalies in pricing formulas for applied ends and fillers; Ray to investigate and adjust. Drawer box pricing formula adjustments pending to align with target pricing. Current validations for width and depth on products like J Fillers are inconsistent and confusing; awaiting corrections. Spreadsheet provided by Cabinet Trends was helpful for validation work; Ray acknowledged its clarity. Decisions Made Cabinet Trends will prepare a list of applied ends, fillers, and their desired pricing, including dimensions, for Ray to update formulas accordingly. Josh will notify Ray to implement drawer box formula changes and complete validation removals. New user Sadie Hale was added to Allmoxy with full permissions for testing and order processing. Removal of the Back Material attribute and addition of new drawer box material options confirmed. Challenges or Concerns Cabinet Trends team is hesitant to spend significant time testing until validations and pricing issues are fully resolved. Ongoing confusion around attribute labels (width vs. depth) is causing delays in accurate catalog setup. Decision pending on whether future adjustments should be handled live with Ray or asynchronously via email/screenshots. Action Items Cabinet Trends: Compile and send a list of applied end panels and fillers with sizes and preferred pricing. Determine if screenshots or printed order pages will be used for documenting pricing discrepancies. Await notification from Josh once validations and drawer box formula changes are complete before resuming extensive catalog work. Josh Baker: Notify Ray to: Adjust drawer box formulas to reach the $70 target price. Complete validation removal and notify when finished. Remove Back Material attribute and add new drawer box material options (5/8 Hard Rock Maple and Specify Color). Follow up with Cabinet Trends once Ray completes the above tasks. Next Steps Cabinet Trends to send compiled product/pricing list by the next day. Josh to keep the team informed on Ray’s progress with validations and pricing updates. Schedule the next meeting with Ray after current tasks are complete to finalize catalog adjustments and training on additional Allmoxy features.

Meeting 4/30/25 with Brett (product training to come later) Meeting Summary Josh Baker met with Brett from Canyo to discuss Allmoxy training and strategy during Canyo’s temporary service pause in May. The primary focus was on understanding and adjusting catalog pricing, refining product attributes, and preparing for a smoother user experience. The conversation also involved exploring pricing discrepancies, catalog simplification, and leveraging implementation support with Allmoxy engineer Ray. A follow-up meeting was scheduled for Tuesday, May 6 at 1:00 PM. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Brett expressed interest in using the pause in May to clean up and improve the catalog rather than focus on employee setup. Josh demonstrated how product pricing is driven by attributes like door type and front material, with pricing and margin adjustments possible via the "Product Attributes" settings. They reviewed attribute visibility toggling (without deletion) and how to experiment safely. Brett requested to rename “Thermally Fused Laminate” to “Textured Melamine” for clearer customer understanding—Josh updated both the value and header accordingly. They explored examples using various combinations (TFL, paint grade, stain grade) to analyze how pricing fluctuates. Midwest Flooring (another Allmoxy customer) was discussed as a cautionary tale for using Allmoxy’s visual rendering tool, confirming Brett’s decision to avoid it for end users. Technical Issues & Fixes Brett encountered unclear validation errors in the product builder; Josh explained these could be turned off and noted that current filtering by attribute (e.g., auto-filtering finish options based on door type) is a pending feature. A confusing pricing outlier (~$1,800) was noted, but not identified during the session—Brett plans to review this in his notes. Decisions Made Rename “Thermally Fused Laminate” to “Textured Melamine” across catalog. Use Allmoxy primarily as a dealer order-entry tool instead of for direct public use. Refrain from using Allmoxy’s rendering tool due to usability limitations. Brett will conduct self-testing and documentation before the next session with Ray. Regular weekly or biweekly meetings with Allmoxy support are planned to refine catalog setup and pricing. Challenges or Concerns Brett reported major time spent with Cabinet Vision tech support and being temporarily short-handed operationally. Discrepancies between expected and actual pricing raised concerns—Brett needs time with his notes to validate and prepare questions. Ongoing difficulty in navigating attributes without more dynamic filtering logic. Action Items Josh updated “Thermally Fused Laminate” to “Textured Melamine” in both value and header fields. The next call with Brett and Ray is scheduled for Tuesday, May 6 at 1:00 PM. Josh will send: The original product pricing spreadsheet (re-sent to keep it top of inbox). A short video tutorial on adding products and building orders. Brett will: Locate his notes and document pricing anomalies. Continue testing the catalog by adding products and making notes on high/low prices. Experiment cautiously with pricing attribute values (without deleting). Highlight any changes in the spreadsheet using fill color for easy tracking. Next Steps Brett will review and test the catalog, comparing real output against his notes. Josh and Ray will support a working session on May 6 to begin implementing concrete changes to pricing formulas and attribute logic. Continued weekly or biweekly working sessions are likely, with optional paid engineer support to accelerate pricing structure adjustments.

Onboarding/ testing with Brett 3/5/25 Difference between categories, products, product attributes Sells to homeowners and contractors Covering today Site navigation What he sees vs what his customer will see Theme Creating an account (done) Does he want their users to log in? For a customer Individual and Company Adding employees Permissions Testing notes Specify color needs to be an option (but it doesn't price) It should say "same as front material" same upcharge as front material Wall vertical 1 shelf seems high How can we trim down the lists and make it easier for customers to select? Can we simplify? Brett to slim down front material finishes Doesnt look great https://share1.allmoxy.com/KouRbxKj Export for cabinet vision. Meeting Summary: Josh Baker and Canyo discussed the testing and onboarding process for Almoxy. They reviewed various cabinet configurations, pricing discrepancies, and product attributes. The conversation focused on ensuring accurate pricing, simplifying front material selection, and refining the order entry process. They also touched on customer account creation, order management, and potential system improvements. Key Takeaways: Testing & Onboarding: Continued testing Almoxy’s features, including order entry and product selection. Pricing Concerns: Identified inconsistencies in cabinet pricing; some products appeared overpriced. Product Attribute Refinements: Discussed limiting front material selections to streamline customer experience. Validation and Restrictions: Reviewed validation messages to prevent incompatible selections. Customer & Order Management: Explored best practices for structuring contractor accounts and organizing orders. System Improvement Requests: Noted areas for enhancement, including dynamic attributes, dropdown simplifications, and UI refinements. Action Items: Canyo: Review and refine front material options to limit unnecessary selections. Cross-check pricing against internal spreadsheets for accuracy. Determine key pricing adjustments for front materials. Explore options for room-based order organization. Josh Baker: Retrieve original pricing sheet for cross-verification. Discuss UI and dropdown simplifications with implementation engineer Ray. Send follow-up email with training materials and CSV export of product attributes. Provide insights into integrating Almoxy with Cabinet Vision or alternative design tools. Next Steps: Follow-up Meeting: Scheduled for Monday at 8:30 AM. Homework: Canyo to finalize front material adjustments; Josh to investigate pricing documentation and UI improvements. Further System Refinements: Continue refining the user experience and preparing for wider customer adoption. .

Canyon Creek engagement meeting 2/13/25 Dave, his partner wants to go a different direction Out from Canyon Creek Dave allergic to dust, get really sick Next steps for Brett, he is trying to figure out where Allmoxy fits into his plan. Just a basic drawing, since a lot of people can't see what they are getting Do we have an example of a design drawing (finished) Frustrated with his partner Dave. Brett felt like he was the only one doing stuff. He wants to keep going and get something figured out. End customer: Brett still does some sales, wants to try to get a few salesman that do it. Eventually, he wants to do RTA cabinets that he can ship. Sells to homeowners and contractors. Uses Cabinet Vision Wants to have some people do the installs for him. Found a guy that can do it locally. Brett has doing install himself. And has to shut down his shop for 2 days while he does that. Wants to eventually do flatpack/ RTA

Onboarding meeting 6/6/24 with Brett Harris (didn't end up doing any CS training, just going through his catalog but he is very happy with the progress Juan made since yesterday) Review How the customer will log in What the customer sees vs what they see Theme (still need logos from them) Setting up employees/ permissions Covering today Price adjustments Do you have certain customers that are priced differently than others? Some that are discounted or surcharged? Customer basis, product, etc? Price adjustments will be your go-to in these situations. Say you have a customer that is your oldest and most loyal customer, you always give them a 10% discount no matter what. Accumulation For when there are multiple price adjustments competing with one another If one discount has a higher priority than the other, the highest priority will override the other discount Ex. if you have a new customer discount and a summer sale discount going on at the same time, you would want the new customer discount to have a higher accumulate than the other. Tip: Use the Always setting, so you know if you are discounting or surcharging every time. You can also discount customers or products with certain tags Tags (identifiers that groups customers together for a certain purpose) Companies, orders, products Ex. you have a group of customers who are developers, you can give them the "developer" tag and give that group of customers a price adjustment You can also group customers by price tier (10% surcharge, 10% discount, 20% discount, etc). How do you think you would group customers together? You can also add tags to products You can tag orders with sales reps, good with the order reports. Triggers How are you currently interacting with customers? Is it manual calls, emails, etc? Triggers can automate that process for you Notes Wanted to go in the other day and it wasn't doing pricing. Juan emailed yesterday saying he made some changes, now his selection are a lot better. Still putting a few things in. When he goes to a door type (looks a lot better) a slab door type his options are a few things. Wants to figure out what makes sense as far as naming goes. Has to come up with better names. Needs to clarify some of his names for Door Type. Why does it give you options for Glide type on Base: 1 Drawer - 1 Shelf Inside profile for Stiles and Rails? Wording is confusing on some of the validations. Door Type and Inside Profile Validations.

Title: Zoom Meeting with Sarah Pierce Date: Tue May 14 2024 15:45:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: cabtrends1@gmail.com, sarah@allmoxy.com, ray@allmoxy.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 20 Overview The meeting started with a sense of urgency due to **Juan's wife going into labor**. **Sarah** and **Ray** discussed **taking over Juan's tasks**, focusing on catalog options, validations, and pricing methodology. They reviewed slab thermal TFLs, door types, front materials, and dynamic attributes affecting product configurations, aiming to optimize offerings. **Sarah** emphasized clear costing calculations based on square footage costs and margins to streamline pricing strategies for profitability without compromising quality. The discussion extended to integrating assembly options to improve customer convenience and enhancing the user experience through **image integration** for visual representation of products like wood panels and laminate textures. Suggestions were made to source images accurately from suppliers. Low pricing estimates prompted a thorough examination of cost components such as box parts, door fronts, drawer sides, and material finishes, with a focus on refining pricing structure. The goal was to ensure profitability and quality standards alignment. The participants aimed to select top-selling doors from Lewis Cabinets, enhancing the catalog options and profit margins. Plans were outlined to investigate missing data points in pricing formulas through post-meeting follow-ups. The objective was to finalize efficient catalog options while considering improved profit margins. **Sarah** provided insights and suggestions to ensure comprehensive resolution steps towards achieving the desired pricing structure for the product line. Action Items **Brett** Investigate pricing structure and margins (12:58) Review the square foot cost of materials for accurate pricing (14:19) Consider adding assembly as a dropdown option (23:32) **Sarah Pierce** [+ 3 More] Outline Chapter 1: Project Progress and Next Steps (00:16 - 03:13) - **Sarah** discusses the need to expedite project completion. - **Brett** inquires about moving to phase two and pricing considerations. - Decision to focus on pricing and methodology for product catalog. Chapter 2: Pricing Strategy Discussion (03:13 - 09:51) [+ 12 More] Shorthand Bullet 📊 **Reviewing Progress** - **Sarah Pierce** to review current status and progress with **Brett** on catalog work. - Discussion on transitioning work from Juan to a new team member, with input from Raymundo Cantu. 📑 **Phase Two Planning** - Planning for phase two tasks and next steps in the project. [+ 13 More] Gist Urgent meeting due to Juan's wife in labor. Discussion on tasks, pricing strategies, image integration, and profit margins. Bullet Gist ⏰ Urgency: Meeting started urgently due to Juan's wife going into labor. 💼 Tasks and Strategies: Discussion on tasks handover, pricing strategies, and product optimizations. 💰 Profit Focus: Emphasis on profit margins and cost components for pricing structure. 🖼️ Image Integration: Suggestions for visual representation through image integration. 🔍 Exploration: Plans to investigate missing data points and refine catalog options. Short Summary The meeting commenced urgently as Juan's wife was in labor. Sarah and Ray delved into assuming Juan's tasks, refining pricing strategies, and enhancing product options like slab thermal TFLs and door types. Sarah emphasized clear costing calculations and suggested image integration for better customer experience. The focus was on optimizing profit margins while maintaining quality standards and exploring top-selling doors from Lewis Cabinets.

This customer is ready for a services agreement


Send Agreement and update the amount on the deal card.

CS Onboarding Playbook Is this a VIP/Legacy customer?(If you're unsure check with leadership) No Is our Services team being utilized for this build? Yes, Services owning entire build Notes: Juan is owning the build. 6 weeks and 10.5K Who is the main POC within the customer? Brett Harris Confirm the customers desired Outcome for using Allmoxy Online Storefront(No more hand to paper ordering) Notes: Brett has been running around and measuring out jobs himself. He goes back to the shop, puts it into his computer, puts everything in cabinet vision, and builds. He wants to eliminate that part and have people enter orders in themselves so they can focus on the building. When is the customers goal launch date? 05/20/2024 What cadence of meeting schedule did you set with the customer? Notes: Weekly Ask the customer to define their benchmark for success Eliminate measuring jobs himself, wants customers to order online What Software(s) does the customer have? How will Allmoxy work with these softwares? Cabinet Vision, Quickbooks Is the customer looking to fit Allmoxy around an existing ordering system or is this their first?(Utilize your Services Rep to more deeply understand how this impacts the catalog build.) Notes: This is their ordering system. Is the customer letting you drive the meeting and follow an agenda or do they want to drive and take charge of meetings? Either can be okay, but dependent on the customers approach to onboarding you will need to pivot your approach to working with them. Notes: I will have them drive, but stick a rough agenda. Any initial concerns with the customer and their ability to launch(personnel, resources available, understanding of product, etc?) Notes: They don't seem the most tech-savvy, but they are open to our suggestions and want to launch with what they need and add things on later. I think this will run smoothly.

First onboarding meeting 4/12/24 Goal for this call: the introduction to onboarding, making sure that I am on the same page as you guys are far as expectations, what you're looking to achieve, and when we are looking to do it by. My main goal for this first part is so I can make sure you guys are getting everything you need to Juan and things start being built. In the meantime we can talk about more big picture stuff. Process for shipping, accounting, how you are going to be billing, and how they run orders. Stripe and payment (terms or pay before processing?) 1) Introductions and background Brett has been in business 30 years. They build cabinets for local. They are a small shop. 6-8 employees. Now they are ready to start selling online. They want to build flat pack mostly. Cut them out, drill and dow, stained and painted cabinets, flat pack and ship wherever. He has been installing all of the cabinets he has built. 2) Timeline, we want to make sure that we are staying in line with those expectations from the get-go. 6 weeks 3) Getting started with Allmoxy- what I can help with vs. what our services team will be helping with and how we will all collaborate. 4) Outline the next steps and how we will be working together moving forward 45 minutes weekly Questions to ask How are you currently running orders? What does that process look like? Brett is running around and measuring individual jobs himself. He comes back to the shop, puts the measurements into his computer, uses cabinet vision, and starts the job. They eventually want to do shipping, right now just will call. With Allmoxy, are you planning on having customers create accounts and place orders themselves OR will you be using it more of an internal tool at first? They want customers to sign up and place orders themselves. Customers dealers Who are going to be the main users of Allmoxy? Brett and Dave Is there a specific date to get this launched by? (From what we typically see, the catalog build is the most complex part and where the majority of the work stems from- it really depends on how much time is available to dedicate towards getting the correct information) What do we want to consider as a success for this first phase of Allmoxy integration (working with services and myself) Online ordering for customers and dealers. He doesn't want to be running around, measuring jobs, putting in the computer/ cabinet vision, and repeating. He wants Allmoxy to help alleviate that process. Get Allmoxy up and running so he can focus on getting more business and finding dealers. Reiterate This is an iterative process and we see that customers who find the most success with Allmoxy are the ones who don't try to set up everything under the sun to the last minute detail before launching. It can muddy the waters a bit and become too many moving parts at one time --> we recommend setting up to the point where you can place and run orders effectively, receive payments, and automate your business with Allmoxy. That is where the value is found the quickest, after you get used to using the system you will definitely come up with ideas and process improvements that you will implement. Adding things halfway through the build process that are outside of the original scope call can also delay things. Which is totally fine if you guys want to do that, it's just important to acknowledge that it could extend the timeline. Suggest Ask about the timeline and how they feel about doing shorter 30-minute check-ins each week until services is halfway through the build.   They are open to our suggestions, but they aren't very tech-savvy from what I can tell so we may need to be more hands on. Notes They were confused on what information Juan is asking for --> Juan sent them an example and I am also going to send them an article on pricing methods They are no longer going to be doing doors, he thinks he is going to be buying doors from Lewis. Shipping is new to them, they aren't to that point yet. They are taking a big step with Allmoxy They use Cabinet Vision and QB, with

engagements.title: Brett Harris, Canyon Creek Cabinets Inc- Allmoxy Scope / Integrations
engagements.body: Brett Harris, Canyon Creek Cabinets Inc- Allmoxy Scope / Integrations

$899 USD/month. Twelve month contract agreement Sam Alexander initiates a call, ensuring the participant is not muted. He then discusses Canyon Creek's business model with Brett and their interest in online sales. Sam provides insights into Allmoxy's background and its integration capabilities with software like Cabinet Vision. They discuss Canyon Creek's current software usage and future goals of expanding beyond local markets. Sam explains how Allmoxy can streamline the online ordering process for dealers and contractors. Brett shares details about his cabinet shop experience, plans to scale the business, use optimization programs, accounting methods, and growth targets for quadrupling last year's revenue. Sam introduces features like resource allocation for inventory management, order creation within Allmoxy including pricing calculations based on measurements inputted by users. Sam delves into detailed explanations about various functionalities including customer-facing screens showing outstanding balances and invoices in real-time as well as creating orders with customized options using catalogs within Allmoxy. The discussion further covers integrations with Quickbooks for seamless accounting processes along with triggers to automate tasks such as sending emails or assigning job-related responsibilities. The conversation extends to discussions on trade shows attended by both parties leading to potential discounts offered by Allmoxy at trade shows or through referrals from existing clients. Pricing structures are detailed based on revenue tiers alongside integration costs involving catalog setup aligned between Cabinet Vision and Allmoxy systems. Sam discusses materials and customization options available within Allmoxy for paint finishing. Brett expresses readiness to proceed with onboarding steps after cabinet vision setup. Sam outlines the onboarding process, including assigning reps and scheduling calls for integration. They discuss the 3D designer tool and future improvements. Sam mentions plans to integrate designs from platforms like 2020 into Allmoxy. Brett inquires about real-time design updates for dealers using the platform, leading to a discussion on functionalities and attachments for customer collaboration. The meeting concludes with Sam offering support and sending over onboarding steps, expressing excitement about working together. Action items: Responsible Person: Sam Alexander Action Items: Ensure the full catalog is built out in Cabinet Vision - Brett Discuss integration with QuickBooks Online and determine any additional setup needed - Brett Action Items: Sam Alexander: Send the onboarding steps after the call. Assign a customer success rep and an integration rep. Schedule an initial scope call with the integration team for onboarding. Provide information such as cabinet vision catalog and pricing during onboarding calls. Brett: Look over the provided materials list and be ready to roll by tomorrow. Set up Cabinet Vision in the next week or two. Prepare for upcoming meetings, including training and machine setup next week. If you need further assistance or clarification on these action items, feel free to reach out!

02/19/26 - Cancellation Update Churn risk playbook context: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/4910812/record/2-39181518/22287937875/?engagement=100576749312 Phoenix Direct has confirmed that they are closing their business and in their words "it no longer exists". Email chain can be found here under Eric's Hubspot Contact. Madison and I have pushed them over the last month and a half to try and find a resolution that works for everyone, either a buyout of their contract or potentially giving them a pause if they were going into manufacturing. Ultimately they are saying they won't pay the buyout as they are closing their doors for good. We've escalated this to Brady and he approved no further action at this time. The remaining 75% buyout amount would be $5,392.80.

02/09/26 - Escalation Meeting (Madison, Brady, Carson, Nicole, and Eric) Agenda: Meeting Agenda: Phoenix Direct Partnership Discussion 1. Purpose and Framing Confirm attendees Align on goal: discuss current challenges, future fit, and partnership path forward 2. Phoenix Direct Current State Acknowledge lean team, cost constraints, and dealer-model limitations Confirm why automation is not a near-term need 3. Allmoxy Context and Prior Investment Recap what has already been built in Allmoxy Reinforce Allmoxy’s role as a long-term manufacturing solution, not a CRM 4. Retention and Flexibility Discussion Explore whether there is a viable way to continue the relationship short-term Avoid pricing or buyout specifics in the meeting 5. Manufacturing Future Alignment Discuss Phoenix Direct’s long-term plan to manufacture Position Allmoxy as the preferred platform when that transition occurs Address data retention and reuse at a high level 6. Contract Context and Next Conversations Acknowledge buyout interest without discussing numbers Set expectation for internal review and follow-up Introduce a future discussion with Allmoxy ownership/sales leadership 7. Wrap-Up Confirm mutual intent to preserve the relationship Align on next steps and post-meeting follow-up Didn't show.

6/18/25 - IMP Meeting (Parts and Validation Distribution) - https://app.fireflies.ai/view/Meeting-with-Mike-Frick-from-Phoenix-Direct::01JXWHMAWX5MH3KXXV2BN3J1KY Here is the structured meeting summary, ready for HubSpot: Meeting Summary The team met to discuss automating the validation process for door product frame sizes, focusing on minimum and maximum style and rail dimensions. Michael demonstrated his current manual setup using the Adobe 830 as a test case. Aaron provided a more efficient method for handling validations by consolidating part attributes into a single part, which can then be propagated across multiple products. Discussions also included the use of proxy variables and future scalability considerations. Michael provided updates on his progress and outlined next steps. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Michael has created manual validations for the Adobe 830 door style, checking min/max rail and style dimensions. Aaron proposed restructuring by consolidating all part attributes into a single "validations" part, each attribute representing a unique validation formula. This allows for streamlined propagation using the Allmoxy toolset and simplifies future updates. Technical Issues & Fixes Original setup involved separate parts and validations, which would be cumbersome to duplicate. Aaron advised creating part attributes under one validation part instead of multiple parts to ease duplication and maintenance. The strategy improves future scalability and allows validations to be managed centrally. Decisions Made Michael will reconfigure his validation approach by implementing Aaron’s method. All validation formulas will be included as attributes within one part for better management and propagation. Proxy variables will not be used to drive validations in this case due to their complexity and existing dependencies. Challenges or Concerns Michael expressed concern about reworking previous configurations, indicating this approach would have been preferable from the start. The process of editing each door style is time-consuming and susceptible to human error if done for too long without breaks. The complexity of proxy variables makes them unsuitable for reuse in validation unless carefully managed. Action Items Michael to restructure current validation setup into a single part with multiple part attributes as advised. Test new validation part thoroughly on Adobe 830. Remove existing individual validations to prevent conflicts. Email Aaron after testing for review and further guidance. Prepare to propagate new validation setup across all wood doors. Next Steps Michael will continue updating door style part attributes with min/max dimensions using data from Sarah’s Excel file. Upon successful validation of the test product, begin propagation of the unified validation part across wood products. Post-validation, proceed to update thermofoil/RTF doors, focusing on the color and back color attributes. Coordinate with Aaron for a final review and use Allmoxy’s toolset for broad implementation.

05/08/25 - Decorative Export Meeting - https://app.fireflies.ai/view/Phoenix-Direct-Allmoxy-Decor-Export-Working-Session::01JTK8PR7Y319GTJKJRZM41ZYF Agenda: Aaron reviewed the schema and sample order Need Aaron and Mike to work together to ensure the file is outputting the necessary codes Next steps? Meeting Summary The team conducted a detailed working session focused on finalizing the export schema and order flow between Allmoxy and Decorative. The discussion primarily involved ensuring accurate XML file generation, aligning attribute mappings (especially decorative codes), clarifying shipping logic, and verifying product and material configurations. Participants reviewed product part number mapping, validated attribute fields, addressed conditional logic for shipping addresses, and confirmed how to test and eventually automate the export-to-Decorative workflow. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Export Schema Review: Aaron shared the current state of the export configuration including headers, footers, and product-level data. The team confirmed that headers and footers are properly structured and only product-level attributes need further configuration. Customer Code Handling: Decorative's need to differentiate shipping addresses (e.g., to Phoenix Direct vs. end customer) was discussed. Allmoxy's export currently uses shipping address data, but enhancements may include a toggle to specify destination. Attribute Matching: Michael highlighted that each attribute (e.g., door style) has a corresponding "Decor Code" that must be correctly picked up in the XML. Capitalization mismatches (e.g., "Decorative" vs. "decorative") were identified as potential pitfalls and corrected. Schema Guidance: Sanjeev and Sarah from Decorative will need to provide naming conventions for additional attributes like left/right rails and styles. Technical Issues & Fixes Decorative Code Capitalization: An inconsistency in the casing of the "Decorative Code" attribute was causing issues in the export. Aaron updated the proxy variables to match the capitalized version. Product Mapping Errors: Confusion around product variants (e.g., Adobe 289 P1 vs. mitered) and permissible configurations (solid vs. quarter-inch panels) was clarified. Some misalignments will require validation rule updates. Shipping Logic Validation: Currently, shipping address is used from the order record. Future updates may include a user-defined toggle to streamline internal orders or drop-shipping. Decisions Made Proceed with current XML export structure for testing. Use the "Decor Export" button from the order ribbon to generate XML for QA purposes. Decorative orders will initially be tested via manual file review, then progressively automated using email triggers and potentially Zapier-to-FTP automation. Michael will contact Sarah and Sanjeev to update the schema with new attribute names for left/right rails and styles. Challenges or Concerns Product validation logic needs to reflect current business rules (e.g., decommissioned products must be flagged for manual orders). Not all decorative codes were visible or linked correctly, requiring further checks across attributes. Schema incompleteness on Decorative’s side may delay final automation steps unless naming guidance is provided promptly. Action Items ✅ Aaron: Finalize proxy variable mapping, especially material attributes and decorative codes. ✅ Aaron: Update export logic to reference appropriate attribute names for rail and stile widths. 🟡 Michael: Contact Sarah and Sanjeev to request schema updates for left/right and center rails/stiles. 🟡 Michael: Begin running test orders and reviewing XML files internally before sending to Decorative. ✅ Carson: Confirm capability for Allmoxy to automate export-email-to-FTP via Zapier. 🟡 Team: Review attribute tables for blanks/missing values; validate using Excel exports as needed. Next Steps Michael to send schema update request to Decorative and await their revised structure. Team to complete internal test orders, review XMLs thoroughly, and compare against UI selections. When ready, trigger QA submissions to Decorative’s review email for verification. If tests pass, consider scheduling a live order test and begin refining Zapier automation for end-to-end integration.

engagements.title: Phoenix Direct & Allmoxy | Decor Export Working Session
engagements.body: Phoenix Direct & Allmoxy | Decor Export Working Session

04/23/25 - Decorative Export Meeting - https://app.fireflies.ai/view/Phoenix-Direct-Allmoxy-Decore-Decore-Export::01JSDAHR8H3JJ9RJGVK11WR7YP Agenda: Review the export outline that Juan previously created Reconfirm which of the data points need to come over to Decorative Ensure these are all outlined within Phoenix's products Aaron to share the best way to test this/approach building the export Get confirmation on any other needs we'll have Meeting Summary The meeting centered on finalizing the approach for exporting product data from Allmoxy to Decorative (referred to as "Decor") via an XML interface. This integration is essential for streamlining order processing and ensuring accurate transmission of product details, including style codes, part dimensions, and customer-specific configurations. The goal was to align on technical requirements, testing procedures, and data flow logistics for a successful implementation. Key stakeholders included Allmoxy (Carson, Aaron, Michael) and the Decor team (Sanjeev, Sarahn), along with contributions from Eric and Nicole. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Export Strategy: Allmoxy will export product data to Decor using XML files. These files will include detailed product specs, customer codes, and dimensional information. Schema Customization: Sanjeev presented Decor's flexible XML schema structure. It can be customized to support Allmoxy’s unique configurations, such as French light door variants and customer-specific codes. Integration Scope: Orders can contain multiple line types (doors, drawer fronts, glass doors, drawer boxes), and the XML will handle them in a nested structure. Data Elements & Logic: Orders must include correct part numbers and style codes to avoid rejection. If certain parameters (e.g., inside edge) are missing, defaults tied to the style code are used. Glass door configurations (e.g., 2-light stacked vs side-by-side) will require specific codes. Customer Code Handling: Multiple customer codes must be embedded in the XML, specified in the order header. Technical Issues & Fixes Trigger Limitations: Triggers in Allmoxy currently cannot send files directly to an SFTP; they can only email or send webhooks. FTP integration may require API development. Schema Evolution: Previous XML files (developed with Juan) may need validation against the updated schema from Decor. Decisions Made Decor will send the current draft schema and sample XML files to Allmoxy (Michael & Aaron). Initial test orders will be emailed manually for QA review before automating the export. An SFTP pipeline will be set up for automated XML submission once validation is complete. Challenges or Concerns Potential for missing codes in Allmoxy’s system for less common configurations. Need for robust validation on Allmoxy’s side before submitting files to Decor to avoid rejections. Coordination required to ensure all light pattern configurations (2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10 lights) are properly mapped and coded. Action Items Task Owner Deadline / Notes Send updated XML schema + sample files to Aaron and Michael Sanjeev / Sarahn Immediate Review schema and align it with Allmoxy’s database Michael, Aaron Before next test phase Identify and provide any missing light pattern or style codes Sarahn As needed upon request Validate generated XML files before sending to Decor Aaron Ongoing Investigate FTP file delivery via API or alternative method Aaron + CTO Follow-up needed Setup trigger-based email for test file submission Michael Temporary solution Coordinate SFTP setup for QA phase Sarahn, Sanjeev In progress Next Steps Schema Review: Michael and Aaron will review the schema to ensure it aligns with internal data structures and product configurations. XML File Generation & Testing: Begin generating sample XML files using existing orders and configurations. Files will be emailed for initial QA. FTP Integration Plan: Determine feasibility of automating FTP transfers from Allmoxy. May involve support from Allmoxy’s CTO. Ongoing QA Feedback Loop: Decor will provide feedback on submitted XMLs. Allmoxy will iterate and resolve issues based on validation results. UI Mapping Review: Future meeting to review Allmoxy’s order interface (particularly for French light doors) to ensure all necessary selections are captured for XML export.

04/07/25 - Check-in Meeting with Mike - https://app.fireflies.ai/view/Phoenix-Direct-Allmoxy-Bi-weekly::01JR3PCA1XG44P24YK1YRES5D2 Agenda: Progress on the cloning? How many products left in each category? Wood - complete RTF - complete? MDF Expression Edgebanded Final stages When will you need help diving into the export? Next Steps Once all products are complete we'll schedule a meeting with Aaron our head engineer to start the building process for decorative. It'd be helpful if we could get an example file ahead of time of how the data will need to come from Allmoxy in the XML format. (need to know what we're trying to match or create so the fields can be mapped appropriately) Notes: Made a lot of progress, gotten everything cloned. Talked with Eric about scheduling a call with our team and decorative. Method of how we do it setup prior to testing Skeleton export built Meeting Summary This meeting focused on final preparations and coordination for initiating XML file transfers from Allmoxy to Decorative. The primary goal was to align on testing strategies, establish a clear method of transmission, and ensure all necessary technical and logistical components are in place before testing begins. Discussions covered status triggers, email automation, potential use of webhooks or Zapier, and the need for joint alignment with Decorative’s Sarah Nichols. The call emphasized the importance of setting up the correct production-ready method before initiating any test transmissions to avoid rework. Key Takeaways Important Discussions XML Integration with Decorative: The team is preparing to begin testing the transmission of XML files from Allmoxy to Decorative. Discussions highlighted the need to validate both the structure and content of the XML files and how they're processed on the Decorative side. A skeleton XML export, originally created by Juan, is already set up in the system and will serve as the starting point. Triggering Mechanism: The approach involves using an order status change (e.g., “verified” or “in progress”) as a trigger to send the XML file. Allmoxy can attach the XML file to an email triggered by this status change. Alternative Delivery Methods Considered: Webhooks were mentioned as a potential alternative for transmitting data. Carson suggested exploring tools like Zapier to process email attachments and automate routing the XML file to the appropriate system at Decorative. Clarification Needed from Decorative: It’s crucial to understand how Decorative expects to receive and process these XML files. Questions remain regarding whether Decorative will automate file intake into their shop order system or require manual vetting. Decisions Made Testing should be based on the final method intended for production to avoid redundant work. All communication and coordination regarding scheduling will go through Carson, who will loop in Allmoxy’s lead engineer (successor to Juan). Challenges or Concerns Decorative’s system specifics are still unclear—Sarah Nichols needs to confirm how incoming files should be handled. Data field mapping inconsistencies exist; some decorative-specific parameters are missing in the Allmoxy database and will need to be clarified by Sarah. Action Items Michael Frick: Coordinate with Eric and Sarah Nichols to identify a suitable time for a joint call. Validate that all necessary parameters are available in the XML and identify any gaps requiring input from Sarah. Carson Ward: Provide availability windows for Allmoxy’s head engineer (ideally 8–10 AM MT) to facilitate scheduling. Prepare for the kickoff meeting by reviewing Juan’s XML skeleton and confirming its structure. Sarah Nichols (Decorative): Review the XML structure and clarify: How files should be received (email, webhook, direct integration). If automation is in place or if manual steps are needed for intake into their system. Any required parameters or formatting specifics. Next Steps Finalize scheduling of a kickoff call including Allmoxy, Decorative, and internal stakeholders (Eric, Sarah, Carson, and the head engineer). Review and finalize the XML file structure and identify any missing parameters. Confirm Decorative’s system expectations and workflow for receiving and processing XML files. Begin formal testing once the production-ready transmission method is established.

Subscription Reduction Requested - 02/17/25 Context: When reaching out to Phoenix Direct for a renewal on their contract, Nicole a co-owner, has requested a discount or "courtesy credit" as they felt they weren't supported during their initial sign-up stage with Allmoxy. Their Summary: It has been a very difficult road for us with one year of paying for Allmoxy under our belts without getting any use of it. The service we received throughout our first year was not up to par, in my opinion. We were unable to get started in a timely manner due to difficulty booking time with Juan. His schedule was always very tight. We had to consistently wait and wait. My stance is that we deserve a courtesy credit of at least the next 3 months waived, or we negotiate a permanent lower monthly payment. We need to either complete the build-out or be done with it. I do not think we were adequately supported during our first year to get Allmoxy up and running so we can actually use it. Allmoxy was a huge money pit for us in our first year of business, and I am in the process of exploring other avenues, as we speak. Please let us know what you can do. Thank you. Research: In reviewing the account's activity logs I'm unable to find any record of Juan not being available or where Allmoxy caused a delay in their ability to move forward with their catalog build or implementation overall. In the scoping meeting it was discussed that their objective was to approach this as a hybrid build and have training with Juan. In total they met with Juan 27 times throughout Jan - Aug of 2024 and at no point shared that they felt it was hard to reach Juan or complete the catalog work. Next Steps - drafting a response to share that at no point did we fail to provide service that supported their goals in utilizing Allmoxy. @Madison Theisen @Sarah Pierce for vis.

Title: Allmoxy Scope Call - Neu-Bau LLC Date: Wed Jun 25 2025 16:45:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, bcaldwell@neu-bau.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 64 mins Overview - Neu-Bau needs to create an Allmoxy catalog from scratch; Steve requested examples to guide input requirements. - Allmoxy offers unlimited customizable dropdowns for materials and finishes; Steve needs clearer communication on customization. - Cabinet Vision catalog file provided by Bobby matched the demo catalog; integration workflow was explained despite Steve's connectivity issues. - Each cabinet configuration requires separate files in Cabinet Vision; material schedules must align with Allmoxy dropdowns. - Neu-Bau's pricing strategy uses a 3x material cost multiplier, with Bobby suggesting a cost-plus margin approach. - Neu-Bau plans three tiered catalogs targeting different market segments; Steve seeks resources for pricing strategy improvement. - Thorough understanding of throughput accounting and its benefits emphasized for effective pricing and financial planning. - Custom images can replace default catalog images in Allmoxy; accurate part counts are necessary for pricing models. - Action plan centers around studying Allmoxy's catalog structure and defining product attributes; further integration steps identified. - Ongoing challenges with organizing menu items and drawing from scratch in Cabinet Vision were noted for future troubleshooting. Notes 🔧 Technical Setup & Initial Discussion Meeting recording initiated with audio troubleshooting resolved Steve requested an Excel file or catalog example for input requirements Neu-Bau needs to create a catalog from scratch for Allmoxy system Bobby directed Steve to demo.almoxy.com for existing cabinet catalog examples 📚 Catalog Structure & Customization Requirements Allmoxy dropdowns are unlimited and customizable for materials and finishes Steve expressed confusion about communicating customization requirements Bobby explained the catalog serves as a starting point for customizations Steve lacks organized spreadsheet of menu items and needs guidance Current Neu-Bau process involves drawing from scratch in Cabinet Vision 🔄 Cabinet Vision Integration Process Bobby provided Cabinet Vision catalog file matching demo catalog Demonstrated import/export process for Cabinet Vision Steve experienced connectivity issues but understood the technical handshake Integration workflow explained from customer orders to nesting process ⚙️ Product Configuration Requirements Steve clarified need for separate Cabinet Vision files for each cabinet configuration Bobby confirmed each product type requires individual files Construction methods handled through dropdown parameters Material schedules in Cabinet Vision need mapping to Allmoxy dropdowns Neu-Bau recently cleaned material schedule to include only actively used materials 💰 Pricing Strategy & Business Consultation Neu-Bau uses 3x material cost multiplier with market competitiveness concerns Bobby recommended cost-plus margin pricing approach Neu-Bau planning three catalog tiers for different market segments Steve seeking pricing strategy resources due to limited market knowledge Bobby introduced throughput accounting concept and its benefits 🗂️ Implementation Planning & Next Steps Neu-Bau inputs jobs into Cabinet Vision and applies 3x margin Bobby validated current approach aligns with throughput accounting Action plan includes studying Allmoxy catalog structure and defining product attributes Custom images can replace default catalog images Accurate part counts needed for pricing models Action Items Steve Download Cabinet Vision catalog file from Allmoxy Knowledge Base support section Study throughput accounting principles starting with Wikipedia research Review existing Allmoxy demo catalog structure to understand product attribute requirements Build parallel Cabinet Vision catalog configuration matching Allmoxy structure Create individual Cabinet Vision files for each cabinet product type needed in catalog Define dropdown menu options for box materials, door types, and construction methods Bobby Lewis Provide guidance on mapping Cabinet Vision material schedules to Allmoxy dropdown menus Assist with pricing model setup and margin calculations during implementation Help configure product attributes and export names for Cabinet Vision integration

Meeting with Brian, Neu-Bau Team, and Bobby 6/12/25 Meeting Summary Josh Baker (Customer Success Manager at Allmoxy) met with the full NuBo team—Brian, Lane Ferris, Steve, and Bobby Lewis—to reconnect and explore how Allmoxy might better support NuBo’s workflow. The meeting centered on assessing Allmoxy’s fit for NuBo’s evolving business model, particularly in leveraging Revit as a design platform and linking data into Cabinet Vision. Bobby provided deep insights into Allmoxy's B2B strengths, order handling capabilities, and technical integration paths. The conversation clarified potential misalignments with current tool usage and opened a pathway for a tailored integration approach using custom catalogs, CSV imports, and API tools. Key Takeaways Important Discussions NuBo initially intended to use Allmoxy for direct-to-consumer (DTC) RTA cabinets but has since pivoted to internal operations and B2B engagements. Their current goal is to automate their shop drawing process by integrating Revit with Cabinet Vision, bypassing graphic interfaces like those in Cabinet Vision. All three team members are architects familiar with tools like Revit and Rhino and want to build a workflow that reflects their design-forward methodology. Allmoxy is primarily designed as a manufacturing and order-processing tool for B2B transactions—especially for outsourcing manufacturing and enabling resellers or designers to place configurable orders. Technical Issues & Fixes Allmoxy may not be ideal as a middleware tool strictly between Revit and Cabinet Vision unless NuBo also serves external customers. Bobby confirmed that while Allmoxy can receive imports and has open API functionality, its configurator is not built for advanced graphical interfacing like Revit. The challenge lies in automating the transfer of cabinet specs (CSV export from Revit) into Allmoxy and then into Cabinet Vision. The ORD (order) files generated by Allmoxy can accommodate various cabinet specifications but are limited when it comes to reconfiguring internal cabinet structures dynamically. Decisions Made NuBo acknowledged Allmoxy's current catalog-building process is a barrier to entry but agreed it's critical to implementation. Both sides agreed that building the catalog is the next logical step, starting from a provided example/demo catalog. The team agreed to send Allmoxy their current Revit-to-Cabinet Vision CSV files and an overview of their Revit families to allow Allmoxy to provide an aligned setup plan. Bobby committed to sending a demo link, example Cabinet Vision catalog (PKG), and attribute spreadsheet for Revit export alignment. Challenges or Concerns NuBo does not currently have a built Cabinet Vision catalog and is concerned about how to structure it for seamless integration. They expressed a desire to avoid ever drafting in Cabinet Vision again and focus strictly on using it for nesting and part generation. The team flagged that only 7 data columns currently work in Cabinet Vision imports—posing a challenge for deeper customization. Catalog building feels daunting due to unknown starting points, potential formatting pitfalls, and undefined product structures. Action Items NuBo Team to send: Sample CSV export from Revit that they currently use for Cabinet Vision. Sample Revit family/cabinet catalog structure for analysis. Allmoxy (Bobby Lewis) to send: A demo link showing a complete Allmoxy catalog. A Cabinet Vision PKG file with the matching catalog. A spreadsheet with attribute columns required for seamless CSV import into Allmoxy. A link to schedule a scope call post-review. Next Steps NuBo will evaluate the sample catalog and identify which cabinets or configurations need to be added or modified. Once reviewed, they will schedule a follow-up scope call with Bobby to define catalog buildout specifics and Revit integration logic. Further exploration of Allmoxy’s pricing engine and throughput accounting methodology to potentially improve NuBo's internal costing model. Allmoxy will support aligning CSV data output from Revit to match attribute expectations within the system to ensure future import compatibility.

They have been paying 899/ month and have not missed a payment since 2023 They never launched
