02/12/26 - Sarah, Spencer, and Carson - https://app.fireflies.ai/view/CDU-Allmoxy::01KH7CMJE246AQZW2HVGZF1ACR Agenda: Opening statement: I know there’s been frustration around the cut list accuracy, and that’s completely fair. The good news is we’re not dealing with a system limitation or broken logic, we’re down to aligning exactly with the formulas Door Pro is using. We’re extremely close. Once we have those calculations clearly outlined, we can mirror them directly and apply them globally. Today is really about locking in that final piece so we can cross the finish line.” 1. Goal Confirm the exact formulas Door Pro is using so we can match them 1:1 and finalize cut list accuracy. 2. Main Issues to Resolve Arch doors with mid rail & stiles Exact panel height formula Any arch-specific adjustments (e.g., 1/16 variance) How top rail sizing impacts panel calculation Standard square doors Why basic shaker doors are still producing incorrect cut lists Confirm rail/stile deductions and inset logic Confirm order of operations Mid rail and stile logic How deductions stack with multiple mid rails Any differences between square vs. arch logic 3. What We Need from Door Pro Written formulas for: Panel height (square) Panel height (arch) Panel height with multiple mid rails Clear order of operations (what is subtracted and when) Confirmation of inset values being applied 4. Closeout Plan Build one comprehensive test order. Compare results against Door Pro. Once formulas match, implement globally and finalize. Notes: Meeting Summary Continued troubleshooting incorrect panel height calculations for arch doors in the ordering system. Confirmed that panel sizing for arch doors should ultimately match square doors; the only true difference is additional rail material for arches. Identified inconsistencies in how the 15/16 inch and 1 3/4 inch arch additions are being applied in formulas. Discovered mid rail deductions and panel insets are not being consistently incorporated into height calculations. Agreed that order of operations and hard-coded logic in the system are likely causing panel height discrepancies. Reviewed Door Pro configuration, arch style IDs, and associated rail dimensions for multiple arch types (14, 24, 44, 54). Determined the need for additional cut sheet comparisons to validate deduction logic across different mid rail configurations. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Arch doors are built square and carved afterward; therefore, final panel dimensions should match equivalent square door configurations. The only structural difference is increased top rail size for arches. 15/16 inch is added once per shallow arch and twice for double arches. 1 3/4 inch applies to deep arches, once per arch (twice for double deep arches). Current system logic hard-codes certain values and may incorrectly multiply additions. Mid rails must deduct material and include inset calculations on both sides of each rail. Panel rail inset (7/16 inch per side) must be accounted for across top, bottom, and each mid rail. Technical Issues & Fixes Panel height currently calculating approximately 5/16 inch too tall in some configurations. System was multiplying arch additions incorrectly in certain cases. Mid rail deductions were either missing or not incorporated properly before panel division. Order of operations (when additions and deductions occur relative to dividing by panel count) is likely incorrect. Formula adjustments were made to test adding arch values once (instead of twice) for single arches. Need to confirm rounding behavior to nearest 1/16 inch. Investigated possibility of viewing Door Pro backend calculations or modifying reports to display deduction logic. Decisions Made Arch additions must be applied once per arch instance, not automatically doubled unless double arch. Deep vs. shallow arch determines whether 15/16 inch or 1 3/4 inch is added. Panel inset must account for all rail interfaces, including mid rails. Testing will continue using total door size (not opening size). Challenges or Concerns Existing formula logic is difficult to follow due to hard-coded values and nested conditions. System may not be properly dividing panel height by total panel count in certain scenarios. Door Pro backend calculation transparency is limited. Risk of overcomplicating logic instead of aligning arch logic directly with square door baseline math. Action Items Lori to generate cut lists for an 11 3/4 inch by 81 1/4 inch door with: No mid rails One mid rail Two mid rails Three mid rails Ownership: Lori Lori to email the cut sheets to Spencer and Carson once completed. Ownership: Lori Continue refining panel height formula logic and test corrections. Ownership: Spencer Review square door calculations to ensure baseline panel height math is correct before replicating to arch doors. Ownership: Spencer Next Steps Compare cut sheet deductions across different mid rail configurations to isolate exact per-rail deductions. Validate arch panel additions (15/16 inch vs. 1 3/4 inch) across all arch styles (14, 24, 44, 54). Confirm correct rounding behavior to nearest 1/16 inch. Follow up tomorrow after formula testing and cut sheet comparison.

engagements.title: CDU & Allmoxy
engagements.body: CDU & Allmoxy

CDU & Allmoxy February 12, 2026 1:00 PM MST 78 mins sarah@allmoxy.com spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com carson@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com View Meeting Recording Arch Door Panel Calculation Issues The team identified critical gaps in the current formula for arch door panel sizing, mainly around rail dimensions and mid rail impacts (02:29). Formula Complexity Clarified by Spencer (02:58) Arch door panel sizes match square doors except for the top rail width difference of 15/16 inch. Panel size calculations incorrectly apply this rail difference twice, causing errors. Spencer explained panels should be calculated as if for a square door, then adjusted by the arch rail only once for single arches. This misunderstanding has caused the panel height and total door size calculations to be inconsistent. Order of Operations and Missing Mid Rail Logic (04:05) Carson and Sarah noted confusion about when to subtract rail widths and add insets within the formula. Mid rail deductions are currently not accounted for in the arch door panel height calculations. Sarah confirmed no mid rail condition exists in the arch door formulas, causing miscalculations on doors with mid rails. Spencer and Sarah agree mid rails must be added similarly as in square doors for correct panel sizing. Hard-Coding of Rail Width Additions Causing Errors (26:14) Sarah discovered the 15/16 inch rail addition is hard-coded rather than dynamic. Carson recalled this was based on a prior decision to hard-code rail sizes for arch types. The team agreed this attribute needs to be made adjustable per arch door style to handle variations like shallow and deep arches. William and Carson explored the Door Pro system to uncover how these hard-coded values impact calculations and if customization is possible. Panel Height Offsets and Rounding Issues (19:59, 38:49) Panels are consistently off by about 5/16 to 3/8 inch in height compared to Lori’s expected sizes. Sarah found calculations add rail insets twice when they should be added once for single arches, causing panels to be oversized. Rounding to the nearest 16th of an inch is inconsistent, adding to the sizing confusion. The team plans to standardize rounding and ensure rail inset additions occur before dividing by panel count. Mid Rail and Panel Inset Adjustments Accurate panel height depends heavily on incorporating mid rail widths and panel insets, which are currently misapplied or missing in formulas (32:05). Mid Rail Insets Must Multiply by Panel Count (01:04:37) Carson clarified that the panel inset value must be multiplied by two for each rail side, then multiplied by the number of mid rails plus top and bottom rails. Sarah tested adding 7/16 inch panel inset times six (for two mid rails plus top and bottom), which brought panel height calculations closer to the target size. This adjustment resolves previous underestimations in panel height by accounting for all rail insets correctly. Need for Conditional Formula Logic Based on Mid Rail Count (01:00:41) Sarah identified formula errors where division by the number of panels was not correctly implemented due to parentheses misplacement. Properly dividing the adjusted door height minus rail widths by the total number of panels (mid rails + 1) is critical for accurate sizing. William confirmed the requirement for conditions that handle zero or multiple mid rails to automate panel count calculation in the formula. Visual and Measurement Validation Planned with Lori’s Cut Lists (01:15:22) William will have Lori generate cut lists for the 11 3/4 inch by 81 1/4 inch door with zero, one, and two mid rails. This will provide authoritative reference data to verify panel sizing and rail inset deductions. Sarah and Carson will use these to finalize the formula corrections and confirm accuracy. Panel Widths Consistent; Height Is the Primary Problem (32:46) Lori confirmed panel widths are aligning well across door styles. The main discrepancy is with panel height calculations, especially with arch doors having mid rails. The team focused efforts on fixing height formulas to match physical cut list requirements. Arch Door Style and Rail Width Differentiation Understanding and correctly applying different arch door styles and their rail widths is essential for precise panel sizing (24:05, 41:00). Arch Door Style IDs Define Rail Widths and Arch Types (12:10) Arch door styles like 14, 24, 28, 44, and 54 correspond to specific arch shapes and rail dimensions. For example, style 14 has a top rail of 3.3125 inch and bottom rail of 2.375 inch, while style 24 has a top rail of 4.125 inch. Double arch styles add complexity by requiring rail width additions twice. Sarah and William clarified that only double arch doors should have the 15/16 inch rail addition applied twice. Deep vs. Standard Arch Variations (41:42) Deep arches have larger rail widths adding up to 1.75 inch increments, while standard arches use 15/16 inch. The team determined that the depth attribute might be less impactful than style ID and arch count for rail width calculations. Sarah hard-coded rail additions temporarily, planning to simplify the formula by removing deep arch conditions. Hard-Coded Rail Additions Need to be Attributes (44:29) Carson and Sarah agreed rail additions like 15/16 inch should be attributes tied to the arch door style rather than fixed values. This will allow formulas to dynamically adjust based on door style selections, improving maintainability and accuracy. Arch Door Panel Carving Explained Visually (34:02) William showed that arch door panels are cut as square panels initially, then carved to shape. The final panel dimensions correspond to equivalent square door panels once carving is complete. This supports the approach of calculating panel size as if it were a square panel before arch adjustments. Door Pro System Limitations and Workarounds The Door Pro software’s opacity and hard-coded formulas present challenges for accurate panel calculation, prompting research into reporting and calculation transparency (35:12). Limited Visibility into Calculation Logic (35:35) Carson researched Door Pro’s ability to show underlying math behind size calculations. The system reportedly can output nominal sizes, deductions, and finish sizes side by side in reports, but requires admin-level customization. William explored a “view calc” feature that shows formula steps for line items, though it is not widely used or understood. Dependency on Original Developer Knowledge (46:00) William and Sarah acknowledged heavy reliance on the original Door Pro system designer, Ray, who is the only expert on the complex formulas. This knowledge bottleneck poses risk if Ray retires or leaves, as the system is difficult to update or fix without him. Interim Process Proposal to Bypass Cut List (01:11:14) William suggested allowing orders with arch doors to be processed without generating cut lists temporarily. This would let them price orders correctly while manually entering measurements into Door Pro for production. This workaround aims to reduce bottlenecks until formulas are corrected. Door Size vs. Opening Size Data Entry Confusion (57:29) Carson and William discussed that Door Pro accepts both door size and cabinet opening size inputs, configurable per order. The team suspects confusion over which measurement is used in calculations is causing discrepancies. They confirmed their tests are based on total door size input, but Door Pro’s internal math may be tuned for opening sizes, complicating formula accuracy. Next Steps and Ownership for Formula Fixes The team agreed on clear next steps to finalize formula corrections and validate calculations with real data (01:15:50). Lori to Provide Cut Lists for Panel Validation (01:15:22) Lori will send cut lists for the test door with 0, 1, and 2 mid rails. These will help quantify deductions per mid rail and validate formula logic. Sarah Taking Lead on Formula Refinement (01:16:18) Sarah plans to fix formula errors, especially around parentheses and division logic, to ensure proper panel height calculation. She has availability tomorrow to work intensively on this and will update the team. Carson and William Supporting Research and Testing (01:16:05) William will assist by comparing Lori’s cut lists and running Door Pro tests. Carson continues investigating Door Pro reporting capabilities and formula customization options. Focus on Making Rail Insets Dynamic Attributes (44:30) The team agreed to remove hard-coded rail inset values and replace them with attributes linked to door style IDs. This change aims to future-proof the formulas and reduce manual errors. Temporary Workaround to Process Orders Without Cut Lists (01:11:24) To avoid production delays, orders with arch doors might proceed without cut lists while new formulas are finalized. Manual measurement entry will ensure production accuracy during this transition. Action Items Sarah Pierce Work on fixing the panel height formula to correctly handle arch panel adjustments and division by number of panels. (01:12:56) Coordinate with Lori to receive cut lists for arch doors with zero to three mid rails to analyze panel breaks and deductions. (01:15:52) Continue debugging and refining calculations for arch and square doors, especially handling panel rail inset multipliers and the correct order of operations. (01:07:00) William Turner Request Lori to generate cut lists for doors with varying mid rails for comparison and calculation verification. (01:15:14) Assist in testing adjustments and verifying panel calculations based on received cut lists. (01:11:10) Carson Ward Continue researching Door Pro capabilities to display detailed calculation math behind the final door and panel sizes for improved debugging. (35:54) Support Sarah by clarifying door style IDs, arch type attributes, and assist with formula logic edits based on hardcoded values and confirmed rail widths. (01:04:15) Spencer from Cabinet Doors Unlimited Share detailed door dimensions and panel sizing data with Allmoxy team for reference in formula corrections. (13:58) Join on computer to assist with deep dive into calculations and formula edits away from phone for clarity and efficiency. (18:07) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KH7CMJE246AQZW2HVGZF1ACR 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

02/06/26 - Check-in Meeting Agenda: Reviewing what Ray completed this week and confirming the formula breakdowns we need. Updates: Below is a clean, executive-ready hit list you can use to walk Spencer and Lori through exactly what has been updated, what is complete, and where confirmation or decisions may still be needed. This is aligned directly to your Product Update Outline and reflects what Ray actually implemented or intentionally left unchanged. END PANELS Create End Panel Parts What to highlight: Individual part definitions were created for End Panels, mirroring door logic: Door Specs Panels Stiles and Mullions Rails Mid Stile Mid Rail All parts use the same dimensions, quantities, and material logic as standard door parts. Parts were copied from existing door logic (square and arched) to avoid introducing new math or risk. Callout: Mid rail and mid stile logic was validated to ensure parts only appear when quantities are greater than zero. Any prior issues with mid rail appearing incorrectly have been corrected. New End Panel Output Page What to highlight: A dedicated End Panel cutsheet/output page was created. End panel parts are isolated and do not appear on door or drawer cutsheets. Output page includes: Door specs Panel sizes Stiles and rails Mid stiles and mid rails (only when applicable) Naming was updated from “Door Specs” to “End Panel Specs” for clarity. End Panel Cutlist Formula What to highlight: Uses the same core cutlist math as doors. Confirmed exceptions are implemented: No upcharge logic when stiles or rails exceed 2 3/8 inches. End panel labor price is applied as $0.00. No new pricing logic was introduced beyond what was explicitly requested. ARCHED END PANELS Arched End Panel Pricing What to highlight: Arched End Panel pricing is implemented as: Original End Panel price formula × 2 This multiplier applies to all arched end panel selections. Important clarification to flag: Existing arch upcharges were already baked into the original formula. Because of this, arched pricing may appear higher than a simple visual “double.” This is intentional to preserve previously approved pricing logic. If Lori and Spencer want a true flat “double only,” the existing arch upcharge can be removed quickly. Arched End Panel Part Calculations What to highlight: Arched End Panels use the same geometry and part logic as arched doors. Supported arch types fully match doors: 14 24 28 54 Panel, stile, rail, and mullion geometry is consistent with door arch logic. CUSTOM END PANELS Custom End Panel Product What to highlight: Custom End Panels were implemented using standard end panel geometry. Square vs arched behavior is still respected. Uses the same underlying part calculations as standard end panels. Pricing What to highlight: Flat $100 base upcharge was added. Uses the existing attribute exactly as specified: Attribute ID 267 Upcharge is additive on top of normal end panel pricing. Attribute was placed intentionally so it can be reused for future custom upcharges if needed. PUNCH LOGIC (ALL DOOR PRODUCTS) Punch Quantity Requirement What to highlight: Validation added: If door height is 40 inches or greater, customer must specify punch quantity. Enforcement occurs through validation tied to the comment field. Punch Location Comment Box What to highlight: A dedicated Punch Location comment field was added to all door products. Allows free-text punch location descriptions. Validation fires when punches are selected (left, right, or pair). Field name was explicitly changed to “Punch Location” to avoid customer confusion. New Door Specs Cutsheet What to highlight: A new Door Specs cutsheet was created specifically for machining and sizing. Includes only: Finished door size Outside edge profile Punch quantity Punch location comments Explicitly excludes panel and stile/rail breakdowns. Currently enabled on slab doors for validation and can be turned on for all door products in minutes once approved. CAB MARK Remove Cab Mark Pricing What to highlight: All cab mark pricing logic has been completely removed. Cab mark now functions as an informational field only. No pricing calculations reference cab mark anywhere in the product logic. MITERED DOORS Mitered Stiles and Rails Adjustment What to highlight: For mitered door styles only: +1/16 inch is added to the reported cut length of each stile and rail. Adjustment applies only on cutsheets, not nominal door size. Applied once per piece, not compounded. Logic aligns exactly with Spencer’s explanation of shop behavior. KEY DECISIONS / CONFIRMATIONS TO ASK FOR Use these as explicit discussion checkpoints: Confirm whether arched end panel pricing should: Preserve existing arch upcharges, or Be a strict “double only” of square end panels. Confirm whether the new Door Specs cutsheet should be enabled across all door products. Confirm if any additional punch-related attributes should appear on the Door Specs cutsheet beyond quantity and location. This list should give you a precise, defensible walkthrough that shows progress, intentional restraint on scope, and clear decision points without reopening solved work. What we still need Chris and Lori Calculation breakdown for everything we need the formulas for each configuration so we can review and apply the logic if needed Meeting Summary Team reviewed ongoing cut list calculation issues within Allmoxy and Door Pro integration. Primary concern: inaccurate cut lists for both arch doors (with mid rails) and standard square doors. Significant frustration expressed regarding continued inaccuracies despite extended time and financial investment. Agreed that the core issue is lack of full visibility into Door Pro’s calculation logic and order of operations. Identified need for clearly documented, simplified formulas for all relevant door configurations, especially arches with mid rails. Additional inconsistencies identified in miter drawer front configurations and output fields. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Need a complete breakdown of calculation formulas, especially for: Tall arch doors with multiple mid rails (panel height calculation). Standard shaker doors where cut lists are still reportedly inaccurate. Current approach of fixing isolated issues is causing regressions in other configurations. Team needs a holistic rule set covering: Square vs. arched doors. Mid rails and mid styles interactions. Insets and how they affect panel height and width. Arch door math appears to result in only a 1/16 inch variance in panel height vs. square door, potentially due to top rail sizing and arch shaping. Confirmed miter adjustment: additional 1/16 inch was previously added to overall lengths. Identified UI and output mismatches: Arch options showing for miter drawer fronts when they should not. Door style field duplicated. Inside/outside edge fields appearing on cut list despite not being selectable during order entry. Reported cut length field appearing unexpectedly. Drawer fronts showing punch information when none should apply. Technical Issues and Fixes Miter drawer front configuration updated live to: Remove arch options. Remove duplicate door style field. Panel size for reviewed miter drawer front confirmed correct after clarification. Remaining technical gaps: Arch door with multiple mid rails panel height formula still undefined. Standard square door cut list inaccuracies still unresolved. Output fields (inside/outside edge, punch) not fully aligned with order input fields. Decisions Made Focus first on documenting and confirming correct formulas rather than continuing incremental adjustments. Compare square vs. arch door configurations in Door Pro to identify exact variance and isolate calculation logic. Create a comprehensive “all combinations” test order to validate calculations across all scenarios once formulas are finalized. Use matching comparison orders to validate output accuracy. Challenges or Concerns Continued inaccuracies in standard square door cut lists are a major concern. Investment concerns raised due to extended timeline and unresolved core functionality. Lack of transparency into Door Pro’s internal calculation sequence is creating repeated rework. Arch doors increasing in volume, making resolution more urgent. Action Items Lori to send Spencer a detailed list of required formulas, including the arch with multiple mid rails scenario. Spencer to meet with Chris on Monday to: Review required formulas. Sit down and document the exact calculation logic. Clarify arch panel height math and mid rail deductions. Carson to: Create a comprehensive test order covering all possible configurations. Share with team for validation once formulas are finalized. Lori to: Create matching comparison order(s) to validate outputs against Door Pro results. Carson to add drawer box calculation guidance to header once provided (opening-to-box math for undermount and side mount). Carson to review and remove unnecessary output fields such as reported cut length and ensure inside/outside edge and punch fields align correctly with order inputs. Next Steps Spencer and Chris to confirm and return documented formulas early next week. Team to validate square door cut list calculations once formulas are confirmed. Apply confirmed spacing and inset logic globally across all products. Re-test full configuration matrix using comprehensive test order. Reassess system stability and accuracy after formula validation phase.

CDU & Allmoxy February 6, 2026 12:00 PM MST 49 mins sarah@allmoxy.com carson@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com shorty1421@gmail.com spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com info@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com View Meeting Recording Cut List Calculation Accuracy and Formula Standardization The team agreed that defining a uniform set of calculation rules and formulas for door and panel dimensions is crucial to fix persistent cut list inaccuracies (02:15). Carson Ward emphasized the need for a comprehensive formula breakdown covering all door styles, mid rails, and panel interactions to eliminate guesswork and ensure consistent calculations. Lori and Spencer highlighted that while some formulas from Chris helped, others—especially for complex arch doors with multiple mid rails—remain unclear or inaccurate, preventing correct cut list outputs (07:40). The lack of visibility into Door Pro’s exact order of operations and calculation logic is a key barrier to resolving discrepancies between expected and actual cut list sizes (10:07). The team plans to have Spencer obtain detailed formulas from Chris by Monday, including drawn examples from Lori specifying required panel height calculations for arch doors to finalize the math (43:00). Despite ongoing issues, the team identified that the core math is simple addition and subtraction once the correct insets and rail sizes are known, making this a solvable problem with the right data (15:50). Arch Door Specific Challenges and Insights Arch doors with multiple mid rails present unique calculation challenges that differ from standard square doors, requiring special formulas to correctly size panels (07:52). The team found the height differences between square and arch door panels to be about 1/16 inch, primarily due to the arch’s shaping and rail insets, which slightly affect panel dimensions (36:00). Spencer explained that the arch’s top rail is physically larger to accommodate the curve, which offsets panel sizes slightly, but the overall math remains consistent across panels (40:30). This insight suggests that adjusting for a 1/16 inch variance might resolve the arch panel height issue if correctly incorporated into the formulas (42:10). The team will test these minor adjustments Monday to verify if this approach resolves the arch door cut list problems (43:15). Standard Door Cut List Issues and Frustrations Persistent inaccuracies in cut lists for standard doors, especially shaker styles, continue to frustrate the team despite efforts to resolve them (17:50). Spencer expressed strong dissatisfaction with ongoing errors in basic cut list outputs, noting that the system should produce accurate lengths for styles, rails, and panels without complexity (18:55). The team acknowledged that factors like inset sizes, edge profiles, and punch information sometimes appear incorrectly or inconsistently on cut lists versus order inputs, causing confusion and mistrust in data accuracy (22:00). Carson confirmed recent updates to reduce redundant or incorrect attribute displays on orders, such as hiding arch options where irrelevant, aiming to simplify inputs and outputs for standard doors (28:20). Despite UI improvements, the root issue remains the correct calculation logic, which the team will continue addressing alongside formula finalization (33:00). Process Improvements for Formula Validation and Testing To ensure accuracy, the team will create an all-encompassing test order covering every door and panel combination to verify formulas once finalized (45:50). Carson plans to build this comprehensive order with all variable permutations to test the application of final formulas and confirm output correctness. Lori will prepare a matching order to compare actual cut list results against expected values, enabling direct validation and quicker issue identification (46:55). This method aims to create a reliable litmus test that prevents future calculation errors and streamlines troubleshooting. The team expects this approach to provide clarity and confidence in cut list outputs after formula corrections are applied. Drawer Box Calculation and Documentation Update The team reported drawer boxes are currently calculating correctly, and a proposal was made to document size calculations for consistency (47:20). Spencer suggested adding clear, simple instructions for calculating drawer box sizes from opening dimensions, differentiating undermount and side mount options. Carson committed to adding this documentation once the team provides the exact formula and guidance needed. This documentation aims to reduce errors and questions for users inputting drawer box orders, improving operational efficiency. Timeline and Investment Considerations The ongoing issues with cut list accuracy put the value of the $20,000 investment into question, with a decision expected by end of May on continued engagement (19:20). Spencer communicated frustration over the prolonged unresolved problems and the financial investment, signaling pressure for rapid resolution. The team recognizes the urgency to finalize formulas and validate accuracy to justify the investment and determine next steps. This timeline underscores the business impact and priority of resolving these issues promptly. Action Items Spencer Coordinate with Chris on Monday to clarify and confirm all required formulas, especially for arched doors with mid rails. (04:51) Analyze and compare Door Pro cut list outputs for square versus arched doors with mid rails to identify formula discrepancies and adjustments. (34:05) Address ongoing issues with incorrect cut list generation for standard doors to prevent continuing operational frustrations. (16:52) Follow up on drawer box size calculation guide implementation for clearer instructions. (47:28) Carson Ward Create a comprehensive spreadsheet/database covering all door style and configuration combinations to enable formula application and cut list validation. (01:36) Adjust order entry interface to hide irrelevant attributes (e.g., arch option on miter drawer fronts) to reduce data entry errors. (24:30) Incorporate drawer box size calculation formulas into system documentation as directed by Spencer. (47:56) Lori Provide Spencer with a detailed list of specific formulas and calculations needed, including arch door panel height with multiple mid rails. (04:51) Draft a sketch or diagram showing measurement requirements for arch door panels and rails to assist formula development. (30:10) Create a comprehensive comparison order to validate formula outputs across door styles. (47:02) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KGQ90Z4EX2HX3XHSM1YZ9JQ9 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

CDU & Allmoxy February 2, 2026 12:00 PM MST 78 mins spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com shorty1421@gmail.com carson@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com info@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com sarah@allmoxy.com View Meeting Recording Calculation Accuracy and Panel Dimension Adjustments The team prioritized fixing the calculation errors affecting panel widths and heights to ensure the product outputs can go to production reliably (00:02). Sarah Pierce and Carson Ward identified that panel widths were consistently off by about 3/8 inch, and heights were miscalculated mainly due to incorrect handling of insets and mid rails (00:32). The panel inset values were initially applied only once instead of being multiplied by the number of panels, leading to under-calculation. They agreed to double the panel inset from 3/8 inch to 6/8 inch to better match Door Pro’s measurements (00:37). Testing with a standard single-panel door showed this adjustment improved accuracy for width calculations (00:38). Complex cases with multiple mid rails and mid styles required further refinement on how insets and rail widths were factored into height calculations (01:00). Lori provided example orders, including one with 82 doors on the Spring River job, to test multi-panel and arch door scenarios (00:38). The height was off by roughly 7/16 inch per panel, requiring subtraction of panel rail inset values multiplied by the number of panels. They explored formulas combining door height minus bottom, top, and mid rail widths plus panel rail insets times two or multiplied by the panel count (01:08). The team debated whether to multiply insets by the number of panels or panels plus one to reflect actual panel divisions accurately (01:09). The arch door panel widths were found to be consistently calculated as door width minus 4 inches, which was a coincidence and not dynamically linked to actual rail widths, risking errors if defaults changed (00:50). They decided to revert height calculations temporarily to subtract a fixed 4 inches, acknowledging it is not a scalable solution but works as a short-term fix (01:15). Sarah and Carson confirmed that mid rail widths are fixed at 2.375 inches, and customers currently cannot change those values, simplifying some aspects of the calculation (01:03). They noted that the panel style inset affects the top and bottom rails only and does not contribute to mid rail or mid style width calculations, clarifying the separation of formula components (00:57). The team recognized that the panel rail inset needs to be subtracted twice for each panel’s height, but the complexity of multiple panels and mid rails requires a more nuanced formula (01:07). The current formulas for width and height require further iteration with assistance from Chris, who may help refine the calculations using his expertise, targeted for the next few days (01:16). Quantity Calculation and Cut List Accuracy Resolving quantity inconsistencies in panels, doors, and rails was critical to generating correct cut lists and avoiding production errors (00:09). The team discovered that the quantity for door specs was exponentially multiplying due to a checkbox setting that caused quantities to multiply by product quantity twice, inflating the count to four instead of two (00:18). Sarah Pierce identified that unchecking the "measures quantity" box fixed this, aligning panel and door quantities correctly (00:20). Adjusting the formula to base quantities on the number of mid rails and mid styles rather than panels wide resolved many issues (00:25). They confirmed that cut list pages needed to maintain some repetition of headers and details for clarity, despite earlier suggestions to remove repetition for conciseness (00:17). Panels and styles & rails quantities were fixed to accurately reflect the physical components, with mid rails quantity now properly set to one when selected, not doubling erroneously (00:26). The team agreed that quantities should be calculated as the product of the number of products and the number of divisions (mid styles), ensuring correct panel counts for multi-panel doors (00:11). Lori pointed out that some cut list pages were confusing for production teams due to overlapping or unclear quantities, emphasizing the need for better labeling and organization going forward (00:14). The quantity fixes were tested on multiple orders, confirming that single-panel doors showed correct quantities of one door and two panels, and multi-panel doors showed accurate multiplications (00:21). Formula Refinement and Attribute Settings Fixing the formula logic and attribute references was key for accurate dimension and quantity outputs (00:22). The team traced quantity and measurement issues back to formulas referencing panels wide vs. divisions, and inconsistent use of attributes like panel style inset and panel rail inset (00:24). They clarified that mid rail and mid style insets are separate and must be referenced distinctly in calculations to avoid miscalculations (00:56). Attributes for panel rail inset were confirmed as 0.4375 inches (7/16), matching Door Pro’s standards and used in height calculations (00:53). The team identified that the inset applied to height calculations should be multiplied appropriately for the number of panels tall, though the exact multiplier (panels tall vs. panels tall plus one) remains under discussion (01:09). They agreed to align formulas so that the total door height subtracts the sum of bottom rail, top rail, mid rails, and panel rail insets times the correct multipliers to reflect real-world dimensions (01:07). Sarah and Carson committed to testing changes incrementally to ensure that adjustments to one formula area do not inadvertently disrupt others (00:58). The final formula adjustments will be cross-checked with actual Door Pro entry data and tested on real orders, including complex arch and multi-panel doors (01:13). Process and Collaboration Next Steps The team decided on a focused collaboration and testing timeline to finalize fixes and prepare the product for production use (01:16). Carson will share updates from Ray on outstanding formula items delivered last week and coordinate to continue deep dives on calculations (01:16). Lori will consult with Chris to apply his expertise to the formulas, aiming to resolve outstanding calculation nuances and send back improved logic within a few days (01:16). They plan a follow-up meeting on Wednesday to review progress, validate fixes, and plan next steps for pricing adjustments once calculations are stable (01:16). The team agreed to maintain open communication during the interim to address any emergent issues and ensure quality output in production cut lists (01:16). Sarah committed to continue working with Lori on Door Pro data and entry sheets to verify assumptions and accuracy of measurements (01:16). The group acknowledged the complexity of arch doors and multi-panel scenarios and prioritized fixing the formulas for these cases to prevent production errors (01:00). Action Items Sarah Pierce Continue working on panel and door dimension formula fixes and test with Door Pro examples. (37:00) Save edits to panel inset and height formulas and inform team when changes complete. (37:30) Coordinate with Lori and Chris to get additional formula support. (01:16:10) Prepare for next meeting Wednesday to continue issue resolution. (01:16:40) Carson Ward Open and review job #33 and #34 cut lists for panels and doors; validate quantity and dimension discrepancies. (03:30) Perform formula toggling tests to fix exponential quantity multiplication issue and confirm panel and style count corrections. (18:30) Adjust test orders to verify mid rail and mid style quantity calculations. (24:00) Implement temporary reversion of arch door height formula if needed. (01:15:20) Share updates from Ray on outstanding issues for formula resolution. (01:16:25) Lori Provide examples of doors with mid rails and mid styles from Spring River job (#34) for testing height and width formulas. (40:00) Take picture of Door Pro entries for arch doors to support formula comparison. (01:14:00) Contact Chris for technical assistance and “magic” on formulas and share results with Sarah and Carson. (01:16:10) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KGAR0PFA9V9N7JMSC3VK49H0 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

CDU & Allmoxy | Product Notes January 30, 2026 12:30 PM MST 90 mins spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com sarah@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com carson@allmoxy.com shorty1421@gmail.com info@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com View Meeting Recording End Panel Pricing and Cut List Configuration The team confirmed that end panel pricing is accurately based on lumber cost plus a fixed markup depending on panel style, with a special doubling of price for arch panels (08:56). Lori clarified that the base price uses lumber cost plus $4 for flat panels and $6 for raised panels, with no labor charge (05:50). Carson confirmed the system calculates price by lumber type and panel style, applying the doubling factor for arch panels as a multiplier on the total (08:56). Action assigned: parts for the N panel need to be created to generate a proper cut list, with a preference to keep end panels separate from door/drawer sheets, as confirmed by William and Lori for shop usability (12:16). The team agreed end panels should be selectable by shape (square or arch) during ordering, maintaining clear differentiation without losing pricing accuracy (07:44). Custom End Panels and Pricing Validation Custom end panels will carry an average flat-rate upcharge to cover additional labor, with a system flag requiring verification for special placement requests (24:30). William proposed an average labor charge of $100 per custom panel, covering typically an hour of work by two staff, which Lori agreed is fair (25:37). The system will duplicate the current end panel pricing structure and add a custom flag to trigger the flat-rate surcharge and require customer confirmation via comments (26:17). This approach avoids upfront customer-facing detailed breakdowns but includes a disclaimer that custom panels may be costlier due to extra labor (27:38). Carson committed to adding this custom option to the pricing formula and validation workflow for Ray to implement. Cut Sheet Enhancements and Highlighting Important Notes The group discussed adding a “ghost” watermark or highlight feature on cut sheets to emphasize critical notes, reducing overlooked special instructions (12:34). William emphasized the need for a visible flag such as a light gray overlay or bold red text on cut sheets to draw attention to special instructions, improving shop floor accuracy (13:07). Sarah suggested implementing attribute-based triggers that change font color or highlight text when specific order flags or comments exist, pending further customer input (17:21). The team agreed to gather a list of attributes and scenarios from Lori to scope developer tickets for this feature (17:56). This feature aims to reduce errors from missed instructions without cluttering the cut list, aligning with the “keep it simple” philosophy (19:10). Cabinet Mark Removal from Pricing and Order Outputs The team decided to remove the cabinet mark charge entirely from pricing but retain the cab mark text field for order accuracy and downstream system integration (32:47). Lori requested removing the 25-cent cab mark surcharge from bids and orders as it is no longer charged (32:50). Carson confirmed the removal of the cab mark charge and that cab mark text will still be recorded and displayed on bids for order review but hidden on cut sheets to avoid clutter (34:44). William supported showing cab marks only on bids for reference but not on shop floor documents to keep instructions minimal (34:51). This change simplifies pricing and maintains necessary labeling information for production and accuracy. Punch Hole Specifications and Output Process The team agreed to add a custom punch comment box per line item triggered when “custom punch” is selected, with a dedicated simplified spec sheet for punch locations to aid manufacturing (39:47). Lori emphasized the need for punch quantity input and standardized location descriptions (e.g., “top to center”) with a default “standard” option (37:40). Carson explained that dynamic attribute visibility isn’t currently supported, so the punch comment box will be present for all line items but only used when “custom” is chosen (40:29). William and Lori suggested referencing line item numbers in punch comments for clarity, with Spencer agreeing this is easiest to start (41:48). A new lightweight punch spec sheet focusing on door size, outside edge, and punch info will be created to streamline shop floor focus (44:25). No punch location validation will be tied to door size thresholds; all punches require location details if custom (47:15). This approach balances detailed input with workflow simplicity and reduces errors in punch placement. Miter Door Rail Length Adjustment A standard 1/16 inch length addition will be applied to miter door rails to avoid manual corrections in production (50:36). William explained that previously staff manually added a 1/16 inch to rails (22 and 24 inch sizes) without documentation, causing inconsistencies (50:46). Carson confirmed this length adjustment will be automated in the pricing and cut list system, reducing manual errors (51:44). Lori clarified the 1/16 inch is added to each part length, effectively a 1/16 inch total per rail, not four times (52:22). Sarah supported the change noting it aligns with typical manufacturing tolerances (52:41). Pricing, Inventory, and Cut List Discrepancies The team identified ongoing discrepancies between Door Pro and El Moxie pricing and cut list outputs, particularly panel sizes and quantity sorting, requiring formula review (54:24). Lori noted inconsistencies in panel width ordering (descending order expected) and inaccurate quantities on cut lists compared to actual orders (58:03). Carson stressed the need to focus on reviewing and aligning the core pricing formulas rather than output appearance, as small formula differences cause widespread errors (01:26:20). Lori agreed to provide photos and examples of the formulas used in Door Pro to help Ray and development diagnose issues (01:27:09). The team agreed to schedule a follow-up meeting Monday at 1:00 PM Mountain Time to review outstanding issues and continue cleanup work (01:14:36). Sarah and Carson confirmed discontinued product versions will be cleaned out to reduce confusion, improving system stability (01:15:23). Process Improvements and User Experience Order-level settings allow batch attribute application to multiple products, speeding order entry and reducing errors (01:01:24). Sarah explained that users can set attributes like wood species at the order level and apply them to all products, streamlining workflows (01:01:38). Lori and William appreciated this feature for managing different wood types and pricing tiers efficiently (01:03:44). The team committed to fixing UI spacing and image positioning on attribute selectors for better clarity (01:04:44). Continuous hands-on order testing by Lori is improving familiarity and surfacing needed tweaks faster, accelerating system readiness (49:27). Tax Handling Simplification The team decided to set customer tax rates to zero to effectively remove tax from orders, simplifying billing (55:09). Carson explained tax cannot be removed from the interface but can be set to zero per customer account to avoid calculation (55:20). William confirmed this approach aligns with current business practice of not charging tax (55:47). The bid and order printouts will hide tax lines for cleaner documents, pending minor UI fixes (56:16). This change reduces confusion and manual tax overrides in orders. Action Items Carson Ward Compile and send detailed action list to Ray to start software updates for panel parts creation, pricing multipliers, and cut sheet organization. (25:18) Remove cab mark charge from pricing and hide it on quotes, keeping cab mark displayed only on bid documents. (33:04) Add punch comment box attribute and develop new punch/specs output sheet focused on door specs, outside edge, and punch details. (43:12) Adjust slab drawer front product settings to hide punch options and clean up discontinued products. (01:02:08) Fix tax display to zero for customers not charged tax and explore removing it from bid output page. (55:09) Adjust image position on product attributes dropdowns for better UI. (01:05:25) Prepare and distribute meeting invite for detailed Monday review on pricing and cut sheet adjustments. (01:14:36) Collaborate with Lori to obtain detailed product formulas for precise pricing validation. (01:26:26) Lori Prepare a list of highlighted attributes/notes needed for software’s highlighting functionality for developer implementation. (17:56) Review and clean up old discontinued product records and inconsistent cut list naming conventions prior to Monday meeting. (01:15:50) Provide pictures or details of current formulas used to calculate panel sizes and prices to assist software team in alignment. (01:27:09) William Turner Confirm labor rate for custom end panels and agree on automated flat $100 upcharge as average labor cost. (25:18) Suggest flagging orders with special instructions to increase floor staff attention using ghosting or highlights. (12:34) Support proposals to separate punch information onto dedicated sheets for manufacturing clarity. (43:12) Share Door Pro cut sheets to provide reference for discrepancies. (01:26:20) Sarah Pierce Implement attribute visibility and hiding fixes for slab drawer fronts and mitigate punch option errors. (59:52) Assist with fixing tax display and attribute UI positioning. (56:37) Monitor and coordinate developmental tickets related to highlighting and attribute presentation per requested features. (18:02) Support cleanup and data validation to achieve output accuracy. (01:15:33) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KG857KMQYARA23ZS7QKAPHDY 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

CDU & Allmoxy CDU & Allmoxy January 13, 2026 11:00 AM MST 63 mins sarah@allmoxy.com spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com carson@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com shorty1421@gmail.com info@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com View Meeting Recording Product Configuration and Order Entry Improvements The team decided to streamline product attribute selections to simplify order entry and reduce confusion (00:30). Finger pull attribute will be moved from line item to group-level settings, allowing a single yes/no choice for the entire order, as explained by Lori and Carson (13:58). This change reflects that finger pulls typically apply to the whole order, but individual doors can still override it with comments. Moving cab mark to remain at the line item level was confirmed to accommodate unique labeling per door. Carson will implement these attribute relocations across all products after the meeting (15:44). This adjustment aims to reduce errors and speed up order entry by consolidating common selections. The punch for hinges and hinge type attributes will have default "none" options and dynamic visibility once the feature is fully developed, as Carson detailed (00:30). Currently, attributes don't dynamically show or hide based on punch selections, but this is a feature in progress. Defaulting these attributes to "none" avoids forcing unnecessary choices and prevents pricing confusion. Validation logic will be added later to require hinge type selection if punch options are chosen. Order level settings will be established for most common attributes like wood type, style, panel profile, and material to enable cascading defaults for entire orders (10:24). Lori confirmed customers often order the same material and style across all doors, making order-level presets valuable. These presets will allow quick changes to the entire order, supporting bidding flexibility and reducing repetitive data entry. Individual line items can still override order-level defaults, preserving customization. End Panels, Molding, and Lumber Pricing Strategy A clear pricing scheme and product structuring approach for end panels, molding, and lumber was established to simplify configuration and quoting (30:49). End panels will be offered with two product options: a standard configurable panel with mid rails and styles, and a custom panel where customers specify detailed configurations in comments, as Carson and Lori agreed (27:22). Lumber upcharges were confirmed: $4 per sq ft for flat panels and $6 per sq ft for raised or solid lumber panels (20:58). Pricing excludes labor and focuses only on material costs, per Lori. Most end panels use square outside edges and standard dimensions; custom requests are rare but supported via comments. This approach balances ease of ordering with flexibility for custom design needs. Molding and lumber products will be priced based on lumber cost plus markup, waste factor, and labor (30:31). Two types of crown molding identified with widths of 4 3/4 inches and 3 1/4 inches (31:27). William will provide pictures and exact specs to assist product setup. Pricing will link to the wood type attribute for consistency with door pricing. Inventory and Purchasing System Setup The group outlined inventory tracking and purchase order automation plans to improve material management and reduce stockouts (38:42). Almoxy will track inventory items linked to product attributes such as panel materials, hinges, and consumables like glue and space balls (45:24). The system will generate a need-to-purchase list based on verified orders, alerting when stock hits minimum thresholds (44:06). Purchase orders can be sent directly to vendors via email or text, streamlining reordering (41:52). Lori will provide vendor information and pricing for setup. William aims to remove ordering responsibility from Chris to allow him to focus on shop operations (44:38). Inventory locations will be grouped by type rather than physical location to avoid confusion, as advised by William and Carson (40:01). Categories like edge banding or sheet goods will organize stock logically. Consumables can be tracked by quantity used per door, enabling granular consumption tracking (46:09). Glue reordering will be managed via calendar alerts linked to purchase orders. Cut List and Labeling Output Enhancements The team agreed to split detailed cut lists into logical sections to improve shop usability and data clarity (51:31). Cut lists will separate panel dimensions, styles and rails, and finished sizes onto different pages as requested by William to reduce clutter and repetition (56:45). Panels and drawer fronts will have dedicated cut sheets tailored to manufacturing needs. Headers on cut lists will be adjusted to avoid repetitive display after Sarah located the setting to reduce them (01:01:29). Carson and Sarah will review William’s cut list examples offline to better align output formatting with current shop workflows (55:35). This restructuring supports shop efficiency by providing focused information to relevant teams without overload. Labeling export formats will be revisited to match existing Accu templates used for panel cutting and routing (34:12). William plans to share his Accu labeling spreadsheet to ensure Almoxy exports align with CNC programming needs. Sarah and Carson noted that the system can support multiple export templates for different customers or vendors. This effort aims to automate CNC input and reduce manual data entry errors on the shop floor. Process Tracking and Time Management Prospects The group discussed potential future use of Almoxy’s time tracking features to improve job costing and workflow visibility (47:47). Currently, timecards appear on login as a home page feature, but no detailed time tracking is in use (47:52). Carson explained that process tracking can be gradually added to log time spent on specific orders or steps without overwhelming staff. William sees value in eventually monitoring order progress and labor time to better understand costs and efficiencies (49:33). The system will also track material consumption per job, enabling net cost visibility even before labor tracking is fully implemented. This phased approach balances adoption ease with data granularity for improved operational insight. Next Steps and Follow-Up Coordination The team agreed to consolidate meeting notes and schedule a follow-up to finalize configurations and product setups (01:00:49). Carson will send a recap email with assignments and propose times for a Thursday meeting to review progress and next steps (01:00:49). William and Lori will provide additional documentation including cut lists and Accu system spreadsheets to support setup (01:00:39). Sarah and Carson will continue offline work to optimize cut list formatting and address header repetition issues (01:01:37). The goal is to keep the project moving efficiently with clear ownership and communication to meet production and quoting needs. Action Items Carson Ward Add 'none' as default option for punch for hinges and hinge type, ensuring attribute fields hide when none is selected. (05:00) Move finger pull attribute to group/order level on all products and confirm associated notes setup. (15:00) Make all group attributes order-level settings for easier bulk selection and customization. (17:00) Set up two end panel product options: standard configurable and custom with comment input. (29:30) Configure molding and lumber pricing based on lumber cost and markup after receiving specs from Spencer. (32:00) Prepare export template for panel size CSV files compatible with Accu systems and collaborate on label printing integration. (36:00) Initiate inventory setup with grouping by product type and vendor linkage, enable purchase order functionality. (40:30) Implement timecard and basic job process tracking for production management. (47:30) Review and revise cut list output to split product details across pages and remove repetitive headers. (55:30) Schedule follow-up meeting and send recap and assignments to team. (60:00) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KEQ82E9DETDQ2EDSRCBD2PK6 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

CDU & Allmoxy CDU & Allmoxy January 8, 2026 12:00 PM MST 92 mins sarah@allmoxy.com carson@allmoxy.com sturner98@gmail.com shorty1421@gmail.com spencer@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com info@cabinetdoorsunlimited.com View Meeting Recording Catalog and Product Configuration The team is finalizing the door panel sizing logic and resolving mid rail length calculations to ensure accuracy and reduce waste (15:00). Carson Ward confirmed that panel sizes for square doors now match specifications with 8 11/16 inches and 13 3/16 inches for two product variants, improving precision in cut list outputs. The only unresolved issue is the mid rail splitting logic, where the mid rails must be split correctly around mid styles; Lori explained exceptions exist mainly for end panels, which require further specification. Lori will consult with Chris from the shop floor to clarify how mid rails should be calculated and manipulated in real-world production, aiming for a clear formula and process by end of day (35:30). For arches, additional logic was successfully implemented to add 15/16 inch twice for bigger arches and multiply by four for double deeper arches, ensuring panel height accuracy. Overrides for highly custom parts exist in Almoxy, allowing manual adjustment of cut list lengths at order level to accommodate unique designs, which Lori found useful for customization scenarios (33:00). The end panel product configuration remains incomplete but is expected to mirror door logic with adjusted pricing and customization once mid rail rules are finalized (22:30, 58:00). Lori is preparing to send detailed drawings and pricing notes to Carson to aid finalizing this product line. Pricing differences include a $4 and $6 markup over lumber costs for flat panel and solid lumber end panels, respectively. The team acknowledged that end panels can be intricate, especially with Amish customer designs, and will revisit once core door calculations are stable. Crown molding and lumber products will be set up as simplified catalog items with straightforward sizing and pricing models to reflect material costs without inventory tracking initially (15:00, 58:00). William Turner noted lumber sales to customers are significant and pricing is competitive, often offering savings of $3 per foot compared to market rates while making a modest margin. Lumber will be added as a supply item for sales tracking but without pushing customer self-ordering due to low volume and complexity. Crown molding will be managed as a square footage and profile product without inventory management since it is made to order. Pricing Strategy and Cost Control The team is refining product pricing structures and cost tracking to improve margin visibility and order profitability (39:00). William Turner is working on drawer box pricing, with current markup around 60%, but is still optimizing to ensure profitability while remaining competitive. Almoxy supports cost tracking by calculating side and bottom material costs multiplied by markup divisors, allowing detailed order-level profit analysis when inventory and labor tracking are enabled. Pricing attributes are centralized in Almoxy’s product pricing formulas, with only the style upcharge currently in use for doors; other price variables exist but are unused, allowing future flexibility (1:09:00). A uniform $2 upcharge across all products was applied by William to cover incremental costs, simplifying price adjustments. The team agreed to create videos and documentation outlining how to update pricing attributes to empower internal control without external dependency. The team discussed credit and order limits to manage customer accounts effectively, particularly for high-volume buyers like Morning Millworks (42:00). Morning Millworks places about $5,000 worth of drawer box orders weekly, requiring a credit limit of approximately $30,000 and a 30-day payment grace period. Carson Ward demonstrated setting up customer accounts in Almoxy with order limits and no sales tax, allowing tailored pricing and order histories per customer without requiring customer portal access initially. Automated workflows are planned to send bids, quotes, order confirmations, and invoices based on order statuses, reducing manual communication and errors. Operational Workflow and Process Improvements Efforts focus on streamlining order processing, improving communication, and enhancing reporting to reduce errors and increase efficiency (11:00). William Turner emphasized the need to get the system fully operational within four months to evaluate its value, citing recent costly mistakes due to lack of inventory tracking and order management. Almoxy is expected to resolve these issues by providing consistent data, operational functionality, and better reporting, as Sarah Pierce confidently assured (12:00). Lori will start sending quotes labeled as “bid” or “estimate” instead of “invoice” to avoid confusion, with system modifications planned to match order statuses automatically (54:00). The team plans frequent meetings, up to three times a week, to improve understanding and adoption of Almoxy’s features for order entry and catalog management (1:02:50). The system supports manual overrides and custom parts to handle real-world production variations like expansion and contraction, which cannot be fully automated (36:00). Inventory linkage and resource allocation are planned to support product-level tracking and cost control (1:05:00). Lumber and crown molding sales will be linked to inventory or treated as supplies, supporting demand forecasting and order fulfillment planning. Since crown molding is made to order, no inventory tracking is required, but the system will track material sizing and pricing for accurate costing. The team agreed to prioritize linking inventory to main products after resolving end panel and mid rail issues. Technology and System Enablement Almoxy’s knowledge base and AI chatbot are key tools to support user training and problem resolution (50:00). Sarah Pierce highlighted the AI chat feature trained exclusively on Almoxy documentation to provide instant answers during off-hours, improving support accessibility. Lori expressed discomfort with AI technology due to personal reasons but will rely on the knowledge base articles and direct support instead. The team confirmed multiple communication channels, including email and integrated chat/ticketing systems, to raise questions and report issues promptly (1:29:00). Carson Ward will send meeting recordings and follow-ups to ensure all participants have access to training materials and can revisit technical discussions. System configuration includes customization of order documents and notifications to better fit customer communication needs (56:00). The system can generate cut lists and quotes with or without pricing details, tailored per customer preferences. Automated emails will be triggered by order status changes to streamline bid, quote, and invoice delivery. Customer access can be toggled, allowing internal order entry with customers receiving only necessary information. The team plans to customize output templates to include relevant product details like style and rail widths for customer review. Timeline and Next Steps The team is targeting a near-term launch phase with a goal to have the system live within two weeks to allow a four-month evaluation period before contract renewal discussions (14:00). Sarah Pierce and William Turner agreed to prioritize launch, with Sarah committing to support and training sessions to accelerate user adoption. Lori will coordinate with Chris to finalize mid rail calculations urgently and send detailed documentation to Carson for implementation (35:00, 1:28:30). Carson Ward and Ray will prepare instructional videos on pricing updates and system usage to empower internal management post-launch. The team plans weekly or more frequent meetings to maintain momentum and address issues rapidly, including involving additional team members as needed. Inventory setup for lumber, crown molding, and other products is planned after core door functionality stabilizes, ensuring smooth integration of tracking and ordering workflows. Action Items Sarah Pierce Support scheduling frequent training sessions for CDU team and provide customization options for bid/order documents. (01:02:58) Adjust default lead time to 14 calendar days in system and shipping method settings for CDU. (48:15) Assist in setting up customer company/vendor pricing and order notifications. (39:40) Provide knowledge base and chatbot support for user questions and system help. (50:00) General Schedule follow-up meetings early next week for continued system implementation and training. (01:04:15) Share meeting recording and recap of next steps with all participants. (01:28:40) Lori Consult with Chris to finalize mid rail splitting calculations and send updated drawings/notes to Carson. (35:29) Continue reviewing and communicating end panel pricing and quote structures for system setup. (01:20:00) Carson Ward Coordinate with Ray and Lori to finalize mid rail splitting logic and update formulas after receiving input from Chris. (15:30) Provide video tutorial and detailed instructions for updating costs and pricing formulas in Almoxy. (01:06:30) Assist with setting up customer companies/accounts, including Morgan Millworks, and adjusting credit/order limits. (42:00) Modify email templates to properly reflect bid, quote, and invoice statuses and automate communications. (54:00) William Turner Review drawer box pricing and finalize pricing strategy ensuring adequate margins. (01:02:35) Coordinate with Lori for system training and continuous improvement meetings. (01:04:00) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KEADM4GC20BYJ5JQW68ZRDXQ 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

engagements.body: Carson Ward just had a great conversation with Spencer about images. FYI

New Customer Kickoff Call Kickoff Call Meeting Notes: Notes: Meeting Summary This was a kickoff implementation meeting between the Allmoxy team (Carson Ward, Ray Cantu, Bobby Lewis) and the Cabinet Doors Unlimited (CDU) team (Spencer, Lori Lindemeier, Chris) to align on system setup, user onboarding, catalog structure, inventory methodology, and implementation process. The teams discussed CDU's current inventory limitations, how Allmoxy will support catalog configuration, and how both teams will collaborate in a hybrid build approach to ensure CDU has full control over their product setup and system management. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Roles Introduced: Carson: Customer Success & operational platform support. Ray: Product configuration engineer. Bobby: Facilitator of the onboarding and hybrid build structure. Lori: CDU Office Manager and main operational knowledge holder. Spencer: Implementation lead from CDU side. Chris: Shop Foreman. System Access & Setup: Lori and Spencer received login access; Carson guided user setup steps. Carson explained contact management in Allmoxy and demonstrated adding internal users. Inventory Handling: CDU currently lacks a standardized inventory format. Inventory will be built as part of the system rollout, beginning with product structure, then tying inventory after configuration stabilizes. A flexible format for data entry was discussed — starting with iPad data collection on the shop floor. Image & Product Visuals: Spencer requested wireframe-style, digital profile graphics instead of photos. Ray confirmed Allmoxy can associate blueprint-style imagery to products. Tooling drawings will be used for accuracy and reusability. Product Catalog Configuration: Categories needed: drawer boxes, hardware (glides, hinges), MDF/slab doors, crown moldings, face frames, etc. CDU confirmed they do not produce MDF doors. Coping/sticking, miters, three-piece, applied molding, and split-panel doors discussed as product styles. Bobby outlined how products, categories, and product attributes (e.g., wood type, styles, profiles) interact structurally. User Training & Ownership: Allmoxy will guide CDU through system build (hybrid build). CDU team will manage product catalog long-term after initial setup. Focus is on enabling CDU to adjust cut lists, pricing, and formulas independently. Technical Issues & Fixes Lori had issues accessing the login initially — resolved by clarifying correct URL and re-sending the email. Carson provided guidance for logging into the Allmoxy instance and locating product attributes in the UI. Scanner integration for timecards was discussed; Spencer confirmed hardware was partially ready. Decisions Made Hybrid build structure confirmed as the preferred onboarding strategy. Catalog build to begin with standard cope and stick doors. Use existing Allmoxy catalog templates to streamline setup and avoid building formulas from scratch. Lori and Spencer to jointly manage data entry and configuration. Challenges or Concerns CDU lacks a current inventory format and expects the system to evolve during setup. Transitioning knowledge from Lori into the Allmoxy system will be critical. Need for quick access to images and drawings, which may not be available for all products. Concern about timely responses from previous vendor (Door Pro), which Allmoxy aims to improve upon. Action Items Carson to send: Summary video tutorial on where to access and update product attributes. Full meeting recording. Lori and Spencer to: Begin reviewing and modifying product attributes (wood types, profiles, etc.). Compare Allmoxy’s existing catalog templates to CDU’s current offerings. Allmoxy team to: Add categories for drawer boxes, hardware, and molding. Create a “shopping list” of required information for CDU to gather. Spencer to share any tooling blueprints for product visual setup. Next Steps Schedule recurring implementation meetings: Next meeting: Thursday at 9:00 AM CDT (10:00 AM MDT). Carson to walk through setup in more detail during the next session. Lori and Spencer to review and populate product configuration fields ahead of Ray’s involvement. Title: Kick off Call - Cabinet Doors Unlimited Date: Thu Jun 05 2025 11:00:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: ray@allmoxy.com, carson@allmoxy.com, shorty1421@gmail.com, sturner98@gmail.com, bobby@allmoxy.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 59 mins Overview The Product Implementation Kickoff Meeting for Cabinet Doors Unlimited (CDU) commenced with team introductions, clarifying roles and the transition from Door Pro to Almoxy due to inadequate support. The discussion covered the necessary product catalog, including various door types, drawer fronts, and hardware, with a preference for digital wireframe images over photographs. A system structure overview illustrated how to modify product attributes and utilize built-in formula logic, followed by a user setup session where key personnel were granted necessary permissions. The implementation plan highlighted the initial steps for updating wood types and profiles, establishing a project workflow, and addressing time tracking features, with an emphasis on entering catalog items ahead of the next meeting. Action items were assigned to team members to facilitate ongoing progress, and the next meeting was scheduled to ensure continued momentum towards having the system operational by the anticipated equipment launch. Notes 🚀 Introductions and Team Roles Bobby Lewis introduced the team: Carson Ward (Customer Success Rep), Ray Cantu (Engineer for formulas) Spencer (appears as CDU Admin) is the business owner, Lori is the office manager, Chris is the shop foreman CDU is switching from Door Pro to Almoxy due to poor support response times Bobby explained implementation will be a 'hybrid build' where they work together to ensure CDU has full control over their catalog 🔍 Product Catalog Discussion Reviewed product types to be included: doors, drawer fronts, drawer boxes, hardware (hinges/glides) Confirmed door types: cope and stick, miters, applied molding, slab doors, hardwood slabs Spencer clarified they want digital wireframe/profile imagery rather than photographs Bobby explained that tooling drawings can be converted to wireframes for consistency Spencer noted that for new tooling, they'll have drawings available to provide ⚙️ System Structure Overview Bobby demonstrated the product structure in Almoxy: categories > products > product attributes Showed how to modify attributes like wood types, pricing, parts formulas, and validations Explained that most formula logic is already built into templates Discussed validation features that can prevent ordering errors (minimum widths, etc.) Lori noted these validations would be very helpful for their processes 👥 User Setup and Permissions Carson added Lori as a user with full admin permissions Spencer confirmed he already had admin access to the system Discussed potential for adding Chris to the system later Team learned how to add new employees and customers to the system Spencer asked about QuickBooks integration (Carson noted it's still in beta testing) 🛠️ Implementation Plan Spencer and Lori will begin updating wood types and profiles in the system Bobby advised starting with core attributes (wood types, door styles) before moving to validations Team discussed workflow: start with products, then cut listings, then inventory Spencer asked about time tracking - Bobby showed how to set up time card scanning Agreed inventory will be added later as it constantly changes during setup 📅 Next Steps and Timeline Scheduled next meeting for Thursday at 9:00 AM MST Carson will send a video showing how to navigate to and modify product attributes Next meeting will review data entry progress and establish regular meeting cadence Spencer noted that CDU expects to have most catalog items entered before next meeting Ultimate goal is to have system ready by the time new equipment is operational Action Items Spencer Turner Work with Lori to begin entering wood types and profiles into the system Help Lori understand how to use Zoom on her desktop for future meetings Get a camera for Lori's computer During the Business Development meeting for Allmoxy and Cabinet Doors Unlimited, key insights were shared regarding the cabinet shop's operational efficiency, as William Turner and Bobby Lewis highlighted their growth journey and the need for streamlined processes. Sarah Pierce introduced the Allmoxy system, emphasizing its benefits for order entry and customer management, while discussions ensued on order processing, pricing strategies, inventory management, and enhancing customer experience through notifications and integration with QuickBooks. Concerns about adapting to the new system were acknowledged, particularly regarding implementation and training needs. The meeting concluded with action items to schedule follow-ups for catalog development and share demo resources, ensuring a collaborative approach moving forward.
