engagements.title: New Sign Up - Three Creeks Custom Cabinets
engagements.body: Signed up for an account in the installer, check to see if a card is on file.

Kickoff Call Confirm the key stakeholders: - (Know who signed the contract before the call from sales/Executive Buyer) and confirm how they will be involved if at all with onboarding. -Who will be the key point of contact for the build? -Sales POC? -Production POC? -Financial/Accounting POC? -Any other stakeholders? Notes: Owner: Michael Ginzel Just him right now, Main POC Why did you buy the product? What were they hoping to achieve when they signed the contract and how does Allmoxy fit into that? If their answer is too broad, ask them to expand on how they envision their business changing when they purchased Allmoxy. Automation Capabilities Notes: Streamlining dealing with contractors, easier to produce changed orders (making it easier to mitigate). Launching a flat panel line and then other products later. When does the customer want to "launch" and what would a successful onboarding look like? How do you know when you're there? Ex. Door Product Line is Launched and Cabinet Line is started. Notes: ASAP (4-6 weeks) wants to get his flat panel line live. Can you think of any blockers that might get in the way? Notes: Mozaik exporting

Kickoff call 6/11/24 with Michael Ginzel Introductions Purpose of this kickoff call is info gathering so we can hit the ground running when you meet with our services team for the official scoping call. Identify key stakeholders and who is going to be involved with the day to day Just Michael Discuss goals and outcomes (automation capabilities & streamlining dealing with contractors, change orders slowing production) Want to get a standard cabinet line down and focus on flatpanel cabinetry since it is more consistent. Whenever they have a change order come up it is easier for the to produce those. Wants to start with flat panel processing and sending out consistent projects with the same components. Identify a launch date and what does that look like? Door product line, cabinet line, etc. Launching in iterations is key. Template already built, we can start off with having the simple products like the flat panel products and get that on a landing page. Then have more detailed integration 4-6 weeks out. Sarah Demo Then we will get into some catalog specifics from a high-level What's next Schedule a scoping call with Juan Sending Juan resources needed to get started (specbook, catalog, pricing, etc.) When our team gets halfway through the build, we will start doing product training Notes Starting with Panels (flat panel doors) Currently receiving orders: they do everything manually, they get BP in via email or in person, he will go over those blueprints and create a design and reverse engineer it into Mozaik. Super manual. He keeps inventory They don't have anything computerizing When he gets blueprints they are coming from a design software and a handwritten BP Using Mozaik Pricing Really depends. He does linear foot price most of the time. Differs between finishes and profiles Typically will tack on an additional 20% each for specialty items He is starting with a base cost and marking up from there. He is flexible in his pricing, the customer success team will help with pricing comparisons. Juan can assist with this? Does he want the general public to view his catalog? Probably have public to start. We recommend he have his front facing website, keep that as primary website --> order now button that links. We want it to have a higher price if he is doing public ordering. Production into Mozaik Shipping Local, pick up, delivery Now he is in flat panel he wants to send out flat packs so he can ship further on a palet Working with RFMX in Denver, he has a pricing schedule on a spreadsheet that we can calculate into his shipping cost He bought a bigger shop from a smaller shop so wants to move quickly.

Call with Luke Sabin
engagements.body: Call wouldn't go through

Update since meeting, they will be moving forward with Allmoxy but with $250 for the first month and $699/month thereafter $699 USD/Month. 12 Month Contract. $1199/Month Discounts: B2B/Dealer account, Legacy, Quickbooks desktop Small Enterprise - less than $1.5 mil 10% discount if paid in full. Catalog: Door front components only thermofoil and 5 piece doors 10-12 categories Will not be supplying materials Using two manufacturers Optimizations: Quickbooks desktop Hein de Hoog Asking for all dealings to remain between Allmoxy and Embolden doors. Have a couple manufacturers that they have built relationships with. Packaging and selling Ikea systems Wanting a software that can handle customers inputting their own orders.

Designer Issues/Ask - 03/17/25 @Bobby Lewis VHC reached out regarding their materials not fully showing in the designer's options. I asked @Aaron Dundas about this and he said that since you were overseeing this project for them that I should route this to you. Here's their main question: We have some problems we are looking at but we aren’t quit sure how to fix them. See attached images for our concerns. I'm assuming these just need to be mapped in their designer or maybe just resynced between the MRP and designer? Please let me know how you'd like me to advise them on next steps for getting this corrected. @Madison Theisen for vis.

03/12/25 - Go-Live Working Session Agenda: Reece's findings What questions do you currently have? What information was missing during the testing? Questions When customer is going through the tutorial, it tells them that the price they see on the bid includes shipping. The hinge type should not be able to be picked for cabs without doors. The emails going to the customer was going in spam. We need a finished left/right end option on the counter tops. If the customer saves their bid before adding more stuff we only get the first email that was sent with the first save with the original invoice. Gave the customer an option to pay before the order was verified. No email to customer when status changed to in progress. No email to customer when order changed to complete. Go-Live Next Steps What do we still need before turning people loose in here? Notes: Appcues - can we disable the appque that mentions shipping price? Hinges - best practice for hiding this and not having it impact pricing. Pete Questions: Marketing Video material - interested in pulling clips from this video to incorporate into their video. Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Allmoxy-Finalized-Items::01JP1CMSVNV2QTZ8CATXFMMRWM

03/03/25 Onboarding Cont. Customer Canva roadmap: https://www.canva.com/design/DAGgtXhwqT4/UQbXZWKCviEAgDEoyOCz8Q/edit Created the customer sign up and order flow reviewed how to export financial data Had Pete run a stripe test transaction Next steps is getting confirmation that the designer is working and that they've completed their system test. After that we'll be ready to get this in front of customers! https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-System-Testing::01JNA72NNH1270W4SPKTVBGX6W

VHC - Services Meeting 02/26/25 Agenda Items: Countertops MRP - Aaron here to help build these out in real time. Cabinet vision Export - Base 3 cabinet drawer still not coming through, need Aaron to troubleshoot. 3D Designer: Current cabinet products are ready to be used Closet components would need to be scoped and built with Aaron Next steps   Get the cabinet and countertop products ready for use within the MRP & communicating with cabinet vision Schedule follow up time for Reece, Pete, Jim, and I to review and test the system (customer account creation, order submission, payment submission, updating order statuses, etc) Notes: Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Allmoxy-Catalog-Countertops-Export-Closet-Components::01JMXB2V433QJP7H7JCA4DB9MQ Action items Aaron Dundas Finalize countertop product setup in MRP (01:09:21) Reece Sarnstrom Test the new countertop product thoroughly (01:12:34) Peter Adams Provide colors for each tier of countertop materials (01:13:12) Carson Ward Schedule meeting for Monday to conduct further testing (59:53) Follow up with Bobby regarding 3D designer status (01:02:03)

VHC - Services meeting 02/17 Issues: Export - when exporting more than one product, the last product in the export doesn't contain all of the information. Materials - Need to provide guidance regarding updating material tables and how that information needs to be set up for the export to pass the information. Anything else catalog specific? Notes: Bobby deleted the cabinet vision export file Aaron recreated the export but now we need to reconnect their products to the new export. Working through this with Aaron on this ticket: https://allmoxy.atlassian.net/browse/SRV-198 Meeting recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Wood-Products-Allmoxy-ORD-Export-Testing::01JM5GXJ7AC37KHS8YPJ853282

VHC Onboarding 02/13/25 Goal - Continue to outline the customer order flow Agenda: Order Flow - Payments & Shipping If we have customer's pay before processing, there will most likely be scenarios where they need to pay more for the actual shipping or you have to provide a refund. Recommendation - Give customers an initial order limit so they can submit an order. Once your team verifies the order you can add the actual shipping cost and confirm with the customer they need to submit full payment. Once payment is received you can then move the order status to in progress. Today's takeaway confirm if we're comfortable with this new flow Review trigger setup and make adjustments if needed Set up the shipping options (will call, local delivery, freight) Set up a new test customer to test all triggers and make a test payment utilizing stripe. Action items Review and complete triggers Set up all shipping options Create a new test customer, using the sign-up flow, for testing triggers and moving a test order through the stages we've outlined (including the actual transaction via stripe) Cabinet Vision Issue: Made a test order for 4 cabinets, the export only brought in three. Whatever last product is in the ORD, it's not bringing across all of the product information. Other issue: Door finish material - needs help getting this coming across in the exporter. 3D - Big concern touch base with Bobby to see where we are at. Next steps - scheduling time with Aaron to troubleshoot the exporter Need Bobby to give me an update on the 3D designer next steps Their working through the triggers and testing the system as a whole Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Wood-Allmoxy-Onboarding::01JKYEA9F8X4KJKWGCKMX4VFGR

VHC Onboarding 02/10/25 Goal - Outline how an order flows through Allmoxy and identify the key steps where team interaction is needed. Agenda: Homework review Trigger templates Add distro email as a contact Order flow Customer sign-up Order placed Order ready for production Order in production Order ready to ship   What's needed in this flow Customer communication - triggers along the way to inform the customer of what's happening Internal communication - who reviews the orders and sets them up for scheduling with production sending the information to the right person scheduling for delivery/pick-up Homework Outline triggers & update email text information Set up your shipping options Sign up as a new customer and walk the order through the system. Account sign-up Create an order (adjust price to $2-3 so you can run a test transaction with stripe) From your side, move the order through the necessary statuses (verified, in progress, completed, shipped) Confirm all triggers are working as desired Next Steps Schedule a review meeting once the triggers & shipping options are complete. We'll run the test together. Notes: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Wood-Allmoxy-Onboarding::01JKR7HB32A7S3BK5GQEF0WKB2

CS Onboarding Playbook Is this a VIP/Legacy customer?(If you're unsure check with leadership) No Is our Services team being utilized for this build? Yes, hybrid build Who is the main POC within the customer? Peter Adams Confirm the customers desired Outcome for using Allmoxy Online Storefront(No more hand to paper ordering) Notes: Using this in hopes to boost sales and help their business recover from a horrible year last year. When is the customers goal launch date? 03/01/2025 What cadence of meeting schedule did you set with the customer? Notes: As much as they can handle. Weekly, if not more. Ask the customer to define their benchmark for success Customers able to sign up, place an order, pay for their order, all within one place. What Software(s) does the customer have? How will Allmoxy work with these softwares? Cabinet Vision, Quickbooks Is the customer looking to fit Allmoxy around an existing ordering system or is this their first?(Utilize your Services Rep to more deeply understand how this impacts the catalog build.) Notes: No, this will be their main ordering system. Is the customer letting you drive the meeting and follow an agenda or do they want to drive and take charge of meetings? Either can be okay, but dependent on the customers approach to onboarding you will need to pivot your approach to working with them. Notes: Yes, they're looking for guidance and are willing to use what we have pre-built. Any initial concerns with the customer and their ability to launch(personnel, resources available, understanding of product, etc?) Notes: No, they are eager to start and ready to get the ball rolling.

02/06/25 VHC Wood Kickoff Meeting Goal - Understand VHC's use case for Allmoxy and better familiarize myself with their strategy. Agenda: Introductions (My role, VHCs history) Current Status (What's completed and what's left for the catalog) Catalog & designer being used? Key pieces of information Payment processing - Stripe Accounting software - ? Machining software - CV and is working? Platform familiarity - where have you explored so far? Inventory management & resource allocation - ? Onboarding Steps Setup a meeting cadence to cover all aspects necessary to utilize Allmoxy Starting point - Setup guide Stripe, update your theme, and create a test account you can use for any/all testing Automation - set up the desired triggers for customers and employees Consider price adjustments needed and if items need to be grouped (tags) Financial data - exporting out of Allmoxy (manually or use third party like transaction pro or SaaSant) Next steps Schedule next meeting to start working through your unique use case Notes: Jim joined the meeting Pricing looked good from Peter's perspective Catalog Cabinets are built Integrating right now with Cabinet vision Using the template catalog - attributes are up to date Pricing is working correctly Countertops - getting a quote from Aaron on the cost to get this built. Accounting QBO - currently used Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/VHC-Wood-Allmoxy-Kick-Off::rzH4XKyiWWI0TvCZ

@Madison Theisen. Can we get a CS rep assigned to this one and I can work lock step with them?  There are the normal CS things that still need to take place and I will work on the other elements as I have committed

engagements.title: Call with Andrew Ryals
engagements.body: URL confirmation

Title: Allmoxy Demo - : Palmetto Cabinet Doors Date: Wed Jun 11 2025 12:00:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, ryalscabinets@gmail.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 50 mins Overview - Bobby Lewis, head of sales at Allmoxy, highlighted his 31 years of experience in cabinet door manufacturing during introductions. - Palmetto Cabinet Doors, owned by Andy Ryals, has evolved into a door manufacturing business since 2019 and generates approximately $500,000 in annual revenue by producing around 75 doors daily. - The current order process relies on text message photos, which creates operational issues for sporadic customers due to manual data entry into the Razor Gauge system. - QuickBooks is used for accounting, but it incurs a 3% credit card processing fee, which is not passed to customers; Allmoxy’s Stripe integration offers a reduced fee of 2.9%. - Inventory management is manual and lacks systematic tracking, leading to inefficiencies and a need for more automated solutions. - Allmoxy's customer portal enables online ordering, instant pricing calculations, and automatic generation of cutting lists and invoices. - Pricing strategy discussion emphasized throughput accounting, allowing for customizable pricing based on wood costs, labor, and market dynamics. - A 12-month subscription agreement for Allmoxy is set at $350 monthly, with an included customer success agent for setup and training on workflow. - Engineering support for custom formula development is available at $150/hour, to aid in pricing formula customization. - The portal for Palmetto Cabinet Doors will be palmettocabinetdoors.allmoxy.com, providing access to their new product catalog. Notes 🏢 Company Introductions and Background Bobby Lewis introduced himself as head of sales at Allmoxy with 31 years experience in cabinet door manufacturing Andy Ryals owns Palmetto Cabinet Doors, a family business operating since 1971 that transitioned from full cabinet shop to door manufacturing in 2019 Company currently generates approximately $500,000 annual revenue producing around 75 doors per day Business operates with 70-80% delivery rate to customers, remainder pickup 💻 Current Operations and Pain Points Current order process relies on text message photos from customers, which works for regular clients but creates issues with sporadic customers Manual data entry into Razor Gauge cutting system from Cabinet Vision software Uses QuickBooks for accounting with credit card processing at 3% fee (not passed to customers) Lacks systematic inventory tracking - relies on manual monitoring and ordering Pricing system based on 200 square inch increments but wants more scientific approach incorporating labor and material costs 🛠️ Allmoxy System Demonstration Customer portal system allows online ordering with instant pricing calculations Automatic generation of cut lists, invoices, and shipping documents upon order submission Integration capabilities with Cabinet Vision for CNC panel nesting Time tracking and job progress monitoring through scanning stations Automated inventory management and capacity planning features 💰 Payment and Collections Management Stripe integration offering 2.9% credit card processing (improvement from current 3%) ACH transfer options for lower cost payments Automated payment reminder system with customizable trigger emails Customer-specific payment method restrictions and pricing tiers QuickBooks Online integration launching within weeks of implementation 📊 Pricing Strategy and Formulas Discussion of throughput accounting methodology (sales minus materials equals throughput) Customizable pricing formulas based on wood costs, labor, and market conditions Pre-built door catalog template available for quick setup Engineering support at $150/hour for custom formula development Capacity scheduling based on 75 doors per day production capability 📋 Implementation and Next Steps Monthly subscription cost of $350 based on $500K annual revenue 12-month subscription agreement with annual review and adjustment Customer success agent included for setup and workflow training Portal URL will be palmettocabinetdoors.allmoxy.com Product catalog includes MDF doors, wood doors, miter doors, and applied moldings Action Items Bobby Lewis Send subscription agreement to Andy for Allmoxy software implementation Create Allmoxy instance for Palmetto Cabinet Doors with product catalog setup Schedule kickoff call with customer success team Send phone number and contact information to Andy Andy Ryals Review and sign subscription agreement for Allmoxy software
@Bobby Lewis Andrew Ryals North Carolina Door manufacturer Using Cabinet Vision Wants to learn more about how Allmoxy helps production work through the shop.

Call with Bud Smith
engagements.body: Spoke with Bud and let him know that he can continue building his catalog while the pause is in place. He was very excited and appreciative of this.

Pause Request 9/30/24 Bud reached out saying that their sales have dropped throughout August and September which lead to his leadership asking his team to cut all unnecessary expenditures. He'd prefer to have a temporary pause (3 months) so they don't lose the work he's completed so far and that they can move forward with leveraging Allmoxy. At this time it seems to be an expense they can't afford. Bud's original email: "So, company sales took a downturn in August. Everyone thought that we would see a rebound in September. Well, that has not happened yet, and management is a bit anxious. I have been asked to suspend any expenditures that I possible can at this point in time.We need to pause or terminate our Allmoxy account as soon as possible. Of course I would rather suspend the account and let it be dormmate until such time that we could reactivate. Hopefully, that will be the first of year. I’m assured that the Allmoxy format is seen as a viable part of the business going forward, but now is a time for immediate cutbacks. The option of complete termination is something that I and the management team do not want to pursue. The likelihood of the company starting from scratch in the future is not likely to happen. Can you reach out to the powers that be at Allmoxy and respond to this email with a course of action that we both can abide by. The suspension of billings needs to be immediate. As in end of business today. If you can make that happen the team at Wedgewood will be very grateful. Please reach out with any questions regarding this request." I've responded to Bud by including Mekenzi and Broc so they can help determine an appropriate plan of action with hopes we can get them reactivated at the beginning of the year as Bud mentioned. @Broc Hill @Mekenzi Falslev for vis.

engagements.title: New Sign Up - Wedgewood Cabinetry
engagements.body: Signed up for an account in the installer, check to see if a card is on file.

Onboarding Meeting Notes Q2 2024 Jeff Jennewein 5th Meeting 5/15/24 Agenda: Invoicing (Financial/Account Overview) Triggers - Use triggers or account settings to send invoices to customers If they're paying through the system you can link them back to their account page to submit payment. Leverage Tags if you need to send to multiple people Order Settings - Set up your terms and conditions for your order settings Output page - Customize your invoice to show exactly what you want Export - Export invoice queue for invoice movement into Sage Pricing You'll need to review this with Juan - He's out on Paternity leave for this week and next. If you can share examples of your pricing questions I can pass these along to my team, otherwise we'll need to wait for Juan. Your Agenda items What questions do you have? Next steps determine how close the pricing is working and either schedule a training session with our team or wait for Juan's return. Work on the output pages - Invoice, packing slip, etc Follow up on any other items Notes: Question - on pricing - need to be able to match their cv pricing in Allmoxy (how can they do this) Package video from Juan - PKG file Allmoxy master custom pkg file - should be able to upload into CV Found a restriction - version of CV 2021 Tried it in CV 2023 Perametric pricing - in CV right now. 4th meeting 5/2/24 Agenda: Financial settings Have you given more thought to how you, Bud, and the accounting team would like this to operate? Payment Processing Stripe - shared the incorrect link - we're 2.9% across every card type. Authorize.net- would need to connect to whichever processor you'd prefer. Simple Pay - Payment would have to take place outside of Allmoxy. Paperwork Allmoxy export orders to sage - if we use stripe or auth then customer's pay through Allmoxy. If using simple pay then customers would order through allmoxy and receive the invoice and link to pay once the orders are exported into Sage. (issue here is the delay in export time - Ex: customer could submit Friday but you don't export until Monday so their order doesn't move at all.) Credit Lines vs. Pay before Processing This flow will also impact if we're offering credit lines or making individuals pay before you're starting. If we're paying through Allmoxy then customers credit lines or lack of would need to be managed here. If payments aren't managed then customers could see large overdue balances unless you're also updating their invoices on our end. Next Steps Continue to discuss internally how you'd like this to flow. Determine a deadline of when this decision needs to be made by. Meeting next week to discuss catalog progress and automations (triggers, price adjustments, and tags) Notes: Using Sage Version: Sean sitting for Laura, she wasn't able to make it. Shared that our Stripe rate is 2.9% across all card types. Sean understands that it might be easier to just use Stripe. Going to push that we use Stripe so it's easier for customers to manage their payments and access their invoices. Next week Going through PO and invoicing process Bud to schedule time with Juan to review his master product pricing before cloning to other products. 3rd Meeting 4/23/24 Agenda: Catalog/Product Update Have you been diving in? Any issues so far? Questions for Juan? What do you think your timeline is based on the work you've done so far? Allmoxy Comfortability What do you feel comfortable with? What don't you feel comfortable with? What will be needed to launch with customers Customer Accounts & Contacts Permissions Financial settings Pay simple and sage impacted Automation Triggers, Tags, and Price Adjustments Output Pages Exports Shop paper work Shipping Options Next Steps Provide articles needed for whatever's covered Share recap email Notes: Got the PKG in CV and going through the product on that side. Going well from his view and not concerned about that. Confirmed that he has to build the products and can't just import with the PKG file. Next weeks meeting will be circling back on the topic of Sage/Pay Simple (financial settings) May 8th we'll check in on the catalog build and discuss triggers, tags, and price adjustments 2nd Meeting 4/17/24 Agenda: Pay Simple & Sage Questions How does Allmoxy calculate sales tax? We calculate tax based on the ship to and ship from address. Can Allmoxy redirect to a Pay Simple landing page once an order is placed? We can't, however, customers can place an order in Allmoxy, export these orders into Sage, then sage can send the invoice along with the payment link. Ideal world would be we could connect Pay Simple as a gateway and then customers could be invoiced and pay within Allmoxy while the accounting information could simply be transferred to Sage. Catalog/Products Update How was your meeting with Juan? How much time are you dedicating towards the products each week? Based on that do you believe May 27th is doable? Allmoxy Comfortability What do you feel comfortable with? What don't you feel comfortable with? What will be needed to launch with customers Customer Accounts & Contacts Permissions Financial settings Pay simple and sage impacted Automation Triggers, Tags, and Price Adjustments Output Pages Exports Shop paper work Shipping Options Next Steps Determine if weekly meetings are necessary while you're building the catalog and adjust cadence accordingly. Reschedule next weeks meeting Stripe & Authorize.net Meeting with Juan all went well PKG package was shared Has the printout of the current product Didn't talk timelines Doesn't think it'll take too long Using this for commercial - artcitech, builders, etc - no designer working on this, their goal is for customers that know what they're doing to enter orders themselves. Possibly creating a new order flow for this type of customer due to the limitations of exporting orders to sage and then invoicing through Sage while paying through pay simple. 1st Meeting 04/11/24 End Goal - Allmoxy Onboarding Introduction Agenda: Introductions and Background My role and what you can expect from me. People and their personas Utilizing Allmoxy to input orders online, correct? Main goal? How do you envision Allmoxy working in your flow? Who is going to be your Allmoxy power user? Catalog build information (products and pricing) Design information - 20/20 software (screen to machine person) Operations information - order processing, verification, etc Goals and Timelines What would deem this successful for you? What's your goal launch date? How much time are you able to dedicate to this weekly? Wanting to pursue a hybrid model for the integration/catalog build? Caution - the importance of the catalog build and how it impacts your launching ability Getting Started With Allmoxy What's your familiarity with Allmoxy? How comfortable are you with the system? Support Center (highlight the support sections) Review creating an order and have the team review the template to get ideas of how he wants his catalog setup. Next Steps Set up a weekly meeting cadence Wednesday 11 - 12, or 1 - 3 Thursday 11 - 12 Friday 11:30 - 1 Meet with Juan next week for the catalog scoping Homework: Connect Stripe create test orders and review how the template catalog is set up so you can bring this feedback to Juan. Next week we'll kick off with our full week 1 onboarding Site navigation Customer & contact creation Financial settings What else would you like to discuss? Notes: Sean - owner Laura - President Bud - head of innovation (CV guru) James? Miguel - part of the team on this Alex - estimates, owner, pushing towards more commercial work Laura and sean - executive sponsor. B2B function as a possibility Going straight to customers - Don't want the designers to use Allmoxy. hard date - 4-6 weeks CV - match the fabrication structure Weekly - list of tasks for each meeting Invoicing structure May 27th - Go live date Payment options Has an online payment system - PaySimple Sage - accounting software AWI compliant CS Onboarding Playbook Is this a VIP/Legacy customer?(If you're unsure check with leadership) No Is our Services team being utilized for this build? No Notes: Bud wants to build his own products. Who is the main POC within the customer? Bud Smith Confirm the customers desired Outcome for using Allmoxy Online Storefront(No more hand to paper ordering) When is the customers goal launch date? 05/27/2024 What cadence of meeting schedule did you set with the customer? Notes: Weekly Ask the customer to define their benchmark for success Customers utilizing Allmoxy for ordering. What Software(s) does the customer have? How will Allmoxy work with these softwares? Sage Accounting, Cabinet Vision Is the customer letting you drive the meeting and follow an agenda or do they want to drive and take charge of meetings? Either can be okay, but dependent on the customers approach to onboarding you will need to pivot your approach to working with them. Notes: Bud has let me drive so far. Any initial concerns with the customer and their ability to launch(personnel, resources available, understanding of product, etc?) Notes: Only concern is the timeline they have considering Bud wants to build the products himself. Also Simplepay is their payment gateway which we don't work with and they use Sage so all of their invoicing will most likely be handled outside of Allmoxy.

engagements.title: Allmoxy Savings Agreement Renewal | UltrashelfRoss
engagements.body: Allmoxy Savings Agreement Renewal | UltrashelfRoss

Renewal 2024 In attendance: Ross, Kenzi, Broc Primary use is B2B, they were paying $1899 a month and paid a lot of money and didn't see value for years and felt some frustration so Broc gave them a price break last year. They have not been paying since. Doing B2B with Lewis. Lots of new equipment in 2023 and hoping for big things for 2024. Madi and Sarah have been huge helpers. Ross is very grateful for all they've done! Broc wondered if some shopify customers are good for B2B?? Midwest is paying through Shopify. Brackets for example, b2b would automatically populate so Midwest could use some help or some push there. Now how do capitalize and scale out with the deal with Lewis and others? Broc says there are some good relationships we can introduce to some B2B clients that would merit a conversation for an increase in price for their monthly subscription amount. Ross has been talking to Todd Lewis with Design to Size which has some potential. Canada is Broc's only concern. But Ross isn't worried about doing B2B with Canada. Ross would like some connections with some high-volume people on the East and West coast area. They have Sierra Wood Products, etc. who they might B2B with but don't want to saturate the West too much to protect the people they do business with. Kenzi will reach out to our established clients in the Midwest and East, make an intro, and say would you be open to an intro on a great client who offers floating shelves, etc. for B2B accounts, try to add some value from the sales side. Once we establish pricing, we will go year to year on the agreement. Broc and I will evaluate pricing and then eventually go to a multiyear agreement to save them some money. Kenzi will reach out to entertain B2B with some accounts before we set pricing. Ultrashelf is the only one doing floating shelves within Allmoxy. Ross is looking to continue to work with Madison and Juan to diversify the catalog quite a bit with their floating shelves. They have their mother company Ultracraftsmanship be their new name. It would just be a rebrand. We might see barn doors, hardware, etc. coming down the pipeline which is really exciting. Has a pretty diverse offering coming down the line. Ross will ping Madi and Kenzi when barn doors, etc. are ready so they are both ready for those offerings. @Madison Theisen for visibility Clients who would be a good fit (Kenzi will add to this list) Midwest (Madi, what does this look like with Midwest so far?) Wurth LAC Kenzi will go through the installer for other good prospects.

@Broc Hill For renewal this year... In the last TTM. they paid 4 months at $1899 then nothing for the remaining 8 months. Ultra Shelfs' primary use is B2B so they want to keep that in mind with the subscription rate. They started running revenue and have done 26k in verified revenue the last TTM. How much longer are we willing to give them a break on their subscription and how would we like to strategically price them?

engagements.body: Churn Risk Playbook Please share the most up to date health pulse from the CSM's perspective. Red(this customer is not in good standing and is on track to churn) Has this customer asked to pause or cancel their account or is this playbook being utilized preemptively? Requested to cancel Notes: They requested to pause for 2 months until March because they "didn't have time for Allmoxy right now". They seemed like they were coming back and eager to get started again. Now they are booked out for the entire year and are unsure if Allmoxy fits into the big picture of the recent changes they have made. "Yes, we are booked for most of the year and won't have time to implement it. In addition, we aren't sure how it will even integrate with our current business model. We have a lot of other major changes going on currently, and want to focus on those." How much is the customers monthly subscription? Please note if the customer has received any reductions in MRR during their lifetime with Allmoxy as well as any significant, recent implementation costs. $250.00 Industry Segment (Data Confirmation) Custom Cabinet Shop Business Size (Data Confirmation) SMB How long have they been a customer of Allmoxy? 04/15/2025 Is this customer launched? No Is this customer working with our implementation team now, if so please elaborate on the nature of the work/project. No Is this customer a legacy/strategic account? No Has the customer proposed any solutions that would eliminate or delay their churn risk? This could be a price reduction or delivery on a specific feature. Notes: No, they are already paying our lowest price. They are just too busy to keep going. Please share any other strategic context that we should consider related to this account. (Ex. Industry influence/connections, personality details, known expansion opportunity, competitor tech stack, etc.) Notes: This customer had a rough start to implementation. They expected Aaron to tell them how to price and had no pricing method prepared. When asked how they currently priced their products, they didn't really know. They got frustrated about the export process, and thought that Aaron should be the Mozaik experts and not them. They didn't really understand their own software. But then things started to get much better implementation-wise. We were going strong for a few weeks, and then they just kept pushing meetings back because they got too busy. If they had clear documentation to start the process, I think we could have cut implementation time down by a month. Who's the main customer contact associated with this escalation? Ezra For reporting purposes, please check that this playbook is completed. Yes

Onboarding meeting #2 with Ezra and Michael (10/13/25) Review Stripe Connected? Triggers (enable Ezra and Michael to receive emails or texts for tasks) Review triggers Hiding categories to only show to verified customers? For testing, make sure to have a test account with a verified customer trigger. Today Price adjustments Financial settings Shipping Meeting Summary Josh and Michael discussed several key configuration areas within Allmoxy, focusing on tax setup, shipping preferences, financial workflows, and price adjustment mechanisms. The goal was to tailor Allmoxy for both B2B and consumer workflows, ensuring flexible payment handling, controlled shipping access, and pricing customization for various customer types. Key Takeaways Ezra’s Initial Questions Ezra created a test account but noticed sales tax wasn’t being calculated. It was later confirmed the system was correctly applying the 6% tax. Discussion arose around how Allmoxy handles tax-exempt customers. Michael mentioned Cornish had never charged sales tax and was exploring how to handle tax going forward. Josh clarified Allmoxy uses TaxJar for sales tax calculations, updating automatically based on customer or company zip codes unless a custom tax rate is applied. Shipping Michael asked how to restrict shipping for homeowners and require local pickup. Solution: Use tags (e.g., “homeowner”) to control which customers see specific shipping methods. Created a “Will Call” method only visible to users with the “homeowner” tag. Businesses can see both “Will Call” and standard delivery. Custom shipping formulas were implemented: <10 miles = $75 10–20 miles = $150 >20 miles = $250 Josh used ChatGPT to generate and test formulas for local delivery pricing. FedEx/UPS shipping integration is available but relies solely on weight, not dimensions. Price is returned from the carrier, and a multiplier (e.g., x2.3) is applied to estimate final charge. Recommendation: test average order weights to balance profitability and fairness. Financial Settings Allmoxy defaults to a “pay first” model, like Amazon. Michael expressed preference to offer limited credit to known local companies and default to prepayment for unknown or consumer customers. Options discussed: Use credit limits (e.g., $2,000) and build limits (e.g., $5,000) per company. Set defaults for all new customers and override for trusted accounts. Create tags like “local” or “verified” to govern payment terms. Trigger notifications for overdue payments and include terms in automated messages (e.g., full payment due before shipment). Payment methods: Stripe integration is used. It stores cards securely and allows repeat billing. ACH payments have lower fees. Stripe processing fees (~2.9%) can be offset with hidden price adjustments if needed. Invoices & QuickBooks: QuickBooks Online integration is in beta. In the meantime, use CSV export and third-party tools (e.g., Transaction Pro) to import Allmoxy data into QuickBooks. Manual journal entry is also a viable option for now. Price Adjustments Allmoxy supports automatic surcharges and discounts on a per-line-item basis. Adjustments can be: Global (e.g., 10% discount for all orders), Tag-based (e.g., surcharge for all “homeowner” accounts), Product-specific (e.g., MDF doors only), Company-specific (e.g., a discount for a trusted cabinet shop). Priority settings determine which adjustments apply if multiple are active. Suggestion: clean up inactive or unused price adjustments to reduce confusion. Price adjustments are not shown to customers if intended to offset fees (e.g., Stripe). Action Items Michael to review and confirm tax strategy with his new accountants, especially concerning tax exemption and resale scenarios. Create additional shipping methods as needed, tied to customer tags. Test and finalize the local delivery formula using known addresses. Set up default credit and build limits, and tag-based rules for verified vs. non-verified customers. Determine initial Stripe surcharge offset approach (e.g., via hidden price adjustment). Clean up old or inactive price adjustments in the system. Consider simple rules and low credit limits for new/unknown customers to mitigate risk. Monitor QuickBooks integration progress or explore third-party import tools in the meantime. Next Steps Internally align on credit policy for new vs. existing customers (e.g., default limits, pay-first tagging). Finalize shipping and delivery strategy for homeowner vs. business customers. Build task automation and triggers for overdue payments, order status, and onboarding. Revisit 3D Designer in January once deprecated, and assess new consumer-facing tools Allmoxy may release (e.g., Cabinet Portal). Gather Mosaic or Cabinet Vision files if participating in the developer raffle for file testing.

Onboarding meeting #1 with Ezra & Michael Triggers Trigger for Mosaic manual review will notify the internal team when products with a specific tag (e.g., “Mosaic Manual Check”) are included in an order. This is to flag attributes that cannot be imported directly into Mosaic. New company creation trigger will send an email alert to designated employees (e.g., Ezra and Michael) when a new company signs up. Verified order trigger will notify the customer when an order has been verified and can also send internal users an export file to drop into Mosaic. Welcome email trigger can be sent automatically upon account creation to outline next steps and expectations (e.g., “Please allow 24 hours for account verification”). A proposed trigger will automatically apply a “non-verified” tag to new companies, restricting their access until approved. Tags Product tags: Used to group products requiring special handling or triggering (e.g., for Mosaic manual imports). Company tags: "Verified Customer": Grants access to full catalog and standard pricing. "Non-Verified Customer": Default for all new accounts; limits access until reviewed. "Homeowner" and "Business": Used to segment product/category visibility. Category and product visibility can be controlled using the "company exclusive" setting with tags. Some products (e.g., MDF doors) may need to be duplicated to allow tailored visibility for homeowners vs. business customers. Tags can be used to apply different price adjustments based on customer type. Company Creation Customers will be allowed to create their own accounts rather than requiring manual setup. Upon signup, a company will: Be required to submit their EIN (configured as a custom field). Automatically receive a “non-verified” tag. Be hidden from all product categories unless verified. Admins will receive a notification via trigger upon each new company signup. Admins must manually review the company and change the tag from “non-verified” to “verified customer” if approved. Contact Creation Each company must have at least one associated contact. Admins can create individual contact profiles for employees or customers. Internal employees (e.g., shop staff) can be added with customized permission levels. Customers created manually must have “make payments” permission explicitly enabled. Login credentials (username and temporary password) can be created manually when adding a contact. Internal users can test contact permissions by logging into accounts directly using assigned credentials. Custom Fields A custom field for EIN Number was added to the company signup form. Field Name: EIN Number Variable Name: ein_number Set as required for new business account creation Field is editable and visible during sign-up Additional custom fields can be added as needed for internal qualification or filtering. Custom field responses will populate directly into the company’s contact page. Meeting Summary The team met with Josh Baker from Allmoxy to walk through account configuration, Mosaic export setup, product visibility management, and automation via tags and triggers. Key features were demonstrated and tested, including restricting catalog access for unverified customers, sending automated emails, and configuring shipping methods and Stripe payments. The goal was to finalize backend logic and begin internal testing of customer workflows and pricing configurations. Key Takeaways Important Discussions Product attributes were updated for MDF doors, slab doors, floating shelves, and applied panels. Mosaic exports are functioning for most product types; MDF and slab door exports still need testing. Tags and triggers will be used to manage visibility, approvals, and Mosaic import limitations. Front-end and pricing logic will be finalized before customer onboarding. Orders will be verified internally before being locked from customer edits and moved into production. Technical Issues & Fixes Applied panel pricing formulas were not saving properly; Aaron resolved this during the call. Audio issues at the beginning of the meeting were resolved by switching devices. Bid deletion process was clarified and demonstrated. Decisions Made Self-service account creation will be allowed but controlled using verification workflows. EIN will be required at signup to help distinguish businesses from homeowners. Verified customer tag will unlock full catalog visibility; non-verified customers will see no products. Stripe will be the payment processor; setup will begin soon. Products may be duplicated for different customer types where necessary. Challenges or Concerns Visibility setup using tags and duplication can become complex and needs careful testing. Search bar visibility for restricted products cannot currently be disabled. Stripe requires detailed company information and may delay payment processing if not set up early. Action Items Ezra to: Complete testing of Mosaic exports for MDF and slab doors. Set up and test product visibility rules using dummy accounts and tags. Finalize trigger for Mosaic import alert. Review and configure Stripe payment processing. Set up welcome email and new company alert triggers. Josh to: Send Allmoxy knowledge base/articles to Ezra. Assist with trigger and pricing formula setup as needed. Michael to: Align with Ezra on customer approval workflows and pricing structure. Review Stripe onboarding requirements. Next Steps Begin internal testing using dummy accounts with homeowner and business tag combinations. Finalize Stripe integration and run test transactions. Configure and test customer visibility, order flow, and trigger emails. Duplicate products and assign tag restrictions where needed for specific customer types. Continue exploring triggers, pricing adjustments, and process automation in next week’s session. Next follow-up meeting scheduled for Thursday with a focus on trigger implementation and potential launch preparation.

Call with Ezra 9/3/25 Getting frustrated with the implementation process. They feel like they gave us a lot to do last week and we only delivered on part of it They wanted a few other products in there (slab doors, drawer boxes, MDF doors, and Floating Shelves) All we did was the floating shelves. Highlights how we are currently having a bandwidth issue on the implementation team. The information was in the ticket- I just don't think Aaron had time to get to the rest of it New customers in implementation should be getting more priority than launched customers with project work Meeting Summary The meeting centered around the status of floating shelf product setup within the catalog, including product structure, validators, pricing, and development communication. The team discussed ongoing issues with incomplete implementation, lack of expected deliverables, and the need for a more efficient workflow between the client and the development team. Key Takeaways Important Discussions The floating shelf category was initially imported from another catalog but will now be recreated in-house to match the desired look. A new category for Floating Shelves should include sub-products: Floating Shelf Left, Floating Shelf Right, and Floating Shelf Wall-to-Wall. The current product setup includes only four shelf products, which are incomplete — missing pricing, images, validators, and mock-up data. Technical Issues & Fixes No pricing is being generated for the floating shelf products in the system. Validators to restrict invalid dimensions are missing: Maximum width should be restricted to 96 inches. Minimum thickness should be 2 inches (to avoid users inputting 1.5" thickness, which is unbuildable). Expectations were that the system would automatically apply markups and differentiate floating shelf pricing from cabinet boxes, but this is not happening. Box material product attribute was created, but the floating shelf markup column hasn't been fully implemented. Decisions Made The team will mimic the look of the originally imported shelf product but rebuild it within the internal catalog. Josh will meet with Aaron during the next scheduled session to: Review last week’s progress. Clarify what was expected and what was actually completed. Align on the list of deliverables. Josh requested a clear specification document from the client outlining attributes, pricing, and rules for each product (e.g., drawer boxes, slab doors, floating shelves). Challenges or Concerns The client expressed concern over the slow progress and lack of deliverables despite being billed hourly. Frustration was shared about having to attend development meetings without technical value-add, while still being charged. Client feels the communication loop is broken — lack of clarity on what is needed from them and delayed responses are stalling progress. Client reiterated a desire for offline development based on clear specs, rather than real-time collaboration during meetings. Action Items Josh & Aaron to review: The work done last week. The original meeting recording for specifications (especially for drawer boxes and slab doors). Client to resend the Google Drive with project documents (already sent to Josh B. at Allmoxy). Aaron to: Implement pricing logic and markup for floating shelves. Add necessary validators (width and thickness). Ensure product images and mock-ups are in place. Team to clarify what deliverables were expected vs. completed for transparency and billing alignment. Client offered to provide additional clarification by phone if needed during development — emphasized preference for being consulted rather than delayed due to uncertainty. Next Steps Josh to meet with Aaron during the next session to align expectations and confirm what was completed. Team to prioritize completing floating shelves with: Pricing, Validators, Correct product structure and mock-ups. Client to remain available for real-time clarifications as needed — preferred over waiting for weekly meetings. Further products like drawer boxes, MDF doors, and slab doors to be implemented once specifications are confirmed and last week’s scope is completed. Continued communication to ensure development progresses offline with minimal disruption to the client’s time.

Title: Allmoxy Kick Off Call Date: Wed May 07 2025 12:15:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: aaron@allmoxy.com, bobby@allmoxy.com, joshb@allmoxy.com, ezra@cornishcabinetry.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 73 mins Overview - Team Structure Established: Introduced key members - Bobby (lead), Aaron (implementation engineer), Josh (customer success rep). Ezra’s implementation list shared. Focus on collaborative 'hybrid build' approach with client and Allmoxy. - Implementation Timeline and Strategy: Client aims for quick launch; weeks to implement. Emphasis on chunking product groups (starting with cabinets). Weekly meetings proposed for tracking progress and ensuring quality. - Homework for Client: Critical task for client (Ezra) to define dropdowns & attributes for each product category using a spreadsheet. Aim to maintain momentum with 'homework' before each meeting, next meeting scheduled for Tuesday at 1 PM EST. Notes 👥 Introduction and Team Roles Meeting recording initiated at the beginning of the call Team introductions took place with Bobby, Aaron, Josh, Michael, Ezra, and Danny attending Bobby outlined team roles: Josh as customer success rep and point of contact, Aaron as implementation engineer responsible for formulas and processes Bobby mentioned he had shared Ezra's implementation list with the team 🔧 Implementation Approach Bobby explained they're doing a 'hybrid build' where Cornish Cabinetry and Allmoxy build the system together The client confirmed Ezra will be the main point of contact for the implementation Client expressed they are ready to start selling products and ideally want implementation completed within a few weeks Team suggested 'chunking' the implementation by focusing on one product group at a time 📅 Implementation Strategy and Timeline Josh proposed establishing weekly cadence meetings to track progress toward launch Aaron explained they can launch as fast as the client can accept functionality, with emphasis on quality over speed Client indicated their priority is cabinet implementation rather than doors, though door libraries are needed for cabinet builds Aaron recommended starting with door implementation first for consistency across the catalog 💻 Allmoxy Platform Overview Aaron provided a walkthrough of Cornish Cabinetry's Allmoxy instance Demonstrated how categories and products are organized in the system Explained the group attributes functionality that allows selections to inherit properties Showed how to use company exclusive settings to restrict product visibility for testing 📦 Product Configuration and Categories Aaron demonstrated how to make products visible to specific customers using tags Explained the navigation ribbon that changes dynamically based on context Team decided to start with cabinet boxes implementation first, followed by doors and drawer boxes Aaron showed a sample base cabinet product with one drawer, one roll out, and one shelf as an example Demonstrated how to manage product images with recommendation to use JPG format and square images Explained hidden attributes with default values and their importance in formulas 🚪 Door Type Configuration Aaron showed how to configure door type attributes on products Client identified their main door categories: cope and stick doors, MDF doors, slab doors, and glass doors Under cope and stick doors, client wanted to offer: quarter inch round over, square shaker, beveled shaker, OG, and triple bead ⚙️ Attribute Configuration and Product Display Aaron demonstrated how changes to door type attributes affect the dropdown options on the order page Explained how export field data is used to drive information to Mosaic (their manufacturing software) Aaron emphasized the importance of copy-pasting exact terms from Mosaic to ensure proper integration Showed how to add images to dropdown options to help customers visualize selections Stressed the importance of saving changes before leaving pages Discussed adding an outside edge attribute to cabinet products 📝 Planning Next Steps Bobby suggested the client's most important homework is defining dropdowns and offerings for each product Aaron recommended creating a simple Excel sheet listing attributes and offerings for each product category Discussed setting 'homework' for each meeting to maintain momentum Bobby emphasized that although setup requires upfront work, the system scales remarkably well once configured 🔍 Product Configuration Demonstration Aaron demonstrated how to clone a product to create a test copy Showed how to add a new attribute (edge profile) to a test product Demonstrated how to adjust attribute display width for consistent appearance Explained the importance of reducing friction for customer adoption by configuring attributes similar to current ordering process Aaron showed the complete list of available attributes in the system Warned against deleting attributes as it can break formulas and affect the entire system Explained how attributes feed into pricing formulas ✅ Meeting Wrap-up and Next Steps Client confirmed their homework to define attributes for each product category Aaron explained how to navigate to specific attribute pages Discussed meeting cadence - client preferred weekly meetings due to their custom cabinet workload Scheduled the next meeting for Tuesday at 1:00 PM EST Action Items Ezra Create a spreadsheet listing all attributes and dropdown options for cabinet products Define what attributes are needed for each product category identified in the initial list sent to Bobby Determine cabinet door types and profiles to be offered Prepare for the next meeting on Tuesday at 1 PM EST Aaron Schedule the next implementation meeting for Tuesday at 1 PM EST Be prepared to help implement the client's defined attributes and dropdown options Josh Establish implementation roadmap and cadence with the client Coordinate weekly meetings to track progress toward launch

Title: Allmoxy Scope Call - Cornish Cabinetry Date: Tue Apr 29 2025 11:00:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, ezra@cornishcabinetry.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 66 mins Overview The Allmoxy Scope Call for Cornish Cabinetry focused on product demonstration and implementation planning, led by Bobby and attended by Ezra. The meeting introduced the customization capabilities for Cornish's cabinetry needs, including template catalogs and specifics on product types such as doors and countertops. Key discussions included product prioritization, specifically for doors, columns, and floating shelves, as well as exploring frameless cabinet options and edge banding. Bobby emphasized the significance of balancing customization with order simplicity and confirmed software compatibility. The implementation timeline was established, outlining next steps including signing a subscription agreement and creating a custom instance on the Allmoxy platform. Finally, they discussed pricing strategies, highlighting differences between B2B and B2C approaches, and committed to action items for the next stage of product implementation. Notes 🏁 Meeting Introduction & Product Scope Bobby confirmed the meeting is being recorded Ezra mentioned sending an email previously that Bobby had missed Bobby reviewed Cornish Cabinetry's product needs, confirming custom hoods, solid surface tops, plywood tops with nosing are all doable Bobby demonstrated his database of template catalogs that can be modified for Cornish's needs Bobby explained some products (like countertops) would need to be built from scratch 🛠️ Product Prioritization & Options Ezra stated their priority products: doors, columns/fillers, floating shelves, drawer boxes Semi-custom cabinets would be preferred revenue source but not currently feasible Specific door types prioritized: cope and stick door, routed MDF door, banded panel options Additional options discussed: glass-ready doors, slab drawer fronts Team decided against offering raised panel, valances, moldings and trims 📦 Frameless Cabinet Options Discussion about offering frameless cabinets as Cornish can produce them quickly Bobby shared his experience with flat-packing cabinet components for customers to assemble Discussed edge banding options: 4-5 PVC colors (white, gray, black) plus stain grade options Bobby described his previous business model shipping unfinished components without assembly Bobby shared how successful relationships with local finishing companies worked well 🏭 Software Implementation Process Discussion about cabinetry library complexity and options Bobby advised finding the right balance with customization options Cautioned against too many variables as it can make ordering difficult for customers Showed how to add a comment field for special instructions Confirmed Mosaic compatibility, though with some limitations compared to Cabinet Vision 📊 Implementation Timeline & B2B Focus Ezra asked about next steps and implementation timeline Bobby explained the process: prioritize products, sign subscription agreement, instance creation Bobby clarified instance = cornishcustomcabinetry.allmoxy.com with company branding Instance created within hours of agreement signing Discussion about B2B vs B2C focus - Allmoxy better suited for B2B customers Bobby shared his experience scaling from B2C to exclusively B2B 💰 Pricing Methodology & Business Growth Ezra asked about pricing strategies for B2B vs their current B2C approach Bobby introduced 'throughput accounting' vs traditional cost accounting Bobby explained treating labor as fixed expense rather than variable cost Recommended books: 'Throughput Accounting' by Bob Fox and 'The Goal' by Eliyahu Goldratt Ezra committed to creating prioritized product list and Bobby will send subscription agreement Next steps: finalize product priorities, sign agreement, kick off implementation Action Items Ezra Create and send prioritized list of products to be implemented Review and sign subscription agreement when received from Bobby Bobby Lewis Send subscription agreement to Ezra Prepare templates based on prioritized product list Schedule kickoff call after agreement is signed

Title: Allmoxy Demo - Date: Tue Apr 15 2025 15:15:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, cheneil@allmoxy.com, michael@cornishcabinetry.com, ezra@cornishcabinetry.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 60 mins Overview The Product Demo and Consultation meeting for Allmoxy provided insights for Cornish Cabinetry, a custom cabinet shop that has expanded significantly over the past few years. The session began with an overview of Cornish's operations, highlighting their focus on high-end residential work and collaboration with other cabinet shops. Bobby Lewis introduced Allmoxy, a software tailored for B2B woodworking businesses, boasting features such as a customer portal for seamless ordering, integration with QuickBooks and Stripe, customizable product catalogs, and automated communication solutions. The design editor, while useful for drag-and-drop designs, may require assistance for custom projects. Critical next steps identified include defining Cornish's product offerings and examining the suitability of the current catalog to establish pricing and dropdown options. Action items were assigned to both Cornish’s team and Bobby to facilitate further discussions and preparations for implementation, with a follow-up meeting scheduled to refine catalog specifics. Notes 🏢 Cornish Cabinetry Introduction Cornish Cabinetry is a custom cabinet shop focusing on high-end residential work Company has grown from 5-6 employees to 23-24 employees in the past 4-5 years 99% of their business is residential They CNC parts for other cabinet shops with 8-10 regular customers They build cabinets for other shops that fall behind Occasionally build doors and custom hoods for other shops Realized working for other businesses is easier than working with homeowners 💻 Allmoxy Introduction Bobby Lewis created Allmoxy while running his own cabinet business Allmoxy was designed for B2B manufacturing operations, specifically for woodworking The software allows for a self-serve customer experience - 'the Amazon experience' Company was eventually acquired by a private equity firm The software integrates with QuickBooks online Bobby will send Cornish Cabinetry the demo to explore (already received) 🛠️ Software Functionality Overview Allmoxy provides a customer portal for consistent ordering experience System manages customer accounts with credit limits and payment processing Integrates with Stripe for payment processing Features customizable product catalogs and pricing Automatically creates Mosaic exports for production Handles tax calculations via Tax Jar Manages inventory levels and creates purchase orders Includes automated communication triggers for order status updates 📊 Design Editor Features 3D Design editor available but somewhat limited to template models Custom products in designer require implementation engineer involvement Designer allows drag-and-drop cabinet design with instant pricing Primarily designed for B2B rather than B2C customers Cornish wants to move away from custom design toward catalog-based offerings Finishing services are a bottleneck for many cabinet shops 📝 Customer Implementation Customers create accounts through the portal Pricing is based on monthly subscription model tied to annual revenue Allmoxy has been around since 2006 Has processed nearly $2 billion in transactions Approximately 40,000 daily users Works with both established B2B companies and those transitioning from B2C AI lead generation tools can help find potential customers 🔄 Next Steps Defining what products Cornish wants to sell is the critical first step Examine current product catalog in demo to see if it's suitable Need to determine desired dropdown options and pricing structure Bobby will send a subscription agreement after catalog is defined Implementation engineer helps build the catalog once defined Another meeting will be scheduled to review catalog requirements Action Items Ezra Review the Allmoxy demo to examine the current product catalog Define what products they want to sell through the platform Schedule a follow-up meeting with Bobby to review catalog requirements Michael Work together to determine dropdown options for their catalog Bobby Lewis Send meeting link for follow-up discussion about catalog specifics Prepare to send subscription agreement after catalog is defined

Canary & Allmoxy Sales Training Meeting Canary & Allmoxy Sales Training Meeting December 19, 2025 11:15 AM MST 25 mins sarah@allmoxy.com madison@allmoxy.com sales@canarycc.com ap@canarycc.com View Meeting Recording Customer Account Management and Tagging The team clarified the structured approach to customer account creation and tagging to ensure correct pricing and product visibility. Sarah Pierce outlined the account hierarchy where a company acts as an umbrella for multiple contacts, each requiring login access to Almoxy, with individual homeowner and retail customer accounts as alternatives (03:02) Contacts must be linked to their company to enable login and permission controls. Tags are critical for product availability and pricing, with Tony and Michelle responsible for tag management. Correct tagging ensures contractors and dealers see accurate pricing tiers and product selections. This tagging system directly supports differentiated customer experiences and revenue management. Financial settings were explained, including two checkout flows: pay before processing and credit limit, with recommended credit limits for new customers set between $5,000 and $10,000 (06:27) Pay before processing requires payment before orders reach ordered status, controlling cash flow upfront. Credit limit allows orders up to a set limit before payment, keeping the order pipeline active without upfront payments. Order limits typically exceed credit limits to permit multiple pending orders before payment clears. Grace periods and finance charges are configured to enforce timely payments and manage receivables risk. Order Entry and Folder Organization Order entry processes were optimized for speed and clarity using order-level settings and folder structures to improve client experience and internal handling. Sarah introduced order-level settings that allow global attribute changes (like closet color) across all products in an order, reducing manual updates for customers and sales reps (12:41) Customers can apply changes globally or selectively to new products, streamlining order edits. Folder-level settings extend this control to subsets of products, helping differentiate sections within larger orders. This feature supports faster quote revisions and customization, enhancing client satisfaction. Sarah planned to send supporting articles for further training on these features. Folders act as an organizational tool to divide large orders by space or project segments, such as separating closets or kitchen components, with subtotaling at the folder level improving bid clarity (15:19) Folders enable clients to view and manage the order in manageable segments, improving transparency. Sarah noted potential production implications as folders may split similar product lines, which needs confirmation from Tony to avoid disrupting cut lists. The team agreed the client-side clarity outweighs potential production complexities, pending testing. The folder feature was identified as a solution to client confusion with long, undifferentiated parts lists. Reporting and Sales Visibility The discussion highlighted report usage for sales tracking and commission attribution, emphasizing the need for accurate tagging and order status management. Sarah emphasized that sales reports depend on orders reaching the “status to charge customer,” which varies by client and can affect report reliability (20:19) Some customers are charged at different order statuses, causing timing discrepancies in sales data. Orders report allows filtering by entered date and tags, enabling sales reps to track their orders and commission-related activity. Tagging customers or orders with sales reps’ identifiers is essential for accurate commission tracking and sales attribution. Product tagging can also track push efforts on specific items by filtering orders containing those products. Invoice reports provide comprehensive financial visibility including credit card fees and finance charges, supporting broader accounting and revenue management needs (22:50) Sales focus remains primarily on orders and sales reports, with less emphasis on aging and collections reports for the sales team. The reporting capabilities support performance monitoring and strategic sales efforts. Integration and Workflow Alignment The team confirmed current workflow integration between design and order systems, supporting smooth data transfer and order management. The team confirmed Tony and Michelle handle exports from Closet Pro to Almoxy and export order data as needed for Excel and other reports (23:50) This integrated workflow supports seamless transition from design to order entry without redundant data entry. Exported data supports downstream operational needs such as production planning and financial reconciliation. Continued coordination between sales, design, and operations teams is implied to maintain data accuracy and efficiency. The process supports scalability and reduces errors in order processing. Training and Knowledge Transfer The meeting focused on foundational training for new team members to ensure consistent use of Almoxy and enable effective sales operations. Sarah and Madison committed to sending detailed articles covering folders, order-level settings, company creation, and contact creation to support Melissa’s onboarding (24:20) This training material aims to unify workflows and reduce inconsistent practices across the team. Melissa’s intention to blend different user approaches into an optimized personal workflow was acknowledged as positive. Ongoing support remains accessible through Madison for clarification and advanced questions. The materials and follow-up will improve onboarding speed and operational consistency. Action Items Sarah Pierce Send training articles on folders, order-level settings, company creation, and contact creation to Melissa for further review. (24:20) Melissa Discuss folder usage and potential production impact with Tony to ensure it does not disrupt production workflow. (18:10) Communicate with Tony and Michelle regarding tagging structure and financial settings to confirm accuracy and consistency. (05:40) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KCMG3Q7E7EMN0PDM1AP13TKP 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:
