engagements.body: Meeting with Ariel Rosario from Next Gen Cabinet Doors June 18, 2026 9:15 AM MDT 35 mins aaron@allmoxy.com jonathanr@allmoxy.com sarah@allmoxy.com nextgencabinetdoors@gmail.com View Meeting Recording Pricing Model: Calculates door costs using panel square footage, waste yield, markup, and material pricing with custom fee adjustments. Proxy Variables: Centralize pricing logic for easy updates and consistency, converting all formulas to use proxy functions after raw formula testing. Material Pricing: Dynamic vendor pricing tables manage veneer and wood costs with waste yield and markup, enabling quick manual price updates. Product Catalog: Shaker doors grouped under a submenu enhances navigation; UI changes separate from pricing logic to avoid disruption. Next Steps: Apply proxy formulas to all doors, validate pricing, finalize catalog structure, and prepare for launch to prevent revenue loss. Technical Training: Pricing formula uses platform-specific syntax like JavaScript/PHP; raw formulas aid error checking before proxy conversion. Pricing Model Development The team refined a pricing method that calculates door costs based on panel square footage, waste yield, markup, and material pricing, keeping customer-facing materials hidden (00:31). Aaron Dundas explained the formula uses panel dimensions plus rails and styles pricing based on wood type and square footage. Custom fees apply if any rails deviate from default dimensions, stored in meta attributes for easy configuration without formula edits. Edge charges, panel profile adjustments, and face style pricing are added conditionally, defaulting to zero when not applicable, ensuring consistent calculation. The formula integrates cost-plus markup logic, making sure every price element is accounted for while allowing for flexible pricing adjustments. The team discussed how wood purchasing by board foot differs from the square footage model, emphasizing profitability and waste accounting (05:21). Aaron noted board foot pricing reflects volume and stock thickness, which requires collapsing to square footage in the pricing model to avoid inaccuracies. They agreed to default stock thickness per material and ensure the model captures waste correctly, avoiding financial losses. This approach aligns with vendor ordering methods while maintaining price accuracy within the system. Proxy Variable Implementation Proxy variables were introduced to centralize pricing logic, enabling easy updates across all door products without changing individual formulas (14:03). Aaron demonstrated that the raw formula is used for testing and detailed review before moving to a proxy variable for live pricing. Proxy variables act like functions or constants, called in pricing formulas to maintain consistency and simplify version control. He advised developing formulas in raw mode first, as proxies do not perform syntax error checks, reducing risk of broken pricing. The team plans to convert all door pricing formulas to use the proxy variable for streamlined updates and error reduction. The proxy concept was clarified as a reusable function that consolidates pricing rules into one place (16:16). Using proxy variables allows changes to immediately affect all products referencing it, improving maintenance efficiency. Aaron cautioned about differences between part attribute variables and order/group item variables, requiring careful context checks when converting. The team agreed to apply proxies to all door types after validating each formula’s correctness in testing. Material Pricing and Vendor Integration Material-specific pricing tables will drive veneer and wood pricing dynamically based on vendor rates (24:35). Ariel detailed that different veneers have distinct prices per sheet, which the system will convert into price per square foot for calculations. Aaron confirmed each panel material can have its own waste yield, price, and markup in the table, enabling precise cost control. The system will pull the correct price row dynamically depending on the selected material, allowing manual price updates from vendor data without formula changes. This enables responsive pricing adjustments aligned with supplier cost fluctuations. The team emphasized minimizing manual formula edits by focusing price updates on vendor-supplied tables (30:02). Ariel will maintain veneer and wood pricing attributes to keep prices current. Aaron agreed this approach reduces complexity and risk of errors, making pricing management more user-friendly. Product Catalog and User Experience Plans were made to organize shaker doors under a submenu in the main catalog to improve navigation (31:45). Ariel will group shaker door variants under a "slim shaker" category for clearer customer selection. Aaron confirmed customer-facing changes will not affect backend formulas, allowing free UI adjustments without breaking pricing. This approach balances ease of use for customers with backend pricing integrity. The team agreed that catalog structure changes will be handled separately from pricing logic to maintain system stability (32:02). This separation reduces dependencies between UI management and pricing formulas, enabling parallel workflows. Next Steps and Timeline Aaron committed to applying the proxy pricing formula to every door product before the next review meeting (31:33). This includes validating calculations and ensuring all doors pull pricing from the centralized proxy. Ariel plans to finalize catalog organization and handle product visibility adjustments concurrently. Jon confirmed the goal is to complete all formula updates and pricing checks before going live to avoid revenue losses (32:47). The team expects the next meeting will cover final formula reviews and pricing validation to prepare for launch. Technical System Details and Training Aaron clarified the pricing formula language is similar to JavaScript and PHP but with platform-specific syntax constraints (12:48). He provided guidance to use the system’s built-in syntax rules to avoid AI or external tools generating incompatible code. The team discussed the importance of using raw formulas during development for error checking before converting to proxy variables. Aaron noted that proxies can be used in exports and pricing but lack syntax validation, so careful testing is critical (18:08). Ariel expressed intent to learn the system gradually, appreciating Aaron’s walkthrough and support in simplifying the pricing formula setup. Action Items Aaron Dundas Add non-standard rail and style custom fee for horizontal and vertical mid rails in the pricing method to account for tooling changes and avoid undercharging. (04:26) Add default meta values for rail widths and custom fees to enable scalable adjustments across cabinetry door products. (10:00) Provide the platform syntax rule set to facilitate AI-assisted formula editing and prevent coding errors in the custom pricing language. (12:48) Convert all cabinetry door pricing formulas to use proxy variables for uniform pricing control and easier maintenance, then test calculations for accuracy. (31:33) Copy parts configuration from the Copenstick door to the slim shaker door to resolve part-related issues and align meta attributes. (23:55) Ariel Organize the trim and slim shaker doors within the main product catalog under a subcategory to simplify customer navigation without affecting pricing formulas. (31:45) Maintain panel and wood pricing attribute tables to keep pricing current with supplier cost changes, minimizing the need for complex formula edits. (30:02) Jon Richardson Coordinate a follow-up meeting to review completed pricing formulas and proxy variable implementation before system go-live to ensure accuracy and customer profitability. (32:37) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KV6264B2219D7BYT24SA1PK9 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

Meeting with Ariel Rosario from Next Gen Cabinet Doors June 17, 2026 11:30 AM MDT 40 mins jonathanr@allmoxy.com nextgencabinetdoors@gmail.com sarah@allmoxy.com View Meeting Recording ACH and Credit Limits: ACH payments need activation; start credit limits at $2,500 with order limits $3,000-$4,000 to manage risk. Interest and Bounce Fees: Interest charges start after grace period; bounce check fees set at $20-$25 to enforce timely payments. Offline Payments and Discounts: Offline invoice payments accepted with 3-5% cash discount to boost faster payments and cash flow. Customer Self-Service: Customers create accounts via URL, view dashboards with order/payment info to reduce admin workload. Pricing and Discounts: Use tags for 5-10% VIP discounts based on volume; price adjustments target margins and competitiveness. QuickBooks Integration: Manual QuickBooks Desktop entry now; plan to automate with QuickBooks Online as order volume grows. Technical Issues: Initial delays from audio and connection problems; follow-up session planned to cover shipping options. Financial Setup and Payment Processing The meeting established clear guidelines for setting up financial settings and payment methods in Almoxy to streamline customer transactions. ACH Payment Setup and Credit Limits Explained (17:55) Customers can be enabled for ACH payments globally and individually due to higher risk. Stripe is already connected for credit card processing; ACH needs activation. Credit limits should start low, suggested at $2,500 with order limits slightly higher, e.g., $3,000-$4,000 to keep orders flowing while managing risk. This structure allows customers to place orders exceeding credit limits but pauses shipment until payment reduces the balance. Sarah Pierce advised starting conservatively and adjusting limits based on customer trust and volume. Interest Charges and Bounce Fees to Manage Late Payments (22:42) Finance charges can accrue interest after a grace period, with thresholds to avoid small invoices. Suggested thresholds for interest invoice creation are $1, $5, or $10 to prevent cluttering accounts with trivial amounts. Bounce check fees can be set automatically, with $20-$25 cited as a typical fee. These features help enforce timely payments and reduce bad debt risk. Offline Payment Collection and Cash Discounts (27:14) Customers can pay invoices offline by check, cash, or card via Square integration in Almoxy. A cash discount (suggested 3-5%) can encourage faster payment and improve cash flow. Sarah will enable this setting for Ariel on the admin side. The system applies the discount automatically during offline payment entry, improving customer incentives. Integration with QuickBooks and Reporting Tools (31:00) Ariel confirmed use of QuickBooks Desktop, with potential future migration to QuickBooks Online. Almoxy can push all financial data directly to QuickBooks Online for automation. For now, manual entry of orders into QuickBooks Desktop is manageable with low order volumes. An aging report in Almoxy helps track outstanding invoices and overdue payments, supporting credit management. Customer Account Management and Ordering Workflow The discussion clarified how customers create accounts, place orders, and view order histories to promote self-service and reduce manual workload. Customer Self-Service Account Creation via URL (25:14) Ariel prefers customers to create their own accounts to enter billing and shipping info, reducing administrative work. John Richardson will send Ariel the URL for customers to register themselves. This allows customers to log in independently and manage their details securely. Customer Dashboard and Order Visibility (25:59) The company-level dashboard shows outstanding balances, due amounts, shipped past due orders, and open invoices. Customers see this dashboard when they log in, giving them transparency on their orders and payments. They can view open and past orders, as well as any bids or price adjustments. This visibility supports better customer engagement and reduces inquiry volume. Order Placement with Payment Options (21:30) Customers can place orders with payment either fully upfront or using credit limits with deposits. Shipping charges can be collected either upfront or at shipping time, with trade-offs on collection ease discussed. The recommended approach is partial payment upfront (e.g., 50%) and balance at shipping to improve payment collection. Pricing Adjustments and Customer Tagging The team explored pricing strategies using Almoxy’s price adjustment and tagging features to target discounts effectively. Price Adjustments Using Tags and Conditional Logic (32:54) Price adjustments can be set globally, by company, or by customer groups tagged with labels like VIP. Tags enable targeting discounts or surcharges automatically without manual intervention. Sarah promised to send an article explaining how to create and manage tags since they cannot be created directly in price adjustment menus. Conditional groups allow fine-tuning discounts based on products or customer attributes. Discount Strategy Based on Volume and Customer Type (35:49) Sarah noted no fixed industry standard; discount levels depend on competition and margin goals. Typical VIP or volume customers receive 5-10% discounts to reward loyalty and bulk purchasing. Ariel’s plan is to maintain pricing close to margin targets and selectively give discounts for large orders. This approach balances profitability with competitiveness. Technical and Operational Challenges The meeting also highlighted minor technical issues and operational considerations necessary for smooth ongoing collaboration. Audio and Connection Issues Delayed Start (04:23) Confusion about attendees and audio echoes caused initial delays and distractions. Ariel’s computer crashed initially but was resolved quickly, allowing the session to proceed. Jon Richardson worked on muting and audio adjustments to reduce echo effects. Despite challenges, the team pushed through to cover the agenda. Need for Follow-Up Sessions for Remaining Topics (36:48) Due to time constraints and technical delays, some topics like shipping options were deferred. Ariel requested another session later in the week or early next week to cover shipping methods. John agreed to check calendars and schedule a follow-up meeting. Email correspondence will continue in the meantime for quick questions and clarifications. Documentation and Support Articles to Aid Learning (34:12) Sarah committed to sending helpful articles on tagging, price adjustments, and QuickBooks integration. This supports Ariel’s independent learning and system mastery. Continued support is planned via meetings and email. Strategic Vision and Customer Engagement The meeting reinforced the goal of empowering customers and optimizing financial controls to support growth. Empowering Customers with Self-Service Tools (25:16) Allowing customers to create accounts and view order history improves engagement and reduces administrative load. The system design encourages customers to manage their own billing and shipping information. This aligns with Ariel’s goal to scale operations efficiently. Balancing Credit Control and Order Flow (20:15) The credit and order limits create a controlled environment to minimize financial risk while keeping customers ordering. Sarah emphasized starting with tight limits and adjusting as trust and volume grow. This approach helps maintain healthy cash flow and reduces credit losses. Using Pricing and Discounts as Competitive Tools (35:55) Pricing adjustments and targeted discounts help maintain margins while rewarding key customers. The flexibility of Almoxy’s triggers allows sophisticated pricing strategies without manual overhead. Ariel’s approach to selective discounting supports market positioning and profitability. Integration and Reporting for Financial Oversight (31:00) QuickBooks integration is planned to automate financial data flow as order volume increases. Aging reports in Almoxy provide ongoing visibility into receivables health. These tools support Ariel’s goal of strong financial control and timely collections. Action Items Ariel Rosario Review shared articles on Almoxy system settings and pricing tags prior to the next training session to prepare for further setup. (37:45) Jon Richardson Send Ariel the URL for customer self-registration on Almoxy to enable customer self-service account creation and ordering. (25:27) Send Ariel a meeting recap and any additional training materials or articles shared during the session. (37:52) Coordinate and schedule a follow-up meeting with Ariel to cover shipping options and provide additional setup assistance. (37:31) Provide ongoing assistance to Ariel via email as needed between meetings to support system setup and troubleshooting. (37:52) Sarah Pierce Enable the cash pay discount setting on Ariel’s Almoxy instance to allow offering incentives for faster payments. (29:09) Send Ariel an article explaining tag creation and management for price triggers and adjustments to support pricing setup. (34:12) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KV63AWBYZ5VDMCY5QXNKCMF4 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

engagements.title: Meeting with Ariel Rosario from Next Gen Cabinet Doors

engagements.body: Meeting with Ariel Rosario from Next Gen Cabinet Doors June 15, 2026 10:00 AM MDT 44 mins aaron@allmoxy.com jonathanr@allmoxy.com sarah@allmoxy.com nextgencabinetdoors@gmail.com View Meeting Recording Pricing Model: Uses square footage with 1.5-inch thickness multiplier; includes custom fees for profiles, panels, and rails. Shipping Strategy: Costs based on weight, distance, and custom formulas with adjustments for LTL and own truck deliveries. Implementation Timeline: Next review Friday 10:45 AM MT to finalize pricing; benchmark data due before meeting. Payment Terms: Supports full prepay or credit limits; 50% down payment feature planned soon with workflow support. Catalog Setup: Focus on coping stick doors first; panel species attributes added for MDF, hardwood; simple, flexible options. Communication: John main contact for follow-ups; Sarah and John support shipping and payment setup without engineering fees. Pricing and Catalog Setup The team agreed to build a flexible, profitable pricing model starting with coping stick doors to standardize and replicate pricing across all door types (00:06). Pricing model based on square footage with 1.5-inch thickness multiplier to ensure all material costs are covered and profitability is maintained (00:07). Aaron emphasized avoiding losses by including all material costs plus markup in pricing formulas. The approach supports both square foot and board foot pricing methods without changing behavior. Aaron will help the team maintain and adjust pricing formulas easily post-implementation. Custom fees for specific door profiles and modifications will be implemented based on competitive standards and internal cost considerations (00:13). Common profiles remain standard with no extra fees, but custom profiles like V groove will incur specific fees or markups. Fees will also apply to non-standard rail widths and multi-panel doors due to additional material and labor. Aaron will build toggles at the attribute level to switch between fixed fees or percentage markups for flexibility. Benchmark pricing data needed from the client to calibrate the model with competitive and internal target prices (00:20). Kristi will provide benchmark values for common door sizes including minimum and maximum dimensions. Aaron will use this data to tune the pricing formulas and ensure alignment with market expectations. A review meeting is scheduled for Friday at 10:45 AM Mountain Time to assess progress and adjust pricing. Catalog setup includes material selection and panel species attributes to accommodate standard and engineered panel options (00:40). Aaron will add a panel species attribute to differentiate materials like MDF or hardwood. The pricing system will enable defaulting or hiding certain options for simplicity. Proxy variables may be used depending on future product complexity, but currently, a simpler setup is preferred. Shipping Strategy and Implementation The team focused on establishing accurate, flexible shipping cost methods to avoid losses, especially for LTL and own-vehicle deliveries (00:27). Shipping cost calculations will rely on order weight, distance, and custom formulas to approximate actual expenses, especially for LTL shipments (00:29). Almoxy only calculates shipping by total weight, not dimensions, so additional flat fees or adjustments will cover dimensional impacts. Options include charging a flat fee with post-shipment adjustments or recalculating costs using weight multipliers. Sarah recommended quoting actual shipping costs after packing and updating invoices with any difference. Custom shipping methods to price own truck deliveries will factor in distance, fuel, and employee time using formulas like distance times a per-mile rate plus markup (00:31). The system supports custom variable formulas for flexibility. Customer tagging can limit delivery methods to appropriate geographic zones to control service offerings. Integration with UPS and FedEx provides quotes but lacks dimension consideration; this limits accuracy for assembled doors but is acceptable for flat-packed shipments (00:30). The team will work with existing integrations while managing expectations on shipping cost precision. Follow-up materials and detailed shipping guides will be sent to the client to help them understand and plan shipping pricing (00:33). Sarah and John will provide documentation and be available for questions. Aaron will handle formula syntax for shipping once pricing methods are decided. Implementation Timeline and Collaboration The group established a clear timeline with milestones and ongoing collaboration to ensure the project’s timely completion (00:23). Next review meeting scheduled for Friday at 10:45 AM Mountain Time to evaluate pricing formula progress and make adjustments (00:23). Aaron will deliver the initial pricing structure for coping stick doors by then. Kristi and team will provide benchmark pricing data before this meeting. Additional meeting planned for Wednesday at 11:00 AM to discuss payment processing workflows and shipping setup without incurring billable engineering time (00:38). Sarah and John will lead this session to assist with credit limits, invoicing triggers, and shipping configurations. The team agreed to keep meetings concise and schedule follow-ups if needed. Kristi’s team tasked with providing competitor pricing and internal standards to support pricing tuning (00:26). They will supply example jobs and final prices for benchmarking. Aaron will use this data to calibrate formulas for competitive and profitable pricing. Follow-up emails and invitations will be sent promptly to keep communication flowing and maintain momentum (00:26, 00:38). John is the main contact for ongoing questions and follow-ups. Meeting invites for Wednesday and Friday will be distributed soon after this session. Payment Terms and Order Processing The discussion clarified Almoxy’s supported payment flows and planned enhancements to support partial payments and credit limits (00:35). Current payment options include full prepayment or credit limit with invoicing and verification to control order approval (00:35). Full payment is required to move from bid to order stage. Credit limits allow orders to be submitted and verified with invoice triggers. Upcoming feature to support 50% down payments with release to production is planned within the next few months (00:37). This will automate reminders and enforce partial payment before production starts. Sarah offered to assist Kristi’s team in setting up workflows and triggers for this process. Client values this staged payment approach to reduce financial risk and align with industry standards (00:35). They will collaborate with Sarah and John for configuration outside of paid engineering time. Product and Catalog Details The team reviewed door construction logic and material attributes to ensure catalog accuracy and future flexibility (00:39). Focus on coping stick doors first to finalize logic, then replicate across other door types (00:39). Other doors like engineered panels will use the same pricing and part logic after coping stick is confirmed. Aaron will confirm if panel and rail materials are consistent or need separate attributes. Panel species and material attributes will be added to catalog to allow selection of different panel materials like MDF or hardwood (00:40). This will help maintain accurate costing and pricing across door types. The system will allow defaults and hiding of certain selections to simplify user experience. Decision to keep catalog simple with standard offerings and allow customers to mix panels and doors freely (00:41). No complex named door catalogs or extensive proxy use planned at this stage. This keeps the system easier to maintain and update. Process and Communication Improvements The meeting set clear roles and communication channels to streamline implementation and reduce delays (00:00 - 00:02). John assigned as primary customer success contact for follow-ups and task management to keep engineers focused and projects moving (00:01). Kristi and team will email John with questions; he will coordinate responses and next steps. This structure reduces overhead on Aaron’s billable time. Sarah and John will support shipping and payment workflow setup without billing engineering hours to optimize client costs (00:37). They offered to meet separately with Kristi’s team for process coaching. They will provide guides and explanations to reduce confusion. Team confirmed no current direct machine data exports, focusing on paper cut lists simplifying initial setup (00:02). Aaron’s work will focus on ensuring all needed data appears on cut lists accurately. Shipping and pricing will be integrated into these outputs as needed. Clear documentation and notes encouraged to ease future maintenance as highlighted by Kristi’s notes on code and Aaron’s appreciation (00:06). Keeping code and formulas well-documented supports long-term usability and quick updates. Action Items John Support shipping method setup and shipping pricing strategy for LTL and own vehicle options. Help manage communication and follow-up actions post-meetings. Assist Sarah in scheduling and follow-up tasks. (03:50) Aaron Dundas Build and implement door pricing formulas starting with the coping stick door template, including fees for custom panel profiles, edge profiles, and rail width deviations. Add a hidden meta attribute for formula optimization and user management, and prepare the pricing model for review on Friday at 10:45 AM Mountain Time. Also, add panel species attribute for engineered panel door material pricing flexibility. (19:30) Kristi/Ariel Rosario Provide benchmark pricing examples for standard, smallest, and largest door sizes reflecting local competitor prices before Friday’s meeting. Respond to shipping and payment workflow follow-up emails and review provided guides. (20:46) Sarah Pierce Send follow-up article and documentation about shipping methods, including LTL considerations, shipping cost formulas, and delivery options. Assist Kristi with setting up payment processing workflows and triggers outside of Aaron’s billed time. Coordinate scheduling of Wednesday 11:00 AM meeting and necessary follow-up with John and Kristi. (27:41) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KTWDGTEQQ4AEFQE3ZC51PHTA 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

Allmoxy Kick Off Call June 11, 2026 4:00 PM MDT 42 mins bobby@allmoxy.com sarah@allmoxy.com nextgencabinetdoors@gmail.com View Meeting Recording Catalog setup nearing completion with pending pricing formulas. Engineering support needed for correct price integration reflecting supplier costs. Shop ready for production after receiving final machinery. Job processing methods identified as inefficient; automation could save significant time. Training session on pricing and part configuration scheduled. Engineers will assist with catalog adjustments and ensure future self-service capabilities. Adoption of Utah pricing model aids competitive positioning. Supplier price integration provides flexibility and strategic purchasing insights. Customization of cut lists will enhance production efficiency. Accurate configurations are essential to match shop practices and reduce waste. Catalog Setup and Pricing Strategy The team has made strong progress on the catalog but needs engineering help to finalize pricing formulas and product configurations. Catalog mostly completed with pricing formulas pending (13:45) Ivan has updated product profiles extensively using the template but lacks pricing formulas. Pricing strategy will follow the Utah pricing model with base prices and overages for sizes over 3.5 square feet. Supplier prices from Sierra, Intermountain, and High Mountain are loaded into the system for cost inputs. Engineering support is needed to write and integrate pricing formulas correctly to reflect supplier costs and desired margins. Ivan and team possess strong domain and technical knowledge (15:56) Ivan and Ronald bring over 20 years of combined industry experience, including familiarity with Lewis Cabinet standards. Ivan’s electrical engineering background helps understand coding but he needs business-specific guidance for pricing. Kristi emphasized the need for custom cut times and offsets that differ from standard values based on shop practices. Engineering assistance planned to refine pricing and cut list accuracy (33:17) Sarah recommended a one-hour training call with an engineer to review the catalog and correct pricing and dimension issues. The call will be recorded to allow the team to make future changes independently. Engineers will help dial in panel profiles, style add-ons, cut offsets, and other unique product tolerances. John, a support employee, will monitor progress and ensure timely follow-up after the training. Multi-supplier pricing management clarified (38:10) Bobby clarified that customer pricing is set separately from supplier costs, which are tracked in a supply module. The team must decide which supplier’s cost to use for purchase decisions, likely the highest cost for safety. Periodic auditing will keep supplier costs and customer pricing aligned. Shop Readiness and Production Workflow The shop is ready to start cutting doors with the last machinery arriving, and workflow improvements were discussed to speed job processing. Shop fully powered and prepared to start production (07:33) The building finally has power after about a year of waiting. The last piece of machinery, a sander, is en route to the shop. The team is eager to start full production once final catalog setup is complete. Current job processing methods identified as inefficient (19:07) Kristi described that job take-out currently takes 2-3 minutes per job. Use of the system’s work schedule feature could reduce this to 5 seconds per job by automating scheduling and printing. Bobby demonstrated how assigning ship dates makes jobs appear on the work schedule automatically, but the team has not been using this feature. Plan to adopt work schedule for better efficiency (20:40) Kristi and Ivan acknowledged the potential benefits of using the work schedule for job management. Training or coaching may be needed to change current habits and fully leverage system features. Adoption of this workflow is expected to save significant time and reduce manual steps. Cut list configuration needs customization for shop specifics (24:59) Kristi highlighted problems with formula handling of multi-panel doors and incorrect panel orientations. The sizing and machining offsets require adjustments to reflect the shop’s actual cutter times and rail sizes. Bobby explained that cut variables and style/rail profile attributes control these calculations and will be adjusted with engineering input. Technical and Engineering Support Engineering resources will play a key role in finalizing catalog logic, pricing rules, and cut list accuracy to align with shop operations. Engineering to assist with pricing formula and catalog fixes (18:51) Sarah’s team will review existing catalog data and identify missing or broken attributes causing pricing failures. Ivan will collaborate closely with engineers to encode business rules and tolerances into the system. The team plans a video walkthrough or live call to ensure knowledge transfer for future self-service adjustments. Cut list accuracy to be validated through iterative testing (31:54) Sarah noted that Almoxy allows quick identification of dimension errors, such as a quarter-inch offsets. Ivan’s expertise will help diagnose causes of discrepancies for correction. The team will create orders and test outputs to verify cut lists reflect real-world production needs. Customization of machining offsets and panel orientations (26:35) Shop-specific cut times (e.g., 16 seconds vs. 30 seconds) and larger rail profiles require formula tweaks. Panel grain direction and style/rail adds must be configured correctly to avoid production errors. Engineering will audit these factors in the product attribute tables to tune precision. System setup includes managing multiple attributes and pricing variables (28:15) Cut variables and profile reveals are key attributes influencing cut list lengths and machining plans. Panel profiles also have additives or deductions that affect pricing and cuts. This detailed attribute mapping supports complex product configurations and pricing. Team Coordination and Next Steps The group confirmed availability for training and communication plans to ensure smooth catalog setup and readiness for production. Training session scheduled for Monday at 10am (35:37) The one-hour call with engineer Aaron will focus on pricing and part configuration. Ariel is the main point of contact, with Ivan and Ronald also included in communications. The session will be recorded and supported by John, who will track progress and follow-ups. Team ready to begin work immediately after training (35:18) Ariel confirmed no immediate time pressure but eagerness to finalize catalog and start production. The session will be tailored to keep engineering costs low while enabling autonomy. Communication details finalized for invitations and coordination (36:40) Emails will be sent to the team’s shared inbox to ensure all members receive updates. Bobby and Sarah facilitated introductions and shared local context to build rapport. Positive team spirit and mutual encouragement (39:51) The group exchanged birthday wishes and friendly comments, strengthening relationship dynamics. This positive tone supports ongoing collaboration and problem-solving. Market Positioning and Competitive Strategy The team is aligned on competitive pricing and catalog design to effectively enter the market with a strong value proposition. Adoption of Utah pricing model ensures competitive alignment (15:07) The pricing model includes base prices plus overage charges for larger doors, matching regional standards. This model allows the team to compete effectively with surrounding businesses. Supplier price integration allows cost control and margin management (15:39) Inclusion of multiple suppliers’ prices gives flexibility and insight into material costs. Strategic choice of supplier costs impacts profitability and purchasing decisions. Customization of pricing and cut lists supports differentiation (16:46) Tailoring cut times and machining offsets reflects shop efficiency advantages. Accurate cut lists reduce waste and improve production speed, strengthening market position. Leveraging team expertise to build catalog confidence (13:32) Bobby recognized Ivan’s deep catalog work as a strong foundation for market readiness. The combined knowledge from previous employers supports best practices in product setup. Action Items Kristi / Next Gen Cabinet Doors Provide supplier price lists from Sierra, Intermountain, and High Mountain into the system pricing interface to enable accurate pricing setup. (15:39) Review and flag catalog cut list and door configuration issues during the upcoming engineering training session to ensure accuracy and completeness. (32:31) Use the recorded engineering training session video to independently update catalog and pricing formulas for ongoing refinement. (33:05) Coordinate with Ivan and Ronald on applying pricing formula adjustments after the engineering training session to finalize pricing logic. (33:17) Ivan Ariel Continue catalog refinement focusing on adjusting cut time offsets and panel profile settings to reflect actual shop cutting times and machining accuracy. (16:45) Prepare to collaborate with the engineering team to implement pricing formula coding and troubleshoot derived pricing issues for accurate cost calculations. (30:54) Attend the scheduled engineering training session and actively participate in Q&A to clarify catalog and pricing configurations. (34:29) Sarah Pierce Schedule and facilitate a one-hour engineering consultation/training on pricing formulas and catalog configuration for the customer to enhance system understanding and usage. (34:29) Invite internal observer "John" to monitor implementation progress and provide post-training follow-up assistance to ensure customer success. (37:10) Arrange for engineering review of catalog pricing and cut list formulas to identify and correct errors such as attribute failures for improved accuracy. (31:00) Bobby Lewis Assist in troubleshooting and instructing the customer on using the work schedule feature to speed job processing and improve workflow efficiency. (19:07) Provide ongoing support and encouragement as the customer transitions to operational readiness to ensure smooth system adoption and issue resolution. (39:44) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KTVZC1QRF1HTZZ6A9KW8154W 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

Hey guys, I had a long conversation with these guys. They're in kind of an interesting situation. They got an instance of Allmoxy and then ran into facility problems big time. They're set up, for the most part, by Ivan, who knows the system well, but then they ran into facility issues, so they weren't able to get power for the building. Rocky Mountain Powers just finally signaled that they're gonna do it, so I've granted them active pause for quite some time. The challenge is that they haven't been able to use Allmoxy, so we just go ahead and hit pause. We're really not gonna want to do a renewal like normal on this one, because the account honestly has never even really started.

Good news here. Ivan came in, and it sounds like they were able to get the approval that they were looking for on their building. Rocky Mountain Power has begun installation of the stuff that's needed. Sounds like they'll be getting the green light to turn on the facility soon. Once they do, I will activate their instance at the 250 a month.
These guys came in and are having real issues getting the green light on the building and power .  They have paused the process until they have a facility running.
Made myself the BDR - was in contact with them April 2025 but never made myself the BDR

They are going to run commercial and allmoxy (D2C) Catalogue seperately. They are going to master Standard storage solution, Open cabinets, and work stations to put on a direct to consuemer site. They are ready to start working with payton. I also was able to get them to agree to B2B in with Lewis cabinet for doors.
Richard is sierra's allmoxy ninja

loved the idea and became aware of allmoxy a year ago because they have a systems engineer on staff. Wanted to create a website on their website so that customers could configure their own products. Richard systems engineer found allmoxy and wanted to use it. They cancelled allmoxy because the didn't fully understand the platform and felt mis treated. Jay agreed to continue his agreement and get his instance live. He wants D2C cabinet catalogue for garage shelves and Commercial showroom shelves. He also agreed that when he is in utah next he would let me take him mountain biking. Schling and ding. Richard Call (richard@sierraap.com) will be the Allmoxy Ninja that will be running their instance. Jay is sold on the idea that companies like amazon will be the way of the future because of the economic status at hand with C19.
The email was incorrect in the system which is why none of our emails have been reaching Jay.
Called Jay, He was busy. Is going to call me back in 20 minutes.

Title: Allmoxy Kick Off Call Date: Fri Dec 12 2025 16:15:00 GMT-0500 (Eastern Standard Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, joshb@allmoxy.com, crystalcabinetworks@hotmail.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 35 mins Overview - Implementation Start: Team will upload a structured product template to fast-track Almoxy setup. - Pricing Adjustments Needed: Current pricing model has gaps; accurate costs for materials and finishes are essential. - Payment Processing Choice: Client will use Authorize.net for payments, prioritizing security over convenience. - User Access Management: Multiple users will be added to Almoxy, ensuring appropriate roles and limited financial visibility. - Communication Plan: Written communication will supplement calls to address language barriers and ensure clarity. - Next Steps: Engineer to upload product template, with pricing details and Authorize.net setup needed by Monday. Notes 👤 Introduction of Josh Baker Josh Baker introduced as the Customer Success Manager for the client account and implementation process with Almoxy. 🔑 Login Credentials and Template Preferences Discussion on login credentials and access to product templates. Client interested in exploring the template catalog, preferring default clean slate templates over extensive customizations. 💰 Pricing Concerns Identified gaps in pricing formulas within the provided templates that need adjustments for accuracy based on client-specific costs. 📊 Product Catalog Focus Clarified that the product catalog display will focus on the sales and order entry system rather than manufacturing software (Cabinet Vision). 🌐 Website Usage Plans Client plans to use the website primarily for product display rather than direct ordering, targeting local projects outside the website channel. ⚙️ Template Product Configuration Decision made for Almoxy engineer Aaron to push template products into the client’s instance and configure detailed pricing and dropdown options. 🛠️ Product Types Discussion Discussion on product types offered including doors, cabinets, and drawer boxes, with a commitment to incorporate accurate pricing. 💳 Payment Gateway Options Client prefers Authorize.net despite limitations; Almoxy to support Authorize.net setup. 📅 Active Invoices and Test Transactions Client has active invoices and work orders to process before year-end and wants to run test transactions. 🧾 Tax Rates and Shop Location Setup Setup of tax rates and default shop location for sales tax calculations discussed and partially configured. 👥 User Account Training Training on user account creation and permission assignment provided, indicating multiple users will be added. 📧 Next Steps Agreement Agreement to send client an email with a detailed list of pricing and product information needed for configuration. 🔄 Communication Challenges Concerns raised about communication challenges with the client, highlighting difficulties in understanding. ✉️ Need for Detailed Communication Acknowledgement that most communications may need to be handled via detailed emails or through other staff members. Action Items Bobby Lewis Coordinate with engineer Aaron to push the product templates into the client’s Almoxy instance Adjust payment gateway settings to use Authorize.net as preferred by client Send Dennis an email outlining all required product details and pricing information needed for configuration and implementation Josh Baker Guide Dennis through the implementation process and product catalog setup after templates are installed Assist in configuring financial settings, including tax presence and user permissions setup during the meeting and follow-ups Email client a detailed list requesting square foot pricing and product cost data to finalize pricing formulas Dennis Provide product cost data including pricing for doors, drawer boxes, finishes, and hardware to support template customization and proper pricing Obtain Authorize.net login credentials and transaction key from the payment processor team to enable payment gateway connection in Almoxy Set up user accounts and permissions for other team members who will access the Almoxy platform

Kick-off call with Bobby and Dennis 12/12/25 Intro Relationship between CS and implementation Build type (Hybrid/ Asynch)? Demo information Minnesota End customer: commercial builders and residential Product offering: framed and frameless cabinets Template catalog Cabinet Vision, QBO Resource allocation (eventually) REFERENCE MATERIAL

Title: Allmoxy Demo - : CRYSTAL CABINET WORKS Date: Wed Dec 03 2025 15:15:00 GMT-0500 (Eastern Standard Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, crystalcabinetworks@hotmail.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 42 mins Overview - Onboarding Ahead: Crystal Capital Works to start with $417/month subscription for expected $500,000 annual volume. - Setup Fee: Estimated setup fee of $5,000 to $10,000 for customization and integration. - Demo Instance: Custom demo instance and Cabinet Vision catalog will be provided for initial setup review. - Order Management: Software includes order management, inventory control, and customer self-service features. - Automated Communication: Automated email triggers will improve customer interactions and reduce manual follow-ups. - Timeline for Launch: System aimed to go live for order processing this winter after signing agreement. Notes 🔌 Video Connection Issues Discussed video connection issues at the start but proceeded without video successfully. 📦 Crystal Cabinet Works Overview Crystal Cabinet Works, based in Minnesota, seeks streamlined software for inventory, scheduling, invoicing, and order management, replacing Microsoft Business Electric 5 due to complexity. Company serves both commercial construction and private residential customers with framed and frameless cabinets, cabinet care and cleaning services, and operates a factory in Louisiana. 🖥️ Allmoxy Customer Portal Features Bobby Lewis explained Allmoxy’s customer portal allowing customers to view order status, outstanding balances, and make payments securely via Stripe integration supporting cards and ACH, emphasizing security and convenience. Discussed alternative payment gateway authorize.net for credit card payments but without stored cards; Stripe preferred for security and stored payment info. 📊 Integration with QuickBooks Online System integrates with QuickBooks Online for seamless syncing of invoices and payments, including automated past due reminders via email triggers. Product configurator allows building custom cabinetry orders with instant pricing; expert consultation needed to define product catalog and pricing. 💰 Tax and Pricing Management Software handles automatic tax calculation based on customer location/zipcode and allows manual override for tax-exempt customers. Supports pricing adjustments per customer or product with discounts or surcharges. Customers can specify shipping method with shipping costs automatically calculated and included in orders. 📋 Order Management Features Order management on owner side includes viewing bids, in-progress and completed jobs, inventory allocation and purchase order creation linked with inventory levels. Bobby Lewis confirmed ability to import Cabinet Vision catalogs into Allmoxy for streamlined integration with existing manufacturing software. 🛠️ Customization and Template Features Software allows template creation and supports customizable dropdown menus for product variations, including construction methods and door types. Partial payments and deposit requirements configurable with automated payment request emails containing payment links triggered by order status or customer tags. 📧 Automated Customer Communications Triggers automate customer communications including order confirmation, payment receipts, and shipping notifications to reduce phone calls. Supports itemized invoicing for services like cabinet cleaning and care, with customizable product attributes and photos. 🎨 Branding and Pricing Model Company logos and branding customizable in the software interface via theme uploads. Pricing model based on monthly subscription variable on annual sales volume; estimated $417/month for $500,000 annual volume with a 12-month minimum, plus a setup fee for engineering services ($5,000-$10,000 range) to build product catalog and customizations. 📝 Agreement Process and Next Steps Agreement process: contract to be sent upon meeting end; instance setup and catalog import initiated on contract signature; demo credentials and access to software to be provided for team review. Client emphasized urgency to implement and ability to make quick payment upon receiving contract; expressed strong positive impression of software ease of use and potential for business growth. Action Items Bobby Lewis Send subscription agreement for $417/month based on $500,000 annual volume Provide demo access with sample login credentials for review Import Cabinet Vision catalog matching Crystal Cabinet Works' products into Allmoxy instance Assist in defining dropdown options and pricing customization after initial setup Guide and support team during software onboarding and setup process AA / Crystal Cabinet Works Review Allmoxy demo and template catalog provided Sign subscription agreement promptly to initiate setup and contract process Coordinate internal team to evaluate software features and product catalog fit Make payment upon receipt of contract to expedite implementation Reach out via email with any further questions during setup phase

engagements.title: Call with Cedar Creek Woodworks
engagements.body:
CS: Onboarding Initial Call
(Use knowledge from the sales notes) Tell me a bit more about your company and what led you to Allmoxy.

  • Notes:

    Cabinet Vision

What does full implementation look like for your company? Identify if the customer plans to launch in phases (ie phase 1 is online ordering, phase 2 is exporting to machines, etc) or full implementation to launch (this method takes longer, so it's good to set a healthy expectation that a full implementation in 2-3 weeks is optimistic, for example)

  • Notes:

    Start simple

    • The primary goal is to have customers be able to enter orders. (quoting)

    • Stock cabinets with W, H, & D adjustments

    • Base, wall, tall, and corner cabinets.

    • Eventually do built-ins and lockers

    • Wants to be able to sell raw materials.

    • Potentially be able to use inventory

    • Export to cabinet vision

    Building own catalog




What are your challenges and roadblocks? Any concerns that we can help with?

  • Notes:

    • Money to build a catalog

    • Time to work on the project

    • Multiple other projects going at the time.

Who are the main points of contact or agencies we will be working with? (financial, production, office management, catalog-building etc)

  • Notes:

    Sean will be the only one.

When would you like to be launched?
  • 09/06/2023

Meeting with Sarah Pierce April 16, 2026 2:00 PM MDT 11 mins rolandsells@gmail.com sarah@allmoxy.com roland@quickkitchens.ca View Meeting Recording Technical Connectivity Issues: Persistent audio failures disrupted communication, preventing any productive discussion or agenda progress. Decision to Switch Platforms: The team decided to switch to Zoom due to ongoing technical difficulties, highlighting a need for better tools. No Business Outcomes: No agenda topics, decisions, or actionable insights were discussed during the meeting due to communication failures. Improvement Needs: The need for reliable conferencing tools and pre-meeting technical checks was emphasized to avoid disruptions. Adaptability Shown: Participants showed readiness to switch platforms quickly, indicating flexibility despite significant technical setbacks. No agenda topics were discussed or decisions made throughout the meeting duration (00:00–02:16) The transcript contains only attempts to establish audio connectivity, with no substantive dialogue No metrics, product updates, strategies, or timelines were mentioned or reviewed No assignments or next steps were formally documented beyond the platform change suggestion The meeting was effectively aborted before any substantive content could be shared (02:16) The shift to Zoom was the only planned follow-up action mentioned The failure highlights the critical importance of stable communication setups for virtual meetings This disruption likely delayed project progress and required rescheduling or re-planning Process and System Improvements The need for robust and tested communication technology was implicitly highlighted. Technical failures underscored the requirement for reliable conferencing tools to support meetings (00:00) Current platform showed layout and audio control issues that impaired usability Switching to Zoom was suggested as a contingency to avoid similar disruptions Establishing pre-meeting technical checks could prevent these issues in the future Clear protocols for troubleshooting and backup plans should be implemented No operational or process improvements were discussed during the session due to lack of productive dialogue (00:00–02:16) The focus remained solely on resolving audio problems rather than advancing workflow or systems The incident signals a need to revisit meeting setup procedures for better reliability Speaker Insights and Interaction Speakers mainly communicated about audio issues, showing willingness to adapt by changing platforms. Roland Sells took a lead role in diagnosing audio problems and suggesting solutions (00:48, 02:09) He acknowledged the technical limitations and encouraged moving to an alternative platform His responsiveness aimed to salvage the meeting despite setbacks Sarah Pierce and others repeatedly tested audio connection with no success (00:39, 00:52) Their persistence showed intent to proceed but was thwarted by technical constraints The interaction remained limited to troubleshooting without advancing business topics The team demonstrated readiness to switch to Zoom promptly (02:13) This quick adjustment reflects adaptability to technical challenges However, it also points to prior lack of platform validation before the meeting Overall Meeting Summary This session was entirely consumed by technical issues, preventing any meaningful business discussion or decisions. The team decided to abandon the current platform and attempt reconvening on Zoom. This experience highlights the critical need for reliable communication tools and pre-meeting technical checks to ensure productive virtual collaboration. Action Items Sarah Pierce Await Zoom invitation and prepare to join the new meeting platform where communication problems are expected to be resolved. (02:13) Roland Sells Explore alternative meeting platforms, specifically trying Zoom, and send an invitation to participants. Meeting with Sarah Pierce March 19, 2026 1:00 PM MDT 30 mins rolandsells@gmail.com sarah@allmoxy.com roland@quickkitchens.ca View Meeting Recording Product Catalog Structuring: Nesting categories for commercial clients will aid Roland in bidding for a $200,000 cabinet job. Retail Experience Simplification: Reducing options for retail clients to 5-6 colors aims to prevent order abandonment and confusion. Retail Site Launch Timeline: Targeting April 1st for a phased launch that will take several months to develop. Automated Payment Integration: Customers can pay online directly to Roland’s bank, streamlining order processing and minimizing manual management. Customization and Guidance: Branding adjustments require image resizing; detailed tutorials for retail clients will be developed by Roland. Sales and Partnership Strategy: Roland will focus on reselling without manufacturing, planning to invest sales revenue back into the platform's development. Product Catalog Structuring The focus is on organizing the product categories to improve commercial client bidding and prepare for retail sales. Folder and Category Setup for Commercial Use was prioritized to help Roland bid on a $200,000 cabinet job effectively (06:35) Sarah is creating nested categories reflecting Diamond CNC’s structure, including doors, full cabinet lines, hardware, accessories, and drawer boxes. This cleanup will replicate Diamond CNC’s organization to streamline ordering for knowledgeable commercial clients. Roland emphasized the urgency of this task to focus on bidding and commercial order placement. Sarah committed to finishing this within about 30 minutes and notifying Roland upon completion. Separation and Visibility of Multiple Catalogs for different cabinetry manufacturers was discussed to maintain clarity (25:39) Roland wants to keep other manufacturers’ catalogs separate and hidden unless authorized. Sarah explored tagging and catalog exclusivity but found no current system feature to hide B2B catalogs from each other. Roland will explore contacting manufacturers directly to manage orders and quotes independently. This reflects the complexity of handling multi-vendor inventory within Almoxy. Retail Customer Experience Simplification A strategic shift is needed to simplify the product offering for retail clients to enable self-service online ordering. Reducing Product Options for Retail Clients to avoid overwhelming customers and enable more straightforward online sales was identified as critical (07:50) Roland wants to limit choices to about 5-6 colors and fewer design lines compared to Diamond CNC’s extensive options. This simplification aims to prevent customer frustration and reduce order abandonment on the retail-facing site. Sarah highlighted that controlling option visibility is somewhat possible via filtering features but is limited by Diamond CNC’s catalog structure. Full custom catalogs for end consumers would require significant engineering effort and cannot be done quickly. Timeline for Retail Site Launch is targeted for April 1st, with a realistic expectation of a multi-month development process (09:14) Roland acknowledges that launching a customer-friendly retail platform will take several months of work. He plans to start with a simpler version and improve the system over time, similar to iterative product releases. Sarah confirmed the complexity and advised that building a full end-user catalog would exceed a two-week timeframe. This phased approach balances go-to-market speed with future platform enhancements. Order Processing and Payment Integration The system supports self-service ordering and secure payments directly to Roland’s bank account. Automated Payment Handling is integrated with Almoxy’s system to allow customers to pay via Visa or other methods online (18:24) Roland confirmed that payments go directly to his bank, simplifying order processing. Sarah validated that customers can create accounts, design kitchens, and place orders autonomously. This setup supports Roland’s goal of minimizing manual order management. It aligns with his desire to focus on sales rather than production or order handling. Customization and Branding Features Branding customization and user interface adjustments are available but require some image resizing and configuration. Site Logo and Side Image Placement were reviewed with Sarah showing how images appear on login and main pages (20:49) Roland uploaded logos and images but did not immediately see them on the site due to typical placement on the login page. Sarah explained image size requirements (1200x1800 pixels) and how header customization works. Roland considered removing the side image due to its current color scheme. This customization helps align the platform visually with Roland’s brand identity. Tutorials and User Guidance are partly supported through tooltips but require additional effort for detailed retail customer education (15:21) Almoxy uses Appcues for system-wide tooltips, but product-specific explainers must be added by catalog owners, like Mike. Roland plans to create his own video tutorials and educational content based on his cabinet-making expertise. This approach will help retail customers navigate complex options and reduce confusion. It also complements the simplified product offering to improve user experience. Strategic Sales and Partnership Approach Roland aims to leverage existing relationships and focus on sales while respecting supplier constraints. Partnership with Diamond CNC remains central, with Roland acting as a reseller driving sales without manufacturing cabinets himself (12:29) He emphasized his preference to outsource cabinet production and concentrate on selling. Roland respects Mike’s established catalog and does not want to disrupt his business model. This dynamic shapes the scope of customization and product offering modifications. Roland’s sales focus drives the need for streamlined product visibility and easy ordering. Future Platform Development Budgeting will be incremental, starting with a functional site and reinvesting sales revenue into improvements (19:39) Roland plans to launch a basic version early, then allocate funds to enhance the sales platform based on generated revenue. This phased investment mirrors common product launch strategies. Sarah agreed this approach is practical given the current system capabilities and market needs. It balances cost control with the ambition to build a robust retail sales channel over time. Action Items Sarah Pierce Recreate and clean up Diamond CNC’s categories and folder structure in Almoxy for Roland to enable easier bidding. (06:08) Obtain and update product images from Diamond CNC corresponding to the new folder setup. (20:15) Notify Roland via email when folder restructuring and updates are completed, expected within about 30 minutes from meeting end. (29:07) Roland Donald Sells Film and produce tutorial videos to help retail clients understand cabinet ordering and product options on the Almoxy platform. (16:29) Review the updated category/folder structure once Sarah completes the cleanup and confirm if it meets bidding needs. (20:33) Investigate options to manage multiple cabinetry product catalogs separately or reach out directly to other manufacturers as needed to avoid catalog overlap. (25:24) Consider resizing and adjusting branding images (site logo, side image) based on guidance for proper visual presentation on Almoxy login and header screens. (20:40) Plan phased rollout of retail online sales platform targeting April 1 launch with the intent to improve functionality over time. (09:14) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KM365C6SN6BP7HC9F3RC0H16 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

Allmoxy Kick Off Call March 17, 2026 4:00 PM MDT 22 mins bobby@allmoxy.com sarah@allmoxy.com madison@allmoxy.com roland@quickkitchens.ca View Meeting Recording Roland’s Sales Role Confirmed: He will resell all of Diamond CNC's products with an 18% margin goal. Platform Customization Options: Roland can import products and build custom ones, focusing on initial product library learning. Technical Issues Identified: Misconfigured platform instance led to pulling incorrect data; reset planned for resolution. Support Commitment: Sarah will provide documentation and follow-up to ensure smooth onboarding and address technical issues. Quick Import Post-Fix: Once resolved, importing Diamond CNC's catalog will take about five minutes to enable selling. User Experience Challenges: Roland faced usability struggles with screen sharing and confusing data in the platform interface. B2B Sales Setup and Product Integration The meeting focused on onboarding Roland Donald Sells as a new reseller for Diamond CNC's products through a B2B platform. Roland’s Sales Role and Product Scope Confirmed (07:36) Roland is a new sales representative focusing on cabinet sales and spinning up a website for that. He plans to resell all of Diamond CNC’s products initially through the platform. Roland aims for an 18% margin, which translates to approximately a 26% markup on product costs. No formal pricing agreement yet exists with Mike from Diamond CNC, so pricing strategy is still flexible. Platform Capabilities and Customization Options Explained (10:52) Sarah clarified that as a reseller, Roland can import Diamond CNC’s product catalog but also add products from other suppliers or build custom products. Roland expressed interest in importing Diamond CNC’s product library first and learning the platform through hands-on experience. Building custom products will require additional learning and potential communication with Mike for ordering specifics. Technical Challenges Identified with Product Setup (11:50) Roland struggled with platform features like configuring inch-and-a-half gables and setting up company branding and logos. Sarah offered assistance and documentation to help him get started with branding and B2B settings. The team acknowledged that product configuration is complex, but internal engineers with cabinet-building expertise are available to assist with custom product frameworks. B2B Account and Pricing Tier Impact Highlighted (14:00) Bobby informed Roland that moving into custom product creation would change his subscription type and potentially increase costs based on volume tiers. Roland confirmed he does not plan to manufacture cabinets himself, only resell, which keeps his account in the B2B reseller tier. Technical Issues and Next Steps for Platform Access The session encountered technical issues with Roland’s platform instance pulling incorrect data, delaying product setup. Instance Misconfiguration Causing Data Pull Issues (16:00) Sarah discovered Roland was connected to a template/test environment instead of the correct production instance. This caused the system to pull non-customer test data instead of Diamond CNC’s actual supplier data. Bobby confirmed this was an internal setup issue on their side, not Roland’s fault. Reset and Reconfiguration Planned to Fix Instance (18:00) Sarah and Bobby planned to log out, reset Roland’s instance by cloning from an empty master template, and attempt to sync correctly. If the reset does not resolve the issue, developer intervention will be required, delaying setup beyond the meeting. Roland agreed to keep the call open and rejoin once the issue is fixed. Follow-Up Support and Scheduling Next Meeting (19:50) Sarah committed to reporting the issue to developers with a goal to fix it quickly. She will follow up by email with documentation on branding and company setup to prepare Roland in the meantime. A follow-up meeting will be scheduled, ideally for the next morning, to complete product import and setup once the instance is fixed. Quick Product Catalog Import Expected After Fix (20:20) Bobby highlighted that once the instance issue is resolved, importing Diamond CNC’s catalog will take about five minutes. This step will enable Roland to start selling products promptly after technical issues are cleared. Customer Success and Support Strategy The discussion emphasized hands-on support and gradual onboarding to ensure Roland can independently manage the platform. Sarah’s Role as Customer Success Lead and Support Resource (08:19) Sarah positioned herself as the main contact for guiding Roland through the B2B structure and onboarding process. She reassured Roland that the team would assist with complex product building and customization. Learning-by-Doing Approach Encouraged (11:20) Roland prefers learning the platform through trial and error, supported by documentation and expert help. Sarah agreed this approach fits well with the available training materials and on-demand engineering support. Documentation and Training Materials to Be Provided (13:30) Sarah will send Roland materials covering branding setup, contact management, and product configuration basics. This will allow Roland to progress independently while waiting for the technical issue to be resolved. Engineering Support for Complex Product Builds (12:20) The team’s engineers have several years of cabinet-building experience and can accelerate custom catalog creation. This resource is available if Roland decides to expand beyond standard product import. Platform Usability and User Experience Challenges The session revealed some usability hurdles that Roland faced with the software interface and screen sharing. Screen Sharing Technical Difficulties Slowed Progress (13:50) Roland initially struggled to share his screen, causing delays in live walkthroughs. Sarah and Bobby provided step-by-step guidance to enable screen sharing successfully. User Interface Confusion Around B2B Settings (15:58) Sarah directed Roland to locate the B2B settings gear icon to access supplier logos and product options. Despite this, the platform showed unexpected template data, causing confusion. Instance Environment Confusion Impacted User Confidence (16:30) Seeing test environment data instead of live production information created uncertainty for Roland. Clear communication from Sarah and Bobby helped maintain trust and set expectations for resolution. Recognition of Need for Improved Instance Management (19:00) The team acknowledged that instance cloning and environment setup could be streamlined to avoid such issues. Bobby expressed openness to adjusting processes if current methods prove unreliable. Action Items Roland Donald Log out and assist with resetting the Allmoxy instance as directed. (18:42) Sarah Pierce Reset Roland’s instance by cloning from the master empty template for accurate product and supplier data. (19:05) Report technical issues to the developers to fix the environment so Diamond CNC products appear correctly. (19:55) Send Roland documentation on setting up companies, contacts, and branding while waiting for issue resolution. (20:25) Schedule a follow-up call after developer fixes, aiming for the next day. (20:25) Bobby Lewis Provide Sarah with the pricing matrix for volume tier subscription changes related to custom product manufacturing. (14:00) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KKSVF21QE57SE3PSFD607XSG 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:
