Quick Kitchens CA Handoff Report from Head of Sales to Madison 1. Account Snapshot Company: Quick Kitchens CA (quickkitchens.ca Ltd.) HubSpot Company ID: 52560026544 Website / Domain: quickktchens.ca (typo in record; brand is “Quick Kitchens CA”) Allmoxy Instance: https://quickkitchens.allmoxy.com (B2B portal for Roland) Lifecycle Stage: Customer Owner (to date): Bobby Lewis – Head of Sales Associated deals: 1 (customer total revenue recorded: $200 so far) Associated contacts: 1 primary contact (Roland; details below) Last sales activity: 2026‑03‑04 Last logged contact & note: 2026‑03‑14 (Instance‑ready email + kickoff plan) From my perspective, this is a very new, high‑potential B2B reseller account that has just converted from evaluation to paid and is entering onboarding. The relationship is positive and engaged, but execution and time‑to‑value will depend heavily on how smoothly you (Madison) run onboarding and coordinate with Mike’s supplier catalog side. 2. Key Contacts & Roles Primary Contact Name: Roland (full name in contact record; primary stakeholder) Email: rolandsells@gmail.com Role: Owner of Quick Kitchens CA’s new online cabinetry channel Also acts as a sales rep for Mike’s commercial accounts (dual role: reseller + rep) Context: Small team (form data: 1–5 employees, $1.5M–$4.5M annual revenue) Already familiar with Allmoxy through existing user (form: “What brings you to Allmoxy? – Allmoxy User (A company that's already using allmoxy)”). He’s not coming in cold; expectations are relatively high because he’s seen Allmoxy in action elsewhere. Very hands‑on, entrepreneurial, and comfortable with tooling (willing to own website/landing page creation and portal configuration with guidance). Other Stakeholders Mike(Diamond CNC). Not a stakeholder (not in our CRM as a contact for this company, but central to the model): Owns the source catalog that Roland will resell. Our system pulls products, pricing, and options from Mike’s Allmoxy instance to Roland’s. Needs to approve Roland as a reseller in his instance, and his shipping/pricing structures are the base for Roland’s margin. For your work, Roland is the operational owner you’ll work with day‑to‑day. Mike is an external dependency we coordinate around for catalog and shipping logic. 3. Relationship & Interaction History (Chronological Overview) 3.1 Discovery & Demo (Early March 2026) 2026‑03‑05 – Allmoxy Demo (Zoom meeting) Meeting: “Allmoxy Demo – : quickkitchens.ca Ltd.” (35 minutes, recorded via Fireflies). Participants: me (Bobby), Roland. Focus: Designing a B2B online sales platform where Roland resells Mike’s cabinetry catalog under the Quick Kitchens brand. Key outcomes from the demo (from my meeting note): Business Model & Catalog Setup Roland will resell cabinetry products online using Mike’s catalog, not maintain his own from scratch. Customers visit a Quick Kitchens branded portal and order products; orders flow automatically into Mike’s system. This eliminates double entry and lets Roland go to market quickly. Customer Journey End customers (mostly homeowners) create accounts before ordering. They upload existing cabinet drawings; Roland is not offering drawing or design services. System supports: Single or multiple item orders Optional credit limits and B2B terms (if he wants to expand beyond homeowners later). Roland can limit which products/options are visible on his instance to keep the catalog simple for end customers. Payments & Cash Flow Payments handled via integrated Stripe: Credit cards and bank transfers (similar to Canadian e‑transfers). Processing fee: 2.9% per credit card transaction. Funds go directly to Roland’s bank account. Offline payments like checks are supported as manual options. Shipping & Fulfillment Mike ships directly to Roland’s customers. Shipping costs will be calculated via configurable B2B shipping formulas, based on Mike’s rates plus Roland’s markup. We stressed that shipping cost logic must be aligned with Mike’s team to avoid margin surprises. Website / Front‑End Experience The Allmoxy side provides the portal (quickkitchens.allmoxy.com) — effectively the “storefront back end”. We recommended a simple landing page to: Explain the Quick Kitchens brand and offer. Warm customers before they enter the ordering experience. I showed Roland the Lovable AI website builder as a low‑cost, fast way to spin up a landing page: Uses his logos/brand colors. Can include a prominent “Order Online” button linking to the Allmoxy portal. ~$20 cost, he can manage it himself. Subscription & Instance Subscription fee: $199 USD / month. Upon signing, we: Create a custom instance for Quick Kitchens. Connect to Mike’s product catalog and apply Roland’s markup. A customer success representative (you/CS) will: Guide him through catalog connection. Help set pricing and shipping formulas. Train him on day‑to‑day use. Strategic Value Roland avoids building a catalog from scratch; time‑to‑market is short. This is one of the simplest, highest‑return setups we see: supplier already on Allmoxy, reseller running a lean online sales operation. Roland also keeps his role as rep for Mike’s commercial accounts, so this system complements his existing activity instead of replacing it. Meeting Action Items (from the note) For Roland: Set up hosting & build a landing page using AI builder or equivalent. Review and sign the subscription agreement I sent. Confirm markup structure over Mike’s base pricing. For Allmoxy (Bobby → CS team): Send subscription agreement and documentation. After agreement: Create the Quick Kitchens instance. Integrate Mike’s product catalog as a reseller feed. Assign a customer success rep (you) to lead onboarding. Support Roland with domain DNS and linking: His quickktchens.ca domain pointing to landing page + portal. Most of these are now in motion (see next section). 3.2 Conversion & Early Onboarding (Mid March 2026) Subscription & Instance Creation Email – “Sign Up” (2026‑03‑12) I emailed Roland confirming receipt of the signed subscription agreement. Key content: I acknowledged the agreement coming through and explained that putting a card on file triggers instance creation and internal processes. I nudged him to revisit the document link he signed to complete payment details and finalize setup. Tone: excited, supportive, and focused on getting to instance creation quickly. Email – “Instance Ready” (2026‑03‑14) This is the critical onboarding transition: I confirmed that we created his instance and are configuring it based on previous conversations. Shared portal login: URL: https://quickkitchens.allmoxy.com/public/login/ Username: rolandsells@gmail.com Temporary password: password123 (asked him to change immediately in his profile). I told him we’re ready for a kickoff call with Customer Success (you & Sarah) and gave him a meeting link to book: Suggested he invite anyone he wants involved, especially the main project point‑person. I set expectations that we’d create a specific plan to get his instance ready to introduce to his customers. At this point, in CRM: Company lifecycle is set to Customer. Total revenue shows $200 (likely initial charge; the main opportunity is in future B2B transaction volume). There is one associated deal and no open deals (hs_num_open_deals = 0), indicating initial sale is closed and we’re moving into adoption/expansion. 4. Product & Implementation Scope 4.1 Current Scope Product: Allmoxy B2B online ordering portal, reseller model. Instance: quickkitchens.allmoxy.com Initial configuration focus: Connect to Mike’s existing catalog. Implement Roland’s markup over Mike’s base pricing. Set visibility rules so Roland exposes only the subset of products/options appropriate for his end customers. Configure Stripe with Roland’s banking details. Define shipping formulas consistent with Mike’s policies and Roland’s margins. 4.2 Planned Customer Experience Prospective customer hits Roland’s landing page (non‑Allmoxy front‑end he will create). They click “Order Online” or similar CTA and are taken to the Allmoxy portal. They create an account and upload drawings. Orders flow to Roland’s Allmoxy instance → then replicate into Mike’s system after Roland’s verification. Payments are captured via Stripe (or recorded offline for checks). Mike ships directly to the end customer. Your onboarding work should make this flow feel cohesive and low‑friction. 5. Commercials & Business Potential Subscription: $199 USD/month (standard for this configuration). Recorded revenue so far: $200 (likely initial billing). Deal count: 1 closed/won; no other open deals. Upside: The value here is not in subscription ARPU alone but in: Volume of cabinetry through the portal. Roland’s ability to scale quickly without adding headcount. If onboarding goes well, there is: Potential to expand catalog coverage. Introduce B2B account features (credit terms, more complex pricing). Eventually add services or additional Allmoxy modules as his operation grows. 6. Sentiment, Risks, and Opportunities 6.1 Sentiment From all interactions and recorded notes: Roland is excited and optimistic: He reacted positively to seeing how fast we can spin up a reseller instance. He liked the idea that most heavy lifting (catalog, pricing) is already done on Mike’s side. No negative sentiment logged; no tickets or escalations yet. Tone from my emails is intentionally encouraging: “We are really excited to work with you… Thank you again for trusting us. Let’s get going!!” While that’s my wording, Roland’s engagement (signing quickly, moving into setup) confirms he is motivated to move fast. 6.2 Key Risks Landing Page & Domain Stall Roland needs to: Choose/confirm a hosting approach. Build or publish the landing page. Configure quickktchens.ca DNS correctly. If this drags, the portal exists but has no traffic, and enthusiasm may fade. Shipping & Pricing Misalignment Shipping formulas require coordination with Mike: If shipping costs are misestimated, Roland’s margins can be wiped out, or customers see unexpected delivery charges. Similarly, if markup is not carefully thought through, he could be uncompetitive or unprofitable. Roland’s Bandwidth He’s a small team (1–5 people) plus rep duties for Mike. If onboarding feels heavy or confusing, he may delay configuration work and underutilize the system. Expectation Management (Existing Allmoxy Exposure) Because he’s already seen Allmoxy in another company, he may expect: High polish from day one. Very quick ROI. If we don’t deliver a “quick win,” sentiment could flip. 6.3 Opportunities Fast Time‑to‑Value Catalog already exists; we’re not starting from zero. If you move quickly with him on: Stripe Shipping formulas Basic page content He can realistically start taking online orders very soon. Case Study Material If this succeeds, it’s an ideal case study: Allmoxy user → layered reseller model → new revenue stream, minimal overhead. We could later showcase this to other shops with similar supplier relationships. Expansion Over time: Advanced B2B features (credit, purchase order workflows). Possible multi‑catalog setup (Roland reselling products from multiple suppliers). Adding internal production management if he ever starts fabricating in‑house. 7. Recommended Next Steps for Madison Here’s what I recommend you focus on in your first 30–45 days as the new owner of this account. 7.1 Immediate (Next 1–2 Weeks) Kickoff Call Use the meeting link I provided in the “Instance Ready” email (or send a fresh invite if he hasn’t booked). Objectives: Align on go‑live target date. Confirm roles (who else, if anyone, will help him internally). Re‑confirm the customer journey in his own words: How he wants customers to find the site. What he wants them to do before they place an order. Discuss success criteria: e.g., “X orders/month by [date]” or “first paying order within Y weeks.” Checklist Review with Roland Walk through a lightweight launch checklist, including: Stripe account connection. DNS and domain pointing (quickktchens.ca → landing page + portal link). User roles (who besides Roland needs admin access). Basic email templates / branding in the portal. Make everything feel concrete and finite, not overwhelming. Instance Health Check Confirm: Quick Kitchens instance is accessible and branded correctly. Roland’s login works and password has been changed. Mike’s catalog integration status (if already wired up by Services/CS, just validate with Roland). 7.2 Short‑Term (Weeks 2–4) Shipping & Pricing Workshop Facilitate a working session to: Review Mike’s shipping rules. Implement B2B shipping formulas in Roland’s instance. Validate that he understands how price → markup → margin works. Where needed, coordinate a joint call with Mike’s team to avoid assumptions. Portal Walkthrough & Training Focus on: Approving & managing incoming orders. Handling payments (Stripe capture, refunds, manual payments). Managing customers (accounts, credit limits if he uses them). Basic reporting so he can see how his channel is performing. Launch Milestone Aim to reach a clear milestone like: “First real customer order placed end‑to‑end through the portal.” Celebrate that with him; it’s a psychological anchor that Allmoxy is “live” and working. 7.3 Medium‑Term (Post‑launch, 1–3 Months) Performance Review Once he has order volume: Review order data, average order size, payment behavior. Explore tweaks to markup, promotions, or shipping to improve conversion. Opportunities for Expansion Based on his results: Offer to help him add: New product lines from Mike. Additional suppliers. More sophisticated B2B features. Discuss whether he wants to use more Allmoxy features beyond the reseller front end. 8. What You Should Watch Closely Is Roland progressing on his landing page and domain? If not, consider giving him: Example content. A pre‑built copy deck. Clear “good enough” standards so he doesn’t overthink it. Has he actually logged into the instance and changed his password? Quick verification during kickoff will confirm if he has engaged. Are there any early frictions with Stripe or shipping formulas? These are the most likely blockers to going live; prioritize unblocking them. Is he still excited after the first month? If activity drops, re‑center the conversation on his original goal: build an online sales engine without building a full manufacturing operation. 9. Summary for You as New Owner Quick Kitchens CA is a fresh, high‑intent reseller customer built around a strong existing supplier (Mike) and a motivated operator (Roland). We have: A live instance (quickkitchens.allmoxy.com). A signed subscription and first revenue in the system. A clear, documented B2B storefront model. Positive momentum from our early interactions. Your success on this account will hinge on: Running a smooth, confidence‑building onboarding. Turning the portal from “configured” into “actively selling”. Helping Roland hit his first few live orders quickly so the value of Allmoxy is obvious. If you need deeper context, the Fireflies meeting recording and note linked in the company’s associated engagements provide a detailed play‑by‑play of the demo and expectations we set.

Allmoxy Demo - : quickkitchens.ca Ltd. March 5, 2026 3:45 PM MST 35 mins bobby@allmoxy.com rolandsells@gmail.com View Meeting Recording B2B Online Sales Platform Setup The meeting focused on enabling Roland to resell cabinetry products online using Mike's existing catalog through a B2B system with seamless order and payment integration. Launch of Reseller Online Store Using Mike’s Catalog (08:26) Roland plans to sell cabinetry products online by subscribing to Mike’s product catalog within Bobby’s system. Customers will browse and order products directly from Roland’s branded portal, which automatically syncs prices and options from Mike’s catalog. Orders verified by Roland are instantly created in Mike’s system, eliminating double entry and streamlining fulfillment. Bobby emphasized this setup requires minimal configuration since Mike maintains product data and pricing. Customer Journey and Account Creation Process (12:56) End customers, mostly homeowners, will create accounts on Roland’s portal before ordering. Customers upload existing cabinet drawings, as Roland will not provide drawing services. The system handles both single and multiple item orders, with options for credit limits and B2B terms if needed. Roland can limit product options shown to customers by configuring settings on his instance. Order Verification and Payment Integration (16:52) Roland receives orders in his portal and verifies them before submission to Mike’s system. Payments are collected through an integrated Stripe setup, supporting credit cards and bank transfers similar to Canadian e-transfers. Payment processing fees are 2.9% per credit card transaction, with funds deposited directly into Roland’s bank account. Offline payments like checks are also supported, providing flexibility for customers. Shipping and Fulfillment Coordination (20:21) Mike will ship products directly to Roland’s customers. Shipping costs will be calculated via configurable B2B shipping formulas based on Mike’s shipping rates plus any markup. Bobby highlighted the importance of coordinating shipping cost formulas with Mike’s team to ensure accuracy and profitability. Website and Landing Page Development Roland needs a landing page to present his brand and direct customers to the ordering system, with AI tools recommended for easy creation. Website Options and Domain Integration (22:27) Roland does not need a fully hosted website; the system can provide a branded portal at quickkitchens.allmoxy.com as a standalone store. However, a front-end landing page is recommended to explain services and build trust before customers order. This landing page can link directly to the ordering portal via an “Order Online” button. AI-Powered Website Builder Recommendation (24:19) Bobby demonstrated an AI tool called Lovable that quickly generates a professional landing page using provided logos and brand colors. The AI builder can add custom buttons linking to Roland’s ordering portal automatically. This tool costs about $20 and allows Roland to create and publish his landing page himself, saving development costs. Domain Setup and Hosting (29:17) Roland will need to configure his domain registrar (CanSpace) to point to the landing page and ordering portal. Bobby advised keeping hosting plans simple and basic since the system is lightweight. The team can start integrating catalogs and pricing while Roland works on the landing page. Subscription, Pricing, and Onboarding Process The system uses a subscription model with clear pricing, markup control, and a guided onboarding process involving key stakeholders. Subscription Cost and Setup (17:30) The monthly subscription fee for the system is $199 USD. Upon agreement signing and payment setup, Bobby’s team creates a custom instance for Roland (e.g., company.allmoxy.com). The instance syncs Mike’s selected products with Roland’s markup automatically applied. Markup Control and Product Approval (33:38) Roland determines his selling prices by adding a markup to Mike’s base prices. Mike must approve Roland as a reseller to allow product access in his system. A customer success representative guides Roland through the approval and setup process. Customer Success and Documentation Delivery (32:44) Bobby is sending the subscription agreement and documentation immediately to kickstart onboarding. A customer success agent will be assigned to assist with connecting to Mike’s catalog and training on system usage. Roland plans to finalize hosting today and start building his landing page to align with onboarding milestones. Strategic Business Impact and Growth Potential This integration aims to create a scalable online sales channel leveraging existing supplier infrastructure, reducing operational overhead for Roland. Streamlined Sales Channel with Minimal Catalog Maintenance (10:56) By leveraging Mike’s product catalog and pricing, Roland avoids the time-consuming task of building and maintaining a product database. This model allows quick deployment and faster time to revenue, critical for Roland’s business growth. Bobby described this approach as one of the easiest setups with a high potential return. Dual Role as Reseller and Sales Rep (10:17) Roland will act both as a reseller through the online portal and as a sales rep handling Mike’s commercial accounts. This dual approach increases sales opportunities and diversifies revenue streams. Leveraging the digital platform complements Roland’s existing rep work, broadening market reach. Competitive Advantage via Seamless Integration and Payment Flexibility (31:32) Integrated Stripe payments simplify customer transactions and fund flow, improving cash flow and user experience. Support for multiple payment types, including credit cards and bank transfers, meets diverse customer preferences. The ability to customize shipping and pricing formulas ensures Roland can remain competitive while protecting margins. Action Items Roland Donald Sells Set up hosting platform and start building a landing page using AI website builder or other means. (26:40) Review and sign subscription agreement sent by Bobby Lewis to initiate Allmoxy service setup. (32:49) Confirm markup pricing over Mike’s product costs for setting up sales pricing. (33:38) Bobby Lewis Send subscription agreement and documentation to Roland for review and signature. (32:49) Upon agreement, create B2B instance for Roland’s Quick Kitchens, integrate Mike’s product catalog, and assign a customer success representative to assist with onboarding. (32:49) Provide support for domain configuration and connection to Roland’s AI-generated landing page and Allmoxy subdomain store. (29:20) Meeting Recording: https://app.fireflies.ai/view/01KJX6N9GVC8SGY9TDDBW0MY1Q 📎 Time markers in this document can be used to navigate to specific points in the meeting recording Processed by:

engagements.title: Call with Ethan Nielsen
engagements.body:
Ethan called me to inform me that they will no longer be moving forward with Allmoxy. It is nothing personal and is not because we have done things incorrectly, etc. just with the future and direction of their company manufacturing overseas, etc. it doesn't make financial sense for them to continue to utilize the system.


They are incredibly grateful for their Allmoxy experience and have nothing but great things to say about their time here.


I told Ethan he would want to fill out the cancellation form before the 20th to ensure we do not charge him an additional month.


I sent him an email with the cancellation form and also added this note to the latest HS note thread.

engagements.title: Call with Juan Perez
engagements.body: Follow up on 2020

Title: Allmoxy Demo - The Premier Hub Date: Thu Apr 03 2025 17:15:00 GMT-0400 (Eastern Daylight Time) Participants: bobby@allmoxy.com, cheneil@allmoxy.com, juan@thepremierhub.com, nathalia@thepremierhub.com Transcript: See full transcript Audio: Listen to audio Duration: 64 mins Overview The Product Demo and Requirements Gathering meeting for The Premier Hub's integration with Allmoxy featured Juan and Natalia from The Premier Hub, alongside Bobby Lewis from Allmoxy, who demonstrated their online sales solution tailored for custom cabinetry. The meeting began with introductions and an overview of The Premier Hub’s business model, currently 70% B2C and 30% B2B, with plans to enhance B2B sales. Key to the discussion was the need for Allmoxy's system to integrate with their existing Mosaic setup while allowing for streamline operations as The Premier Hub prepares to franchise. Bobby showcased Allmoxy's capabilities, including real-time pricing and automatic generation of Mosaic files upon order placement. Despite initial pricing concerns, adjustments were made based on their current sales volume, and a plan for a custom catalog was discussed to ensure operational clarity. Action items were assigned, including sending demo links and catalog templates, as well as a follow-up meeting to explore material options, ensuring both teams are aligned for effective implementation. Notes 🏢 Introductions and Company Background Juan and Natalia from The Premier Hub, a custom cabinetry shop in South Florida Bobby Lewis from Allmoxy demonstrating their online sales solution Juan mentioned they've previously had a meeting with Allmoxy about a year ago Juan explained they're seeking an online sales solution similar to what Allmoxy provides for DOT Cabinets The Premier Hub currently uses Mosaic for cabinet design and manufacturing Juan noted business is currently 70% B2C and 30% B2B, with goal to increase B2B to 45% 🔄 Business Model and Integration Needs Juan explained they need a system that integrates with their existing Mosaic setup The Premier Hub is planning to franchise their business model and partner with a Colorado company Juan described how they compete with Chinese/prefab cabinets by offering American-made custom cabinets Bobby confirmed Allmoxy can integrate with Mosaic, sending data directly to it Juan explained they currently use DOT Cabinets' website to quickly price cabinets Bobby demonstrated how Allmoxy can provide real-time pricing for custom cabinet sizes 💰 Pricing Structure and Implementation Juan questioned how the Mosaic integration works with Allmoxy Bobby explained Allmoxy handles business operations, ordering, payments, and inventory management Bobby explained that Allmoxy can send cabinet specifications to Mosaic for manufacturing Monthly pricing discussed: $1,200/month for $2M annual sales volume Juan indicated this was higher than expected (they pay $382 for Mosaic licenses) Bobby adjusted pricing to $500-550/month based on current monthly sales of $100,000 🛠️ Implementation Details Bobby offered to provide a catalog template that matches with a Mosaic catalog Juan emphasized wanting to start small ('less is more') with limited options Juan wants fewer material options than DOT Cabinets offers to streamline operations Bobby confirmed they can create a custom catalog matching Juan's requirements The integration would allow orders placed online to generate Mosaic files automatically Bobby explained implementation would include formula creation for pricing calculations 📊 Catalog and Product Management Juan asked if Allmoxy needs access to their Mosaic database Bobby clarified he would just provide a product library file that matches Allmoxy's catalog Juan explained his vision for a physical inventory of pre-cut cabinet components to reduce delivery time Bobby agreed the business model is viable and Allmoxy is well-positioned to support it Natalia asked about adding hardware products to the catalog (trash bins, spice racks, etc.) Bobby demonstrated how they could add products and have full control over their catalog 🔗 Final Details and Next Steps Bobby explained The Premier Hub's domain would be tph.allmoxy.com The system would include payment processing via Stripe (2.9% fee) Juan asked about showing credit card fees as separate line items on invoices Bobby will send demo links for Juan and team to explore the platform Bobby mentioned QuickBooks Online integration is coming soon Juan stated his franchise partner may also want to implement Allmoxy for their prefab cabinet lines Action Items Bobby Lewis Send demo link for The Premier Hub team to explore the Allmoxy platform Create and share a Mosaic product catalog file that matches the Allmoxy catalog Send subscription agreement for monthly payment Send pricing matrix showing volume-based pricing tiers Set up a follow-up meeting to discuss material options and dropdowns Juan Review the demo and catalog with the team to confirm it contains what they need Create a list of desired door materials and options for the catalog Connect the franchise partner with Bobby to discuss implementation for their stores Send profit and loss statements from previous year to Bobby for pricing considerations

engagements.title: Follow up on Innovative Cabinetry And Design
engagements.body: Regarding email: Innovative Cabinetry And Design​- Contract Saving Renewal, sent on Monday, August 5, 2024 11:21 AM

Renewal 2024 Things have been going well. Jed finally feels like he has the shop set up that he has worked out the bugs. Now he just needs more customers, plans on hiring someone for sales to help selling the software. I let him know that Carson would be available for training should he decide to have his sales person get super familiar with the Allmoxy system. The biggest allmoxy value add has been getting materials in but then they got busy and sidetracked with some side projects. They are having a hard time going all in on getting pricing correct because of the amount of changes that are happening post importing into cabinetvision. Whether it's the customers fault or whether that's just their flexibility, like they have a lot of flexibility on their end and are not forcing people into what they have to offer. They will pretty much do anything for their customers. So that is probably the main hang-up with going all in. Jed sees the value in clicking a few buttons and sending the invoice and whether its charging a credit card or whatever, because he spends about 4 hours a week at least or more billing on his end but its just all the custom stuff that he does on his end that he can't really just bill for straight out of the box with Allmoxy. He mentioned that even if it was just getting some sort of a custom product created where he could quickly edit the final price or whatever before he ships the products. He said and maybe that's the easiest way to do it because he feels like he is getting close, and has been paying attention to what prices it's been shooting out for most of the jobs and compared to how I'm billing it, and it's right there, plus or minus a percent or two of where he would like to be. But he feels like that's ok, it's just the custom materials it added or once we get it into Allmoxy and he transfers the ORDX, and he transfers the RDX into cabinet vision, then they make changes if they add a cabinet or two, because he usually sends them assembly sheets out of cabinet vision, and then it comes back to him with the changes before he tests it. By doing that, it changes the price and he just doesn't want to lose out on all the money. So he essentially needs a quick way to get into their order, update their price without updating the product, like a different product and trying to make it work or it's just something custom say the add one panel, but they want to be boarded or grooved or they want, like a flower design cut in or something like that. He just needs to quickly edit their order to what he wants it to be before he ships it. Additionally, it would be making sure that he understands the back and forth between that and Quickbooks since that would be a whole different ballgame. He is currently using Quickbooks desktop. He said there is a lot of his business that does not run through Allmoxy because of his own personal jobs or commercial side of things. So he needs it to all run in one financial software type. So he needs to be able to import those invoices or import those accounts receivable or something into Allmoxy or Quickbooks so he can keep track of it. He said the payment will probably still end up going through QuickBooks or obviously if it's credit card, it will go through Allmoxy. But somehow getting those payments imported into QuickBooks so he can keep everything in one tab would be big. The ORDX he thinks is figured out and he has most of the import of the materials done, he still has some to import, but most of the materials stuff is figured out. With pricing finally leveled off a little bit, he thinks the next step is just trying to speed up the invoicing process. He is attending IWF and arrives Tuesday. I let him know he could stop by the booth and talk with you in person @Carson Ward. Or if you miss each other you could link up after the show. Forgive me if these notes don't make sense. I did my best to keep notes and keep up. You can always watch the fireflies if that helps. Let me know if you have any questions. Thank you!

Attribute Sheet Updating Meeting 03/28/24 https://app.fireflies.ai/view/Allmoxy-Attribute-Table-Importing::ezZtd4xe2hycfkl8 Agenda: How much is changing within each attribute? (seems like a handful of additions) Requirements and best practices for importing attribute tables Pull a fresh export, save the name, date, and time. (if you're needing to add (custom columns then do so before the export) Copy this export and edit the copy. You can add rows to this copy but do not assign an existing option ID. (this should be added for you when you upload the new document. Start with one attribute table and make sure everything goes smoothly. rinse and repeat. Document everything that is changed (rows and columns, removed or added) Notes: Showed Jed the errors, he's going to do the export and updating himself. Starting small with one attribute and then going to expand to the rest.

engagements.title: Call with Bela Fodrovics
engagements.body:
Sees value in e-commerce- order flow is working well through cabinivision with a few of his dealers. Works for builders and architects.

1. Web-Based business

2. B2B Function- ability to plug in other catalogs

3. Further automation
What is the value of your business when you're not there. First thing an investor looks at when buying your business is how efficient it is when you're not there.

Appointment scheduled 3:00 PM EST on may 20th. Hius only objection is goin to be figuring out fair pricing. He sells million dollar projects, but his profit is relatively small on those jobs- so he'd technically have to pay a lot for Allmoxy, although his take-home is small. I'll talk to Beau about his situation and see what we can work out.
